title: item_p0000
complex: Fit in four minutes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: It sounds like a headline from a health magazine; an unattainable promise on late-night satellite TV.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: Then you attempt Dr Izumi Tabata’s training protocol – 20 seconds of all-out effort, 10 seconds of rest, repeat eight times – and between sounding like Darth Vader as you desperately suck in oxygen and collapsing in a messy bundle of sweat and defeat, you realize just how wrong you were.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: Tabata has seen it all before.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: “They were dead!” he chuckles as he recalls the first time he inflicted the system that bears his name on his university students in the early 1990s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: “After four minutes’ hard exercise they were wiped out.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: But after six weeks they saw the results and were surprised.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: We all were.” His research followed extensive monitoring of Japan’s speed skating team in the early 1990s when he – along with the team’s coach Irisawa Koichi – noticed that short bursts of brutally hard exercise seemed to be at least as effective as hours of moderate training.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: Tabata set out to show this with a simple experiment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: One group of moderately trained students performed an hour of steady cardiovascular exercise on a stationary bike five times a week.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: The other group did a ten-minute warm-up on the bike, followed by four minutes of Tabata intervals, four times a week – plus one 30-minute session of steady exercise with two minutes of intervals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: The results were startling.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: After six weeks of testing, the group following Tabata’s plan – exercising for just 88 minutes a week – had increased their anaerobic capacity by 28% and their VO2 max, a key indicator of cardiovascular health and maximal aerobic power, by 15%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: The control group, who trained for five hours every week, also improved their VO2 max, but by 10% – and their training had no effect on anaerobic capacity.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: “We have also measured increases in heart size after three weeks of doing the protocol,” says Tabata.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: “And there is also forthcoming research that shows that it lowers the risk of diabetes in humans, something we have already shown in rats.” But there are no half-measures here.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: You can’t go steady on a cross trainer, chewing gum and reading the latest issue of HELLO!
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: The regimen demands head-down bursts on a stationary bike or rowing machine; explosive bodyweight exercises, sprints or suchlike.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: Remember how you felt after doing a 100m sprint at school?
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: Imagine doing eight of them with only a tensecond break to recover.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: “All-out effort at 170% of your VO2 max is the criterion of the protocol,” says Tabata.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: “If you feel OK afterwards you’ve not done it properly.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: The first three repetitions will feel easy but the last two will feel impossibly hard.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: In the original plan the aim was to get to eight, but some only lasted six or seven.” As one commenter on the popular exercise forum T-Nation puts it: “When done correctly you should meet God.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: Most people are incapable of doing it correctly and shouldn’t even try.” Tabata doesn’t completely agree.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: “Everyone can do it but beginners should start with educated trainers so that they can work at the correct intensity for them,” he explains.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: He says that he will soon publish research showing that doing the programme just twice a week, less than half the volume in the original research, still provides significant health benefits.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: Another soon-to-be-published finding, which Tabata describes as “rather significant”, shows that the Tabata protocol burns an extra 150 calories in the 12 hours after exercise, even at rest, due to the effect of excess post-exercise oxygen consumption.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: So while it is used by most people to get fit – or by fit people to get even fitter – it also burns fat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: It’s slightly surprising, therefore, that the plan is still the preserve of the serious athlete and musclehead crowd – although that may change now that Tabata has agreed a deal with Universal Studios that will lead to a network of instructors and a DVD range released towards the end of the year.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: “I decided to do this because I often go on YouTube and, while I am honoured that people are doing it, some are doing it wrong because they don’t realize the intensity you need to work at,” says Tabata.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: So should we all start incorporating this plan into our fitness regimens?
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: Richard Scrivener, a former Assistant Strength and Conditioning Coach at Northampton Saints Rugby Club, says that while the benefits are clear, Tabatas are an addition, not a replacement, to a favoured sport or training method.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: “Runners, for instance, need a high level of running economy, which comes from skill acquisition and putting in the miles,” says Scrivener, “But they could effectively ease off the long runs and reduce the overall mileage by introducing Tabata training.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: This will unload the skeleton and give joints the chance to rest and recover, especially if one is prone to niggles or has a history of injuries – and you would probably therefore get more out of the long runs when you do undertake them.” Gym rats can benefit by doing three strength sessions and three Tabatas a week.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: And the rest of us can build up session by session, week by week, all the time knowing that it will never get easier because every session calls for maximum effort.
label: ignore

title: item_p0000
complex: That’s the cruel genius of the protocol: it is unrelenting – and effective.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: A long time ago, cinema audiences were transported to a galaxy far, far away: one where imperial cruisers battled rebel ghters, where droids rubbed circuits with Wookiees and where a spaceship called the Millennium Falcon could make the Kessel Run in less than 12 parsecs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: That was 1977 but, in 2015, as the franchise approaches its seventh big-screen instalment, interest in Star Wars shows no sign of abating Ð now, there is news of a new lm about Han Solo and of a reappearance for Darth Vader.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: ÒCountless fans around the world are in a constant state of vigilance waiting for the release of new poster art, new trailers and other information,Ó said Paul Dergarabedian, senior media analyst at box-of ce monitor Rentrak.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: ÒItÕs hard to imagine any other movie franchise that could evoke such a level of passion, enthusiasm and excitement.Ó The latest Star Wars mania dates from the Hollywood studio DisneyÕs purchase of Lucas lm from the lmÕs creator, George Lucas, in 2012; the $4bn acquisition was accompanied by an announcement of three more sequential ÒepisodesÓ Ð VII, VIII and IX Ð plus then unspeci ed plans for spin-off movies and Òstand- alonesÓ to ll the gaps in the release schedule.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: Details of the second spin-off have now been made public: an Òorigins storyÓ about Han Solo, the intergalactic smuggler played by Harrison Ford in the rst three lms, which is scheduled to be released in May 2018.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: It will follow the release in December 2015 of Episode VII, directed by JJ Abrams and titled Star Wars: The Force Awakens, the unnamed Episode VIII due out in 2017 and the already announced spin-off, Rogue One, which will arrive in cinemas in 2016.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: That movie will outline a rebel mission to steal the plans of the Death Star, a key plot element of the rst lm in 1977.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: Levels of interest in the rumour that Rogue One will also feature the reappearance of Darth Vader, the black-clad villain of the original series, only con rmed the power of Star Wars nostalgia.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: In creating a multi-stranded, multi-character cinema ÒuniverseÓ around Star Wars, Lucas lm-Disney are taking their cue from the phenomenally successful series of lms produced by Marvel Studios, which Disney also acquired, in 2009.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: Disney has brought a whole new level of marketing savvy to an already popular product: Dergarabedian cites the decision to make all six existing Star Wars lms available on streaming services as Òa brilliant way to build the anticipation for the new lm and reinvigorate the idea of Star Wars in the minds of the fansÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: Whether it needs reinvigorating is questionable.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: The level of enthusiasm that has surrounded Star Wars for at least the last two decades is evidenced by what has become known as the ÒExpanded UniverseÓ: the plethora of novels, comic books, video games and merchandising that Lucas lm has created or licensed over This extra material began emerging as far back as 1978 but was given a massive kick by the mushrooming of the internet in the 1990s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: Role-play and fan participation show no sign of slowing: in the UK, event-cinema brand Secret Cinema have found considerable commercial success with their live staging of The Empire Strikes Back.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: Michael Rosser, news editor for Screen International, suggests that it is this Òshared universeÓ of highly infectious nostalgia that keeps Star Wars in pole position among lm franchises.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: ÒThe great thing about the original lms was that they created a huge universe of characters and possibility that sparked the imagination of viewers,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: ÒFor years, people have been wondering how the different strands would play out.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: This new lm, because itÕs going back to Han Solo and Luke Skywalker, looks as though it will reconnect with the original Star Wars in a way that the prequels failed to.Ó Rosser is referring to the three lms Lucas directed between 1999 and 2005 Ð The Phantom Menace, Attack of the Clones and Revenge of the Sith Ð which chronicled the life of Luke SkywalkerÕs father, Anakin, who transforms into Darth Vader.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: Despite being greeted with less-than-stellar reviews, the prequels took $2.5bn at the worldwide box of ce, shortly after the release of restored and augmented Òspecial editionsÓ of the rst trilogy had netted $469m in 1997.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: ÒIt shows the power of Star Wars that, although they were disappointing, the prequels still managed to make a lot of money,Ó said Rosser.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: In a lm business where branding is all and a successful franchise the answer to everybodyÕs prayers, is there a risk that movie studios will simply become branding machines and lose their interest in cinema for its own sake?
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: Rosser thinks not.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: ÒThey are desperate to ensure the longevity of the franchise and make sure the quality is kept up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: They are also trying to bring people into the cinema at a time when lots are staying home for entertainment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: But you donÕt want to watch Star Wars on your iPhone so I donÕt think itÕs going to run out of steam any time soon.Ó Meanwhile, Dergarabedian is anticipating massive business when The Force Awakens reaches cinemas in December 2015.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: ÒWe are certainly looking at a record opening for December and it should go on to make at least a billion dollars worldwide.
label: ignore

title: item_p0001
complex: Truly, Star Wars is the ultimate movie brand.Ó
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Up to a billion people will remain in extreme poverty by 2030 unless countries focus on inequalities and confront social, economic and cultural forces that block their escape or pull them back into impoverishment, a major report warns.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The report by the Chronic Poverty Advisory Network (CPAN) asserts that many people may rise above the poverty line of $1.25 a day, only to tumble back when they are hit by a combination or sequence of shocks such as drought, illness and insecurity or con ict.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Drawing on household panel surveys, the report found that, in parts of rural Kenya and in South Africa, 30 to 40% of those who escaped from poverty fell back, rising to 60% in some areas of Ethiopia between 1999 and 2009.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Even in successful countries such as Indonesia and Vietnam, the proportion has been 20%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Individual cases highlight the ease with which people can slip back into poverty.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Amin, 61, from rural Bangladesh, has seen his livelihood gradually decline, due to his own and his wifeÕs illnesses, the cost of his sonÕs marriage, the death of his father and loss of goods such as shing nets.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Lovemore, 74, from Zimbabwe, has become one of the poorest people in his village.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: He recently lost his job as a car-park attendant due to ill health and had to take in his ve grandchildren after the death of his daughters.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: ÒWe need to ensure that people lifted out of poverty remain above the poverty line permanently.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Too many families are experiencing Ôtwo steps forward, one step backÕ, where they struggle to recover from personal or bigger setbacks.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Governments shouldnÕt assume that, just because somebodyÕs income hits $1.25, that means job done,Ó said Andrew Shepherd, research fellow at the Overseas Development Institute, a partner of CPAN, and lead author of the report.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: A UN high-level panel considering a new development framework to succeed the Millennium Development Goals after 2015 said the goal of eliminating extreme poverty by 2030 was within reach.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The report, however, argues that Òmore of the sameÓ will not get to zero.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Despite a drop in extreme poverty from 1.9bn in 1990 to 1.2bn in 2010, the report says that progress in the next 15 years will be much harder.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The big gains in China are unlikely to be matched by similar progress elsewhere, while climate-related shocks and deep poverty in parts of sub-Saharan Africa will slow momentum.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The report calls for a shift in thinking about poverty to focus on the chronically poor Ð those who are poor for many years or their entire lives Ð and for more emphasis on stopping impoverishment, the descent into poverty.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: ÒGovernments have been quite good at moving people over the poverty line because that is relatively easy Ð there is a tendency to pick the low-hanging fruit, but they have shied away from the more dif cult job of tackling chronic poverty,Ó said Shepherd.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The report says progress on poverty reduction, while impressive to date, has had less of an impact on the chronically poor than on those who were already closer to the poverty line.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: It will not be possible to get to zero unless development policies prioritize those living in chronic poverty, it adds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Policymakers must aim for a Òzero tripodÓ of separate but interdependent objectives: tackle chronic poverty; stop impoverishment; sustain poverty escapes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: They put forward a trio of policies, all of which require massive global investment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The rst is social assistance Ð a safety net Ð to bring the poorest people closer to a decent standard of living.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: An example of such a scheme is EthiopiaÕs productive safety net programme that allowed thousands of vulnerable households to withstand drought without having to curtail education spending.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The second is education, from early childhood to the start of work, to enable people to escape and stay out of poverty.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Education also has the advantage of being a ÒportableÓ asset.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The third is pro-poorest economic growth policies to ensure that the bene ts of increasing national prosperity reach the very poorest people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: ÒEconomic growth really is critical, but not just any old growth,Ó Shepherd said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: ÒIt has to generate not just employment but decent jobs, not just insecure low-paying jobs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: There are ways of doing this, like IndiaÕs rural employment guarantees scheme, which has boosted urban not just rural wages.Ó Another example cited by the report is South AfricaÕs extension of the minimum wage to groups such as domestic workers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: All this will take money and the report says one obvious implication is that stronger domestic tax systems and greater tax revenues will be required.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Aid will also be needed for the start-up costs for social assistance, universal health coverage and to nance education, including scholarships for the poorest children.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: ÒThere remains a huge role for aid in the next 20 years, as many developing countries spend less than $500 on each of their citizens a year.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Even Nigeria, with its oil wealth, spends only $650 per capita,Ó Shepherd said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Against a backdrop of crises in Syria, South Sudan and the Central African Republic, con ict and environmental disasters have to be addressed, and the report says it is essential that governments work to reduce the risk of con ict and to establish inclusive peace settlements.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: Incorporating Òreducing (income) inequalityÓ as a post-2015 goal would steer the international agenda in the right direction, given that lessening or containing inequality speeds up the impact of economic growth on poverty reduction.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The report argues that if the inequalities that affect the poorest people Ð such as access to land, labour markets and the power relationships between men and women Ð are addressed, this would tackle two goals at the same time: reducing chronic poverty and inequality.
label: ignore

title: item_p0002
complex: The authors urge governments to develop an inclusive national development plan and to work with civil society to ensure the poorest people are represented politically as well as tackling dif cult social norms such as dowries and witchcraft that contribute to extreme poverty: ÒThis often means challenging and, perhaps, sweeping away aspects of the status quo and represents the most marked departure from Ôbusiness as usualÕ.Ó
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Unusually for someone who likes to chat, Kenton Cool can barely speak.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Exerting himself at high altitude has left his voice a throaty growl.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: He is now in Kathmandu, the capital of Nepal, having flown down from Everest base camp that morning.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Cool is reflecting on a startling sequence of climbs completed over the course of the previous weekend.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Early on Saturday morning, he reached the summit of Nuptse, the first and lowest of the three main summits in the Everest “horseshoe” that surrounds the glaciated valley called the Western Cwm.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: That same day, he climbed up to the summit of Everest itself, reaching the top in complete darkness early on Sunday.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: He and his climbing partner then continued on to the summit of Lhotse, the third of this spectacular three-peaks challenge, on Monday morning.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “It was a snatched opportunity,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “For the first time since the late 1990s, there were fixed ropes on all three mountains.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: That doesn’t take away the physical achievement of what I did.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: I’ve set the bar at a certain level.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: But whoever comes along next will move the bar further and do it without ropes or bottled oxygen.” Sixty years after Everest was first climbed, much of the coverage is looking back to Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay and their age of innocence from the modern era of commercialism and environmental degradation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: I’ve asked Cool to look forward and imagine what top climbers might be doing 60 years from now.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “I hate to think,” he says, but mentions the Swiss climber, Ueli Steck, who fled the mountain in April following what Cool terms “an altercation” with a crowd of Sherpas at Camp 2.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Steck, he says, was planning to climb Everest’s west ridge, first done in 1963, descend to the South Col and then immediately climb Lhotse via a new route, all without fixed ropes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “Ueli had been training like a machine,” Cool says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “He’s a climber in a class all his own.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: He’s technically brilliant but he had also taken his physical condition to an astronomic level.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: It would have been amazing to see what he could have done.” What tourism will look like is another matter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: One clue is in the stunning helicopter rescue performed by Simone Moro, Steck’s climbing partner, whose intemperate language provoked the confrontation at Camp 2.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Moro flew back to Everest on Tuesday at the controls of a highpowered helicopter to rescue a stricken climber at an altitude of 7,800 metres.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: It was the highest rescue yet performed on Everest and highlights the exponential rise in helicopter flights in recent years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: By 2073, the infrastructure on the mountain could include a helipad on the South Col, bringing tourists breathing bottled oxygen.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: In the meantime, they are transforming the potential for rescuing both climbers and the far more numerous trekkers heading as far as base camp.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Whether the Everest region can continue to cope with a booming tourism sector remains to be seen, according to mountain geographer and environmentalist, Alton Byers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: The combination of climate change and tourism, he says, is creating new stresses on the Sherpa homeland.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: The retreat, and in some cases disappearance, of glaciers in the Everest region are having a major impact already.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “Everywhere you go, people are talking about how there’s less water.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: There’s less water for agriculture and less water for all the new lodges that are getting built.” In the Sherpa town of Namche Bazaar, he says, a new five-mile pipeline is being laid to bring water to service the growing tourist demand for showers and flush toilets.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: The local stream has become contaminated with human waste and does not provide enough water for a place that, in high season, is bursting at the seams.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “Every village is digging a pit just beyond the houses for garbage.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Khumbu has the highest landfill sites in the world,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Human waste at base camp is now managed well and removed in plastic barrels.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: But, according to Byers, these barrels are emptied into a huge pit a few hours down the valley that could leak into the region’s watercourses.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “These problems can be solved, but we need to get serious about it,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “One climber can spend $85,000 climbing Everest.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: And that’s fine.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: But at some point we’re going to have to address these other priorities.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: For half a million dollars a year, you could solve most of them.” Climate change is another matter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Byers works with local conservation committees to identify and plan for the impacts of climate change, most usually finding new water sources or introducing rainwater harvesting.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: The rapid build-up of glacial lakes that threaten to burst and flood the Sherpa homeland is a constant threat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “There’s going to come a time when people are going to have to get out of their way.” Changing weather patterns are also having an impact on tourism.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: Increased cloud cover in periods of normally clear weather is closing Lukla Airport, the gateway to the Everest region, more often.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: A new road for 4x4s is being built to Lukla to guarantee the flow of tourists and their money, but Byers is concerned that the rapid spread of the road network in Nepal is being done on the cheap, with disastrous consequences in terms of soil erosion and landslides.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “Everest is the icon everyone knows,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: “It’s the canary in the coalmine that everyone understands.
label: ignore

title: item_p0003
complex: It’s the perfect laboratory for figuring out how to address some of these problems, like the impacts of climate change and tourism.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: Valdevaqueros is one of the last remaining unspoilt beaches in southern Spain, where the sky above the golden sands is filled with kites hauling surfers over the waves.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: Currently the beach has little more than an access road lined with camper vans from Germany, France, Italy and Britain, filled with windsurfers and kitesurfers lured by the area’s strong winds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: For decades it has been a world apart from the concrete-lined beaches of Torremolinos and Marbella along the coast, yet on 29 May the local council in Tarifa approved plans to build a tourist complex right next to the beach, with 1,400 hotel rooms and 350 flats.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: Environmental and conservation groups have protested that the project will harm the habitats of protected species, but for most councillors here the issue is simple: jobs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: In this town of 18,000 inhabitants, 2,600 are out of work as Spain faces its worst economic crisis in at least half a century, one that has cast doubt on the future of the euro.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: “Traditional sources of income such as fishing are dying out, now that fleets are being dismantled and fish stocks are depleted, so tourism is the only way out, as long as it is sustainable,” said Sebastián Galindo, a councillor from the Socialist Party, which is in opposition in Tarifa but voted with the governing People’s Party to give the project the green light.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: Tarifa’s Mayor, Juan Andrés Gil, declined to comment on the project, but Galindo said it complies with environmental standards.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: The complex would be 800 metres from the coast, comfortably beyond the minimum of 200 metres stipulated by a law designed to prevent more ugly developments like those that blighted much of Spain’s coastline when mass tourism first descended on its shores in the 1960s and 1970s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: Opponents of the complex say the last thing anyone needs is more housing in a country that already has a million empty homes, although the central government last week proposed a sell-off by granting non-Spaniards residency permits in return for buying property worth at least €160,000.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: The Socialist opposition in Madrid attacked the proposal, and Galindo said it discriminated against migrant workers who flocked to Spain during the boom years, many of them from Morocco, whose coastline is just 14km away and can be seen from Tarifa.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: “It favours moneyed classes rather than those who came here to help Spain get ahead,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: Surfers fear that new buildings in Valdevaqueros would reduce the strength of the famous local Levant wind but fail to lure traditional package holidaymakers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: “It’s not really a family spot.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: Just wait until they see what a Levant is like,” said Henning Mayer, who has regularly made the journey from Augsburg in Germany for 20 years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: “Ten years ago they said they would build a new highway here.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: It didn’t happen, so I think it will be impossible to build new hotels.” At the southernmost tip of Spain, Tarifa is the strategic crossroads between Africa and Europe, where the Mediterranean Sea meets the Atlantic.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: Campaigners say it also has a vital role in the animal world as a crossroads for migrating species.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: The campaign to save the beach was launched hours after the Tarifa council voted for the project.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: The campaign has a Facebook page and is supported by groups including Greenpeace, the World Wide Fund for Nature and the Spanish branch of conservation network Birdlife.
label: ignore

title: item_p0004
complex: “It’s the environmental equivalent of putting a shopping centre right in the middle of the Alhambra,” said Noelia Jurado, who uses her multimedia expertise to campaign against the complex.

title: item_p0004
complex: She also noted that the resort would be near the ancient Roman town of Baelo Claudia.

title: item_p0004
complex: “They could be building on top of more Roman ruins here.

title: item_p0004
complex: Nobody knows.” Also joining the opposition to the planned resort is the Andalusian College of Geographers, which, in a preliminary study charted on its website, concluded that “free areas”, including car parks if not actual buildings, will overlap part of the Alcornocales National Park.

title: item_p0004
complex: The geographers also estimate that the site intrudes on two wildlife conservation areas.

title: item_p0004
complex: One of the areas in Valdevaqueros is home to two species of bat whose survival is threatened.

title: item_p0004
complex: “Money is once again being put before urban laws and European environmental directives,” said Raúl Romeva, a member of the European Parliament who is Vice-President of the Greens group.

title: item_p0004
complex: In Romeva’s view, the project is also at fault because the proposed site has too little water in a town that already suffers from shortages in the summer weather that scorches the southern Spanish region of Andalusía.

title: item_p0004
complex: Many locals are also wondering why a resort should be built 10km away, rather than on wasteland near Tarifa’s picturesque old centre, with its typically Andalusian whitewashed walls and winding streets, dominated by a 10th-century Moorish castle.

title: item_p0004
complex: “My opinion and that of catering workers is that we agree with the complex as long as it creates jobs in the town, which is what is needed, but we are against it being for the benefit of a few,” said Cristóbal Lobato, who has worked at the same beachside bar in Tarifa for 30 years.

title: item_p0004
complex: “If they put it in the centre of Tarifa, where there is space, then clients could visit shops, tapas bars and restaurants.” Overlooking the green fields earmarked for building, biologist Aitor Galán, who conducts environmental impact studies for a living, pointed at one of only two seaside breeding grounds for vultures in Europe.

title: item_p0004
complex: “Anywhere else in Europe, this place would have the utmost protection, but here they want to get rid of it all and cover it with buildings,” he said.

title: item_p0004
complex: “What they want to do is turn this into Benidorm, but what draws people here is wildlife and the wind.

title: item_p0005
complex: Unveiling a car with a top speed of 25mph, two seats and no pedals or steering wheel might not make much of an impression at a motor show.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: But Google, in the US, sent a minor earthquake through the car and taxi industries as it unveiled the latest version of its driverless car.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: The electrically powered vehicle, which Google has begun testing around its headquarters in Mountain View, California, dispenses with all the normal controls, including foot pedals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: Instead, it has a smartphone app that summons it and tells it the destination, and a single STOP button mounted between the two front-facing seats in case the occupants need to override the computer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: The car, in fact, takes over all the tasks of navigation, steering, acceleration and braking.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: The company is building about 100 prototypes for a two-year test.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: The company’s co-founder, Sergey Brin, told a conference in California that the vehicle was “still in the prototype stage” but that the project was “about changing the world for people who are not well served by transportation today”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: He said of the car: “You’re just sitting there; no steering wheel, no pedals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: For me, it was very relaxing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: About ten seconds after getting in, I forgot I was there.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: It reminded me of catching a chairlift by yourself – a bit of solitude I found really enjoyable.” Google says that the principal aim of the project is to improve safety and that, because the car is constructed with impact-absorbing foam at the front and a plastic windscreen, “it should be far safer than any other car for pedestrians”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: The cars, which have been built specially by a company (as yet unnamed) in Detroit, will be used to investigate further how best to make driverless vehicles work.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: Google will run a pilot programme using the cars, which are not yet for sale.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: One challenge is creating high-definition scans of the roads and surroundings before the cars can drive along them because they cannot gather and process enough information in real time.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: So far, there are high-detail maps of about 2,000 miles of California’s roads, but the state has more than 170,000 miles of public roads.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: Google says it is interested in licensing the technology to traditional vehicle manufacturers once it has refined it sufficiently.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: Members of the team had been working on the project even before joining Google, for more than a decade.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: But the prospect of driverless cars replacing human-driven taxis has been the cause of some alarm.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: “If you get rid of the driver, then they’re unemployed,” said Dennis Conyon, the southeast director for the UK National Taxi Association.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: “It would have a major impact on the labour force.” London has about 22,000 licensed black cabs and Conyon estimates that the total number of people who drive taxis for hire in the UK is about 100,000.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: However, Steve McNamara, general secretary of the 10,500-strong London Taxi Drivers’ Association, said: “You won’t get these driverless cars in London for 20 or 25 years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: Maybe, by then, they’ll have a charge point – because there isn’t a single one in London now.” Other car makers, including Volvo, Ford and Mercedes, are working on driver-assisted vehicles, which, unlike Google’s version, do not dispense with the driver controls.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: But Chris Urmson, director of the self-driving car project at Google, said that the new prototypes dispensed with the steering wheel and brakes because there was no guarantee that a human occupant would be able to take over in an emergency, and that it was simpler just to have an emergency stop button.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: Urmson said: “The vehicles will be very basic.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: We want to learn from them and adapt them as quickly as possible.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: But they will take you where you want to go at the push of a button.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: And, that’s an important step towards improving road safety and transforming mobility for millions of people.” So far, the Google versions of the self-driving cars have covered 700,000 miles without an accident caused by the computer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: The company points out that thousands of people die each year on the roads and that about 80% of crashes can be ascribed to human error.
label: ignore

title: item_p0005
complex: But, they could have some way to go to match Conyon at the National Taxi Association.

title: item_p0006
complex: Agios Efstratios is so remote, so forgotten by the banks, the government and most of the modern world that the mobile phone network canÕt process data and there isnÕt a single ATM or credit-card machine on the island.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: Before Greece was plunged into nancial chaos, residents of this tranquil outpost in the northern Aegean managed quite well.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: They did their banking at the post of ce and the few dozen rooms to rent were booked out every summer with people who had heard Ð by word of mouth Ð of its spectacular empty beaches, clear seas and fresh seafood.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: But, because the island still runs on cash, the closure of banks nationwide has been devastating.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: Residents have been forced to make nine-hour round trips to the nearest big island to get cash and Greek visitors say they canÕt get together enough money to come.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒTourist numbers are down 80% this year,Ó said Mayor Maria Kakali, in an of ce in the village where she grew up, home to around 200 people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒEven people born here and living in Athens, who have their own places on the island, arenÕt coming.Ó Kakali has badgered the government and a major Greek bank into promising an ATM within weeks but she still feels it may come too late for this season on an island where tourism dwarfs the two other sources of income: shing and agriculture: ÒWe have almost no reservations in August, when usually we have people calling us up asking to nd a room and we canÕt help them.Ó A hard winter ahead may be slightly improved by 50 workers billeted in the village to expand the harbour but there is an even bigger crisis looming because the government has said it will end a decades-old tax break for islands.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: Created to help island communities survive when they were suffering mass emigration, a lower sales tax contained the costs of living in places where everything had to be imported and made tourism more affordable.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: Tourist favourites such as Mykonos fear that losing the tax breaks will make it hard for them to compete with Turkey but, for Agios Efstratios, it poses a far greater threat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒIf we have to pay a tax of 23%, IÕm sorry to say it but we will all die on the island,Ó says Kakali.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: Food and fuel are already more expensive than on the mainland, there are no economies of scale and little economic exibility on an island which, even in summer, has only three shops, two restaurants and not a single of cial hotel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒThis is an expensive island.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: Everything, even milk or bread, has changed hands three or four times before it gets to us and each middleman has to take a pro t,Ó said Provatas Costas, a 58-year-old sherman.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: For Agios Efstratios and its closest large neighbour, Lemnos, the timing of the crisis is particularly cruel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: They were condemned to relative obscurity for years in part because they are served by slow and unreliable ferries.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: In 2015, the government had nally given the contract to a new, ef cient company, drawing oods of new visitors to explore the islandsÕ largely overlooked charms, before the bank controls hit.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒIt started out as the best season in 30 years and, in one week, became the worst,Ó said Atzamis Konstantinos, a travel agent in Lemnos who used to earn Û15,000 a month as a captain of petroleum tankers and other large ships but came home because he missed it so much.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒI always loved this island, even when I was young.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: I would dock in Piraeus, the port in Athens, take my pay, then get the rst boat over.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: People would say ÔYouÕre crazy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: You can go anywhereÕ but I only ever wanted to come here.Ó Lemnos has dozens of wild beaches, where even at the height of summer you can swim and sunbathe virtually alone, a small nightlife scene and numerous cultural sites.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: It is the eighth largest island in Greece so is in line for the rst round of tax increases in autumn 2015 but far less wealthy than many smaller ones.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: It has just over 3,000 beds for visitors, compared with tens of thousands on an island such as Rhodes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: Its councillors say that they will ght the tax rise, although none can say how.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒWe have been suffering economically in recent years and, now, we will suffer more,Ó said Lemnos Mayor, Dimitris Marinakis.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒWhen there is not enough money, you reduce your consumption and therefore the whole economy declines.Ó The crisis has been particularly hard on people under 40, who often do two or three jobs to make ends meet and still feel they canÕt afford a family.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: Young people fear they are losing their contemporaries in an exodus to match the one that scattered their grandparentsÕ friends to America, Australia and Canada.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒWhat is the future for our generation?Ó said Katerina Fikari, who feels she is extremely lucky to work for the local government in Lemnos.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒIf you work only to pay your bills, how can you have dreams for your future?Ó If taxes go up, even more young people will leave, warns Mayor Kakali, who has devoted her year in of ce to improving education on the island to help keep it an attractive place for families.
label: ignore

title: item_p0006
complex: Because it is one of the smallest islands, Agios Efstratios has until 2017 before the rise is due to come in, so Kakali hopes that the roller coaster of Greek politics means it yet may be spared.

title: item_p0006
complex: If not, though, she plans to travel to Athens to remind the distant government what the tax rise would cost.

title: item_p0006
complex: ÒThe truth is the government doesnÕt pay much attention to the islands of the north Aegean,Ó she said, Òso I would take all the kids from our school to the gates of parliament, to tell them: ÔThere is still life in these islandsÕ.Ó

complex: There are worse things to do in life than stroll along RioÕs Copacabana beach in the sunshine on the way to watch a World Cup match, so it was perhaps not surprising that England fan Anthony McDowell from Liverpool was having none of the doom and gloom that preceded some of the build-up to the tournament in Brazil.

title: item_p0008
complex: Scientists have connected the brains of a pair of animals and allowed them to share sensory information in a major step towards what the researchers call the world’s first “organic computer”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: The US team fitted two rats with devices called brain-to-brain interfaces that let the animals collaborate on simple tasks to earn rewards, such as a drink of water.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: In one radical demonstration of the technology, the scientists used the internet to link the brains of two rats separated by thousands of miles, with one in the researchers’ lab at Duke University in Durham, North Carolina, and the other in Natal, Brazil.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: Led by Miguel Nicolelis, a pioneer of devices that allow paralyzed people to control computers and robotic arms with their thoughts, the researchers say their latest work may enable multiple brains to be hooked up to share information.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: “These experiments showed that we have established a sophisticated, direct communication linkage between brains,” Nicolelis said in a statement.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: “Basically, we are creating what I call an organic computer.” The scientists first demonstrated that rats can share, and act on, each other’s sensory information by electrically connecting their brains via tiny grids of electrodes that reach into the motor cortex, the brain region that processes movement.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: The rats were trained to press a lever when a light went on above it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: When they performed the task correctly, they got a drink of water.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: To test the animals’ ability to share brain information, they put the rats in two separate compartments.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: Only one compartment had a light that came on above the lever.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: When the rat pressed the lever, an electronic version of its brain activity was sent directly to the other rat’s brain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: In trials, the second rat responded correctly to the imported brain signals 70% of the time by pressing the lever.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: Remarkably, the communication between the rats the rats collaborated on a task that required them to distinguish between narrow and wide openings using their whiskers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: was two-way.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: If the receiving rat failed at the task, the first rat was not rewarded with a drink, and appeared to change its behaviour to make the task easier for its partner.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: In further experiments, In the final test, the scientists connected rats on different continents and beamed their brain activity back and forth over the internet.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: “Even though the animals were on different continents, with the resulting noisy transmission and signal delays, they could still communicate,” said Miguel Pais-Vieira, the first author of the study.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: “This tells us that we could create a workable network of animal brains distributed in many different locations.” Nicolelis said the team is now working on ways to link several animals’ brains at once to solve more complex tasks.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: “We cannot even predict what kinds of emergent properties would appear when animals begin interacting as part of a ‘brain-net’,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: “In theory, you could imagine that a combination of brains could provide solutions that individual brains cannot achieve by themselves.” The research is published in the journal Scientific Reports.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: Anders Sandberg, who studies the ethics of neurotechnologies at the Future of Humanity Institute at Oxford University, said the work was “very important” in helping to understand how brains encode information.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: But the implications of the technology and its potential future uses are far broader, said Sandberg.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: “The main reason we are running the planet is that we are amazingly good at communicating and coordinating.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: Without that, although we are very smart animals, we would not dominate the planet.” “I don’t think there’s any risk of supersmart rats from this,” he added.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: “There’s a big difference between sharing sensory information and being able to plan.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: I’m not worried about an imminent invasion of ‘rat multiborgs’.” Very little is known about how thoughts are encoded and how they might be transmitted into another person’s brain, so that is not a realistic prospect any time soon.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: And much of what is in our minds is what Sandberg calls a “draft” of what we might do.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: “Often, we don’t want to reveal those drafts; that would be embarrassing and confusing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: And a lot of those drafts are changed before we act.
label: ignore

title: item_p0008
complex: Most of the time I think we’d be very thankful not to be in someone else’s head.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: Cities don’t often decide to pack their bags, get up and move down the road.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: But that’s exactly what Kiruna, an Arctic town in northern Sweden, is having to do – to avoid being swallowed up into the earth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “It’s a dystopian choice,” says Krister Lindstedt of White, the Stockholm-based architects firm charged with the biblical task of moving this city of 23,000 people away from a gigantic iron-ore mine that is fast gobbling up the ground beneath its streets.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “Either the mine must stop digging, creating mass unemployment, or the city has to move – or else face certain destruction.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: It’s an existential predicament.” Founded in 1900 by the state-owned Luossavaara-Kiirunavaara mining company (LK), Kiruna has grown rich off the vast seam of iron ore below the town, but it’s now facing destruction by the very phenomenon that created its wealth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “We are symbiotic: the town is here because of the mine,” says Deputy Mayor Niklas Siren.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “Otherwise, no devil would have built a city here.” Located 145km inside the Arctic Circle, Kiruna is subject to a brutal climate, enduring winters with no sunlight and average temperatures below -15C.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: But the deep deposit of magnetite has proved a strong enough magnet to keep people here.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: Driven by the insatiable global appetite for construction, the mine has become the world’s largest underground iron-ore extraction site, producing 90% of all the iron in Europe, enough to build more than six Eiffel Towers a day.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: And demand continues to grow.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: In 2004, the mining company broke it to the town that its days were numbered: digging its shafts towards the city at an angle of 60 degrees, subsidence would soon lead to the widespread cracking and collapse of the town’s buildings.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: A decade on, fissures are starting to appear in the ground, creeping ever closer to the town.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “The people of Kiruna have been living in limbo for ten years,” says Viktoria Walldin, a social anthropologist who works with the architects.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “They have put their lives on hold, unable to make major decisions like buying a house, redecorating, having a child or opening a business.” After years of dithering, the city has finally unveiled a master plan for how it will proceed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “Imagine it like a walking millipede of a city,” says Lindstedt, unrolling a plan that shows the town’s streets and squares beginning to crawl eastwards along a new high street, until the whole place has moved safely out of the way of the mine by 2033.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: A new town square is already under way, 3km to the east, with a circular town hall planned by Danish architect Henning Larsen, while 20 key buildings have been identified to be dismantled and resurrected piece by piece in their new home – like an Ikea flatpack on a grand scale.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: Kiruna’s rust-red wooden church, built in 1912 in a form that recalls the indigenous Sami teepees, and once voted Sweden’s most beautiful building, will take pride of place in a new park, while the castiron bell tower will stand once again above the town hall.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: But not everything will be saved.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “I spoke to an old lady who walks past the bench every day where she had her first kiss,” says Walldin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “It’s things like that – the hospital where your first child was born, for example – that are important to people’s sense of identity and all that’s going to disappear.” Billed as “the most democratic move in history”, the project has been allocated the equivalent of £320m by the mining company for building new facilities, including a high school, fire station, community centre, library and swimming hall.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: But top of most people’s concerns is where they will actually live and what process will determine the housing allocation.

title: item_p0009
complex: “These details have yet to be determined,” admits Lindstedt.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: “People are used to very low rents and very high incomes but, in future, this will have to change.” LK has agreed to compensate residents to the value of their homes plus 25% but many locals say this is not enough to afford a new-build house at market rates.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: To aid the valuation process, the architects have monitored the housing lettings in nearby cities over a period of years, and “tagged” the homes in Kiruna with the assets they possess, from internal space and gardens to proximity to bus stops and the city centre.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: They have also proposed a “Kiruna Portal”, a kind of mass salvage yard, where materials from the doomed homes can be brought and hopefully recycled in the construction of the new buildings – although, given that Sweden has no tradition of self-build, it’s hard to see this taking off.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: A closer look at the plan shows the new town bears little relation to the original Kiruna at all.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: The current town is a sprawling suburban network of winding streets, home to detached houses with gardens.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: White’s plan incorporates a much higher-density arrangement of multi-storey apartment blocks around shared courtyards, lining straight boulevards, down which the icy winds will surge.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: It is an opportunity, say the architects, for Kiruna to “reinvent itself” into a model of sustainable development, attracting young people who wouldn’t have stayed in the town before, with new cultural facilities and “visionary” things such as a cable car bobbing above the high street.
label: ignore

title: item_p0009
complex: But it is a vision that many of the existing residents seem unlikely to be able to afford.

complex: It is not just the worldÕs biggest burger chain; it is also a global emblem of American consumer capitalism.

complex: But, these days, the golden arches of McDonaldÕs are looking a little tarnished.

complex: After a decade of expansion, customers around the world donÕt seem to be ÔlovinÕ itÕ any more.

complex: McDonaldÕs has revealed that worldwide sales dropped by 3.3% on 2013 in a set of results that were described as atrocious.

complex: Problems are piling up almost everywhere.

title: item_p0011
complex: In China, sales plunged by 23% after a food scare when local media showed workers apparently caught on camera at a local supplier claiming to use out-of-date beef and chicken in products destined for McDonaldÕs and KFC.

complex: Almost 60 years after Ray Kroc opened his rst restaurant in Des Plaines, Illinois, consumers are losing their appetite for a Big Mac and fries.

complex: McDonaldÕs has seen 12 straight months of declining sales in its massive home market, with sales down 4.1% in the latest quarter.

title: item_p0011
complex: ÒDoes McDonaldÕs beef contain worms?Ó asked another person (ÒNo.

complex: Gross.

complex: End of story,Ó replied the company).

title: item_p0012
complex: On one day in August, one in seven people on Earth, 1 billion people, used Facebook, according to founder Mark Zuckerberg.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: In a decade, the social network has transformed peopleÕs relationships, privacy, their businesses, news media, helped topple regimes and even changed the meanings of everyday words.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: ÒA more open and connected world is a better world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: It brings stronger relationships with those you love, a stronger economy with more opportunities and a stronger society that re ects all of our values,Ó wrote Zuckerberg in the post announcing the numbers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: These are just some of the ways his company changed everything Ð for better or worse.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: 1 Facebook has changed the de nition of ÒfriendÓ ÒTo friendÓ is now a verb.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: And, unlike in real life, when the ending of a friendship can be deeply traumatic, it is easy to ÒunfriendÓ, a word invented to describe ditching a casual acquaintance when they are no longer enhancing your Facebook newsfeed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Although the meanings of the words ÒshareÓ and ÒlikeÓ are essentially the same, Facebook has brought an entirely new weight to the terms.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: School and university reunions have become redundant Ð you already know whose career is going well, whether the perfect pair have split and youÕve seen endless pictures of your schoolmatesÕ babies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: You wonÕt be surprised by an ex in the street with a new girlfriend or boyfriend: you already know theyÕre dating someone else from the romantic sel es.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: But, unlike in real life, Facebook has no hierarchy of friendships.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: A classmate from one project at university who you havenÕt seen in 15 years, a friend-of-a-friend from a stag do or a colleague youÕve never actually spoken to in person Ð they are all Facebook friends in the same way as your closest mate or your spouse or your mum.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: It doesnÕt necessarily mean we see them the same way.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Professor Robin Dunbar is famous for his research that suggests a person can only have roughly 150 people as a social group.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Facebook hasnÕt changed that yet, he believes, but, in an interview with the New Yorker, Dunbar said he feared it was so easy simply to end friendships on Facebook that, eventually, there may no longer be any need to learn to get along.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: 2 We care less about privacy ThereÕs a wise saying: if youÕre not paying for it, youÕre the product.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Facebook embodies that philosophy and created an entire industry from it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: The astonishing thing is that users know that and they willingly hand over that information.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Pew Research Center found that most young people are more than willing to hand over their details.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: An overwhelming majority of 91% post a photo of themselves, 71% post the city or town where they live, up from 61%, more than half give email addresses and a fth give their phone number.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: But, as so much of a personÕs life is shared online, Facebook gives a platform for everyone to cultivate an image and a fanbase.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: In an article for the journal Frontiers in Psychology, academics described a new phenomenon, the emergence of the ÒFacebook selfÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: More than 80% list their interests, allowing brands to target them most effectively.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: But most younger users do restrict their pro les, with 60% allowing friends only.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: 3 Facebook has created millions of jobs Ð but not in its own of ces Facebook has essentially created an entire sector, including indirect employment for people whose job it is to make the platform work for their brand.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: ÒIt is a tool like no other,Ó said Michael Tinmouth, a social media strategist who has worked with brands such as Vodafone and Microsoft.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: ÒMarketers have an understanding of a brandÕs consumers like they have never had before.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: The data and analytics available to you are extraordinary.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: You know who your customers are, who they are friends with and how they engage with your brand.Ó And advertisers pay a lot for that.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Facebook reported ad revenue was up 46%, reaching $3.32bn.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Facebook is also a mine eld for brands.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Suddenly, rather than complaint conversations taking place over the phone with a customer service representative or on a small specialist internet forum, angry customers can post their complaints for hundreds of their friends to see or even on the page where all loyal fans of the brand have been carefully cultivated.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: And an injustice can go viral.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: 4 Facebook has been the tool to organize revolutions Though the Arab Spring was dubbed the Twitter revolution, organizing demonstrations and direct action has been revolutionized by Facebook.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Manchester UniversityÕs Olga Onuch found Facebook had been the key medium for reaching half of all the Euromaidan protesters in Ukraine.

title: item_p0012
complex: Facebook posts signalled the start of the Maidan protests during the hours after it was announced that Ukraine would not sign a free trade and association agreement with the EU, Onuch found.

title: item_p0012
complex: The posts organized live action, not just online anger.

title: item_p0012
complex: Mustafa Nayyem, the Ukraine activist, posted: ÒIf you really want to do something, donÕt just ÔlikeÕ this post.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: LetÕs meet near the monument to independence in the middle of the Maidan.Ó Many of those interviewed in OnuchÕs research said they relied on Facebook for the truth about what was happening Ð unable to trust traditional media.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: 5 Facebook makes news, breaks news and decides what is news Roughly 71% of 18- to 24-year-olds say the internet is their main news source and 63% of users overall, according to the Pew Research Center.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: About a third of Facebook users post about politics and government.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Most people will rst encounter a piece of journalism or an item of breaking news via Facebook or other social media, and most of those encounters will be on mobiles.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Users might never have to leave the site to get their news: Instant Articles will see stories run within Facebook.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: It allows news companies to sell ads around their articles, gaining them 100% of that revenue, while Facebook can also sell ads around that article, with 70% of the revenue from the social networkÕs advertising also going to the news companies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Facebook has also changed the ways journalists write stories.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: It is a resource many reporters cannot now live without.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: For better or (often) worse, it is a directory to nd, contact and glean information for almost any ordinary person, who might suddenly nd themselves at the centre of the dayÕs biggest news story.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Facebook has its own newswire, sharing the most useful user reaction to breaking stories, including pictures and videos.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: 6 Users are changing Facebook It used to be a site to get students connected, with only elite US universities allowed access.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: In 2014, a decade after its launch, 56% of internet users aged 65 and older have a Facebook account.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: And 39% are connected to people they have never met in person.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: Groups have given way to pages, writing on each others walls is passe and carefully curated albums have given way to instant mobile uploads.

title: item_p0012
complex: More than ever, the site is a gateway not just to your friends but to the rest of the internet.
label: ignore

title: item_p0012
complex: We may as well get used to it, said David Kirkpatrick, author of The Facebook Effect.

title: item_p0012
complex: ÒIt might very well go away further down the road but something this big takes a long time to disappear,Ó he told BBC Radio 4Õs Today programme.

title: item_p0012
complex: ÒFacebook has proven its ability to change and it will continue to be a very, very major player.Ó

title: item_p0013
complex: Bogus allergy tests are convincing thousands of people to take unnecessary treatments and put themselves or their children on inadequate diets, sometimes resulting in malnutrition, a group of experts and charities has said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: Allergies and food intolerances are soaring but confusion between the two, as well as the many misdiagnoses, are causing real harm, said the information organization Sense About Science, which has produced a guide in collaboration with allergy specialists and charities.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: “It’s probably the biggest mess for science communication, where myths, misinterpreted studies and quackery collide with under- and over-diagnosis,” said Tracey Brown, director of Sense About Science.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: “The costs are huge – unnecessary actions for some and not enough action for those whose lives depend on it.” Experts fear that restaurants and caterers are seeing so many people claiming they have allergies (which can be dangerous for the individual), when in fact they have a food intolerance (which is not), that they may not take all the precautions they should when serving a person who has a genuine allergy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: “It matters very much,” said Moira Austin of the Anaphylaxis Campaign.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: “If a caterer thinks somebody is just avoiding a food because they don’t want to get bloated, they may be less careful.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: There have been a number of fatalities where people have gone to a restaurant and alerted staff that they have an allergy to a particular food and the meal has been served up containing that allergen.” The guide says most internet and shop-bought allergy tests have no scientific basis.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: They include a home-testing kit that looks for specific IgG (immunoglobulin G) antibodies against food stuffs in the blood.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: While these antibodies are part of the immune system’s response to infections, “the best medical evidence has shown elevated IgG levels do not suggest an allergy”, the guide says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: “Results are frequently positive in individuals who do not have an allergy or a food intolerance.” Also debunked is a test, a mixture of acupuncture and homeopathy, which attempts to measure electronic resistance across the skin while the child or adult holds the suspect food in their hand.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: Hair follicle testing is also pointless, the guide says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: “Hair is not involved in allergic reactions so testing hair samples cannot provide any useful information on allergic status.” Nor should people be deceived into thinking allergies are caused by an “energy blockage” that can be diagnosed by muscle testing and cured by acupuncture.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: “I commonly see children who’ve been put on to unnecessarily restricted diets because their parents assume, in good faith, that they have allergies to multiple foods on the basis of ‘allergy tests’ that have no scientific basis,” said Paul Seddon, a consultant paediatric allergist, on behalf of the UK Cochrane Centre, an independent organization that assesses medical evidence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: “This needs to stop, which can only happen if we debunk these ‘tests’.” Another consultant paediatric allergist, Adam Fox from Guys and St Thomas’ Hospital in London, said: “I get a number of patients, and my colleagues likewise, who will come in having sent their hair off for analysis or having excluded a whole range of foods for their children.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: It is very difficult to untangle that.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: There are two challenges.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: Children need to be given proper diets but more of it is the unnecessary avoidance of things that aren’t harmful, which has a huge impact on the quality of life.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: A child who can’t eat wheat or drink milk can’t go to parties.” The conviction that a child’s chronic lethargy or headaches or eczema are caused by an allergy takes a long time and many tests to prove or disprove.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: It is tempting to go to an alternative therapist who will do a single test and provide a quick, but inaccurate, answer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0013
complex: Allergies are on the rise across developed countries.
label: ignore
