complex: They were among 16 individuals accused of fraud and other offences by the US Department of Justice as it set out a series of kickback schemes in a new 240-page indictment that superseded the previous one in May 2015.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: It takes to 27 the number of defendants charged by the US with a further 24 unnamed ‘co-conspirators’ including former FIFA executive committee members.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: “The betrayal of trust set forth here is outrageous,” the US Attorney General, Loretta Lynch, said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: “The scale of corruption alleged herein is completely unacceptable.” On a day when FIFA’s executive committee had hoped to present new reforms in the midst of an ongoing corruption crisis, Swiss police led away the president of the South American football confederation, the Paraguayan Juan Ángel Napout, and Alfredo Hawit, the head of the North and Central American and Caribbean governing body.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Hawit only succeeded Jeffrey Webb in May 2015, after Webb was arrested as part of the US operation that threw FIFA into crisis and precipitated the downfall of Sepp Blatter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Webb’s predecessor, the controversial Jack Warner, was also seized in May.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: The Swiss Federal Office of Justice said of the latest arrests: “They are being held in custody pending their extradition.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: According to the US arrest requests, they are suspected of accepting bribes of millions of dollars”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Webb and the Colombian former executive committee member Luis Bedoya were among those whose guilty pleas were entered in the US.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Lynch said that eight individuals, five of them unnamed in the original indictment, had come forward with guilty pleas since May.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Eleven current and former members of FIFA’s executive committee have now been charged in the investigation, which alleges $200m in bribes, mainly as kickbacks from TV and marketing contracts but also FIFA’s development programmes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: The last three presidents of the regional bodies CONCACAF and Conmebol have all been indicted.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: “The message from this announcement should be clear to every culpable individual who remains in the shadows, hoping to evade our investigation: you will not escape our focus,” said Lynch.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Teixeira, the former son-in-law of the longstanding FIFA president João Havelange, was charged alongside Del Nero and his predecessor, José Maria Marin, who was charged in May.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Fourteen men had been charged in May 2015, when four additional guilty pleas were entered.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Days later, Blatter won a fifth term as president but later agreed to step down as the crisis grew.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: He was then provisionally suspended alongside the UEFA President, Michel Platini, over an alleged £1.3m “disloyal payment” to the Frenchman.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Both men face possible life bans when their case is heard by the FIFA ethics committee in December if they are found guilty of the charges.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Among those also charged on Thursday were Rafael Salguero, a Guatemalan who left the executive committee in May; the former South American Confederation Secretary General, Eduardo Deluca; Peru’s former football federation president, Manuel Burga; and Bolivia’s football president, Carlos Chaves, already jailed in his own country.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Lynch said: “The Department of Justice is committed to ending the rampant corruption we have described amidst the leadership of international football – not only because of the scale of the schemes alleged earlier and today, or the breadth of the operation required to sustain such corruption, but also because of the affront to international principles that this behaviour represents.” The acting FIFA President, Issa Hayatou, refused to comment on the detail of the latest arrests.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: But he maintained neither he nor the organization was corrupt.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Appearing for the first time before the media since taking the role in September 2015, when Blatter was suspended, Hayatou responded in a similar way to his predecessors in improbably claiming the current crisis was down to a handful of errant individuals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: “FIFA is not corrupt.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: We have individuals that have shown negative behaviour.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: Do not generalize the situation,” said Hayatou, president of the Confederation of African Football for more than 25 years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0030
complex: “There are lots of people who have been in FIFA for more than 20 or 30 years that have not been accused of anything.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: A girl born today in the UK can expect to live nearly to the age of 82 on average, while her brother will live to 78.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: They would have a longer life in Andorra (85 and 79 respectively) but are marginally better off than in the US (81 and 76), while if they lived in the Central African Republic, they would barely make it out of middle age (49 and 44).
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: Nonetheless, almost everywhere in the world, with the exception of countries such as Lesotho, which have been hit by HIV and violence, lifespans are lengthening and the best news is that small children are substantially less likely to die than they were four decades ago.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: There has been a drop in deaths among under-fives of nearly 60%, from 16.4 million in 1970 to 6.8 million in 2010.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: That in itself is justification for the enormous project that the Institute of Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME) in Seattle has led over the past five years, involving nearly 500 researchers, to assess the global burden of disease.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: Knowing how many children die and from what cause enables the world to focus its efforts and resources on keeping them alive.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: There are many lessons to be gleaned from the vast database they have put together, which will help global organizations and individual governments to better care for us all – from a renewed focus on diet to tackling alcohol to keeping up the efforts against HIV in Africa.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: The seven papers published by The Lancet represent a big undertaking and are not without controversy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: IHME has been ambitiously radical in some of its methods.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: In the absence of death registries or medical records, they have been willing, for instance, to take evidence from verbal autopsies – deciding the cause of death by an interview with the family.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: The most startling result has been the malaria figure, released earlier in 2012.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: IHME said 1.2 million die of the disease every year – twice as many as previously thought.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: The big increase is in adult deaths.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: Conventional wisdom has it that malaria kills mostly children under five.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: “The way I was taught as a doctor and everybody else is taught is that, in malarial areas, you become semi-immune as an adult,” said Dr Christopher Murray, IHME Director and one of the founders of the Global Burden of Disease project.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: “We originally went with the prevailing opinion but there has been a shift as we have become more empirical.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: African doctors write on hospital records that adults are dying of malaria a lot.” But, he adds, their fever could be something else.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: The findings have prompted further studies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: Although Margaret Chan, Director General of the World Health Organization, gave the IHME study a warm official welcome, some of the staff are cautious.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: “We need to be very careful in assessing the validity [of the figures],” said Colin Mathers, a senior scientist in the Evidence for Information and Policy Cluster.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: “We need to wait to be persuaded by evidence.” His colleague Dr Tiers Boerma, Director of the WHO Department of Health Statistics and Informatics, added: “People should understand that some of the numbers are very different and the WHO can’t jump with any academic publication that states a different number.” However, said Mathers, “the fact that IHME has pushed the envelope with some of these analyses is stimulating”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: One of the main themes, said Murray, was “incredibly rapid change in the leading causes of death and the pace of that change is a lot faster than we expected it to be”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: Reduced fertility and longer life expectancies have meant a rise in the mean age of the world’s population in a decade, from 26 years old to almost 30.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: It has been dramatic in Latin America, for instance, where countries like Brazil and Paraguay had life expectancy of below 30 in 1970 and almost 64 in 2010.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: That is a 35-year increase in the mean age of death over four decades.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: “In a place like Brazil, the speed of change is so fast that most institutions are ill-equipped to deal with it,” Murray said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: The second theme, entwined with it, is the shift outside Africa from communicable diseases and the common causes of mother and baby deaths to what are sometimes termed “lifestyle” diseases, such as heart disease, stroke, diabetes and cancer – some of which have significant genetic triggers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: That shift has been particularly marked in Latin America, the Middle East and south-east and even south Asia, he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: The third big finding was, Murray said, “a surprise to us”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: That was the sheer extent of disability and the toll it took on people who were living longer but not healthier lives.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: “The main causes of disability are different from the ones that kill you,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: They were mental health problems such as anxiety and depression, musculoskeletal disorders such as arthritis and lower back pain – complained of in every country in the world – anaemia, sight and hearing loss and skin disease.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: In addition, there was substance abuse.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: “The rates for these are not going down over time,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0031
complex: “We are making no progress in reducing these conditions.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: They are the darkness seekers – and they are growing in number.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: On Black Fell, looking down on Northumberland’s beautiful Kielder Water reservoir, a group of people wait in a car park next to a strange wooden building with a minimalist design beamed down from the future.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: This is Kielder Observatory, the centre of Britain’s nascent astrotourism industry.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: And those waiting outside were the lucky ones.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Many more had applied for a night of stargazing at the observatory but numbers are strictly limited.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Inside, next to a woodburner and under dimmed lights, the observatory’s founder and lead astronomer, Gary Fildes, a former bricklayer with Tarzan hair, delivers a pep talk to his colleagues and volunteers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: The team discusses the prospect of seeing the northern lights but Fildes is doubtful.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Instead, they decide to train their powerful telescopes on Jupiter and Venus and later to pick out stars such as Capella and Betelgeuse.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: An additional attraction is the appearance of the International Space Station.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “Remember,” Fildes tells his team, “it’s about interaction, it’s about entertainment, it’s about inspiring people.” He puts on some music.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Pink Floyd, the Jam, the Pogues.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “By 9.30, the sky is going to be sexy,” Fildes says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “It’s going to be epic.” Fildes, 49, is at the forefront of the UK’s burgeoning astrotourism industry.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: The pivotal moment for Northumberland came in 2013 when the entire national park housing Hadrian’s Wall, along with Kielder Water and Forest Park, some 1,500 sq km, was awarded Dark Sky Park status, the only one in England.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Dark Sky Parks are rare.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: The 2013 Star Count revealed that only 5% of the UK population can see more than 31 stars on a good night.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: The Tucson, Arizona-based International Dark-Sky Association (IDA) confers the status only on places that take major steps to avoid light pollution.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Recipients must also prove their night skies are sufficiently dark.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: In Northumberland Dark Sky Park, as the area was rebadged, it is so dark that Venus casts a shadow on the Earth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Duncan Wise, visitor development officer for the Northumberland National Park Authority, helped to spearhead the campaign for dark-sky status after the Council for the Protection of Rural England found it was one of Britain’s most tranquil places.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “We tend to look at landscape as everything up to the horizon,” Wise said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “But what about what’s above it?” Wise and others spent years drawing up their submission to the IDA, collecting reams of light readings and forming an alliance of local councils, parks’ bodies and community groups to produce an exterior lighting master plan that influences the construction of new developments in the area.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Their efforts have been vindicated.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Many of the 1.5 million who visit Northumberland each year are now aware of its Dark Sky status.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “We get a lot of people coming here to see the sky now,” says the man at the car-hire firm in Newcastle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “They come in autumn and winter, when it’s darkest.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Good for the B&Bs as they get business all year round now.” Local hoteliers now issue guests with night-vision torches and put out deckchairs at night.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Those who have acquired some knowledge of astronomy can receive a badge confirming that their hotels are “Dark Sky Friendly”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Wise acknowledges that Northumberland needs to do more to capitalize on its scarce resource and believes the region needs a couple more observatories to ensure that visitors will see what they came for.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: A £14m national landscape discovery centre, which he describes as the north’s answer to the Eden Project, will have an observatory when it is completed in a couple of years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Fildes has grand designs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: He is planning Britain’s first “astrovillage”, one that would house the largest public observatory in the world and boast a 100-seat auditorium, a 100-seat planetarium, a one-metre aperture telescope, and radiomagnetic and solar telescopes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: The multimillion-pound project would feature a hotel and draw in 100,000 people a year, four times the number currently able to use the observatory.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Fildes is cryptic about his backers but believes the astrovillage will be a reality by 2018.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: However, Northumberland faces competition.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Galloway Forest Park in Scotland also has Dark Sky Park status.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Since Exmoor was designated Europe’s first International Dark Sky Reserve – one notch below Dark Sky Park – in 2011, a range of local businesses offering stargazing breaks and safaris has sprung up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: The UK will have to go some way to eclipse northern Chile, which boasts more than a dozen tourist observatories and has some of the clearest skies in the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: The Teide National Park in Tenerife is also becoming a major astrotourism destination.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: So, what is driving the desire to look upwards?
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: The media have helped.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: TV presenters like Brian Cox have attracted a new generation of stargazers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “Brian Cox has made astronomy accessible,” says Wise.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “It’s no longer seen as the province of professors in studies with brass telescopes.” Technology has also played a part.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Apps such as Stellarium now turn smartphones into pocket-size planetariums.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: Ultimately, though, Fildes believes people are starting to appreciate what lies above.
label: ignore

title: item_p0032
complex: “If you had to build a visitor attraction from scratch, what could be better than the universe?”
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: Junior Smart knows a lot about gangs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He is now 36 and his life can be divided into two distinct phases.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He tells how in his late teens, after his mother died, he became drawn into a south London gang, which, at the time, helped fill a huge vacuum.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “They became my new support group,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “At first it was just a bit of fun but then it became more serious, more and more about making money.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: They got involved in criminality.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: That is how it was.” At school Smart failed his GCSEs, then retook them at college and passed the lot.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He secured a full-time job in administration and worked as a DJ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: But, on the side, he was making money illegally as part of the gang.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: Eventually he was arrested for serious drug-related offences and was sent to prison for 12 years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: Instantly, he says, his sense of invincibility was shattered.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “The first night after I was arrested was the biggest wake-up call of my life,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “I had been living a dual life.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: I had been living as one person to my peers and another person to my peers’ enemies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: I spent a long time sorting myself out.” Today, Junior Smart runs a team of 12 full-time workers and six volunteers, which aims to turn young criminals and gang members away from crime.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: Most of those working there are, like Smart himself, ex-offenders.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: A few are still serving their sentences but are regarded as having reformed enough to be allowed out during the day to help.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: They work with the police, the probation service and other, voluntary organizations to help those who feel trapped and frightened in the violent criminal gangs that operate across London.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: For Smart, the extraordinary journey from gang member to mentor began when he witnessed, from within, a prison system that was so obviously failing its inmates.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He recalls a drug addict he befriended who, to his dismay, kept returning.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “I was touched by the people who kept coming back in,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “I couldn’t believe that nothing was being done about it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: I was talking to the inmates and they knew what needed to be changed in their lives, but the problem is that the prison system only deals with the ‘index’ offence.” “One guy had a £300-a-week cocaine habit, which he funded through burglary.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He would tell me stuff about how he would walk into a house, even when he knew people were there.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: So although he had a drug addiction, it never got dealt with.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: The thing that got me about that is that it is simply a revolving door.” That case and scores of others persuaded Smart to start working as a prison “listener” – an inmate who helps reassure new arrivals and talks them through their first days inside.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: From there he developed what he calls his own “little plan” to run his own scheme once out of prison – using the experience of ex-offenders to help others reject the revolving door of prison life.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He was released early, after five years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “At the end of my sentence I got an opportunity to put my little model into practice,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: So what does he think now?
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: Does he believe that, after the riots of last August, and the government’s promise to crack down on gangs, things are getting better?
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He is careful to offer some praise to the police and says much of their work in hauling in gang members has been good.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: But overall he is highly critical of a disjointed government approach that believes that, once the leader of a gang is arrested, the problem is solved.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He agrees with the findings of a report that says the arrest of gang leaders can even make things worse.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He says the effect of removing the leader is often to destabilize the entire gang.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He draws a diagram of the hierarchy on a pad.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “When you arrest the top guy, people start fighting for position all the way up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: Who was the most loyal?
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: Who had the most respect?
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: It is a bit like a family.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: They are more likely to act out, through violence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: It means that the arrest of the gang leaders has been nullified because it has not had a long-term effect.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: They have not even given the community a respite.” Can it actually make the streets more dangerous?
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “It can do, because in the vacuum the recruitment and manipulation of young people becomes even more prevalent, creating new lower levels all the way down ... People take sides.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: If one gang or another territorial street network knows that an elder [leader] has been taken out, then they suddenly think that gang’s weak ... And so we have inter-estate disputes going on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: And what happens when that elder is in prison?
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: He forms alliances with other gang members, or when he gets released he then tries to retake control.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: That is when violence happens.” In order to spread risk down to the lowest levels, he says gangs are now recruiting far more in primary schools.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: The youngest members are called “tinies”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “Over the last years we have seen more and more of this.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: The tinies can be as young as eight to eleven years old.” The young are brought in to shield their seniors from risk.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: It is often they who are charged with doing the street dealing or even the stabbing, he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: Smart says that, with a lack of government funding and commitment to long-term rehabilitation, the challenges are immense, particularly in the current economic climate.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “I try to engage a young person who has been earning £300 a week through illegal methods.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: It was hard before, to try to convince him.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: But with unemployment high and cuts to benefits, it makes things tougher.” That said, his project, which has well over 1,000 clients, is delivering results.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: Fewer than 20% of those who come in for help reoffend.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: From personal experience, Smart refuses to write off a single individual as beyond redemption and that is what drives him on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0033
complex: “I don’t think that about anybody,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: It was a beautiful summer evening and I decided to go for a swim off Doolin Pier in County Clare, Ireland, where I moved in 2012.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: There was a woman in the water with Dusty, a dolphin who has a great relationship with a group of people she regularly swims with.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: Dusty arrived in Doolin in about 2008 and hundreds of people have swum with her since, giving the impression that she’s totally tame.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: She has even starred in an Irish tourist-board ad campaign in which a girl says she would like to touch a dolphin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: That evening, this woman was tickling Dusty’s tummy and it just looked so inviting.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: There were about 20 tourists and locals on the pier, looking at this lovely spectacle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: Just after I got into the water, Dusty left the woman she was with and went ballistic – I found out afterwards that she’s very territorial when she is with somebody.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: Her tail was flapping wildly and, at first, I thought it was a display but, then, I twigged: maybe she’s angry.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I knew I had to get out of the water so I swam towards the pier but, within microseconds, Dusty had ploughed into me with her snout.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: It was very powerful and painful, and the speed was amazing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I went hurtling forwards.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: All these people on the pier were staring down at me open-mouthed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: Dusty was still in the water beside me, her tail flapping crazily.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: That was more frightening than anything: I thought, if she hits me with her tail, I could go under; I’m gone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I was at the pier but couldn’t get out because of my injuries.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I felt pure terror.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I shouted for help and a guy put his arm in and pulled me out on to the steps.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: Then, another man appeared and said he was an orthopaedic surgeon who specialized in marine trauma.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: He had been driving into Doolin when he saw what was happening on the pier.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I was so cold and very worried – I didn’t know how bad my injuries were and my biggest fear was internal bleeding.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: He checked me over and was very reassuring, saying he couldn’t feel any evidence of it but that I probably had broken bones.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I found out later that I had six spinal fractures, three broken ribs and a damaged lung.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I was in hospital for five days, in a back brace for several weeks and off work for five months with limited mobility, stiffness and pain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: Then, I was diagnosed with post-traumatic stress.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: My near-death experience had left me anxious about everything and overreacting in a way I had never done before.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I felt that people were looking at me in the wrong way, I began to struggle with loud noises and I suffered from memory loss.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: Three months before the accident, I had opened a health-food shop but I had to let it go because I could no longer work.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: It was the toughest year ever but, now, it’s all behind me.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I had craniosacral therapy, osteopathy and massage, and am building up my own osteopathy practice now.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I have a new empathy with patients because I have been one.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I am grateful that I am healthy and I really want to prevent other people being injured.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: We have this lovely idea about dolphins and have faith in them – who would think a dolphin would ever attack a person?
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: If you see a ferocious animal coming at you with its teeth bared, it’s scary, but dolphins have this lovely, wide smile.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I don’t have any anger towards Dusty.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I respect her.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: But I was in her territory and she’s a wild, unpredictable animal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: People need to know that.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: So many come here to swim with her and they don’t understand how dangerous it can be.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: Mine were reportedly among several injuries that summer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: After the man pulled me out of the water, Dusty swam away but, then, she came back and was bobbing vertically next to me, looking at me.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: We locked eyes and I felt there was complete remorse in her.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: She was a totally different dolphin; the anger had gone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: The people on the pier were in awe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: When she had that little moment with me, that was the end of the terror.
label: ignore

title: item_p0034
complex: I made my peace with her.

title: item_p0035
complex: In Canada’s Arctic, summers are marked by a bright light that bathes the treeless tundra for more than 20 hours a day.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: For some, it’s a welcome change from the unrelenting darkness of winter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: But, for the small but growing Muslim community of Iqaluit, Nunavut, life in the land of the midnight sun poses a real challenge during the month of Ramadan, during which Muslims typically fast from sunrise to sunset.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “I haven’t fainted once,” said 29-year-old Abdul Karim, one of the few in the city who has carefully timed his Ramadan fast to the Arctic sun since moving from Ottawa in 2011.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: This year, that means eating at about 1.30am before the sun rises and breaking his fast at about 11pm when the sun sets.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “The only reason to stop would be if it hurts my health,” Karim said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: Pointing to his sizable frame, he laughed as he added: “But, looking at my condition, I don’t think fasting will hurt me.” As the end of Ramadan draws near for Muslims around the world, much of the holy month’s focus on community work, prayer and reflection has been a constant in communities around the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: But in Iqaluit and the other Muslim communities in the Arctic, the long days have forced a shift in how the element of fasting is approached.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: Most in Iqaluit adhere to the timetable followed by Muslims in Ottawa, some 1,300 miles south of the city – following the advice of Muslim scholars who have said Muslims in the far north should observe Ramadan using the timetable of Mecca or the nearest Muslim city.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: It still means fasting for some 18 hours a day, said Atif Jilani, who moved to Iqaluit from Toronto in 2015.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “It’s long days, but more manageable.” Many in the 100-strong community break their fast together, gathering in the city’s brand new mosque – completed in February amid temperatures that dropped as low as -50C with wind chill – for nightly suppers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: As they tuck into traditional foods such as dates and goat or lamb curries, the sun shines brightly through the windows.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: It’s a scene that plays out across Canada’s northernmost mosques during Ramadan, as Muslim communities wrestle with the country’s unique geography.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: The 300 or so Muslims in Yellowknife, in the Northwest Territories, have several options when it comes to fasting during Ramadan, said Nazim Awan, president of the Yellowknife Islamic Centre, with exceptions made for those who are pregnant or ill. “There might be some superhumans who want to fast for 23 hours, but the other option is to follow the intent and spirit of fasting by following nearby cities or they can follow the times of Mecca and Medina.” In recent years, much of the community has opted to follow the Ramadan timetable of Edmonton, in Alberta.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: Some, such as Awan – a father of two young children, including a 12-yearold who recently started fasting – follow the timings of Mecca.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: He hopes to encourage his son with the more manageable timetable of about 15 hours of fasting as compared with about 18 hours in Edmonton.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “If I fast Yellowknife or Edmonton times, my son might say, ‘Papa, you are really insane.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: What are you doing?’” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: Faced with the impossibility of following the local movements of the sun, the 100 or so Muslims in Inuvik, a small town that sits 125 miles north of the Arctic Circle, have also been following Edmonton’s timetable.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “We currently have 24 hours a day of sun,” said Ahmad Alkhalaf.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “There’s no sunrise or sunset.” The adherence to Edmonton’s schedule was already in place in 2001 when he moved from Toronto to the small northern community of 3,500 people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “My first Ramadan here was in December.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: There’s no sun at that time; it’s dark all day and night.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: So we used Edmonton time.” At times, it can be psychologically challenging to follow the clock rather than what is happening outside, Alkhalaf said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “You’re supposed to break your fast when it’s dusk and we eat when the sun is out.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: It’s not usual to have iftar [the meal breaking the fast] when the sun is up,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: In Inuvik, where much of the population is Inuit, the Muslim community has sought to strike a balance between Ramadan and the local culture and traditions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: The iftar meal includes dates and rich curries – as well as local game such as reindeer, prepared in accordance with Islamic law.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “We make a soup or curry … but instead of using beef, we use reindeer.” In Iqaluit, as the Muslim community prepares to mark the end of Ramadan, some reflect that 2016’s timing – stretching across some of the longest days of the year – has made it one of the more challenging of recent years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: It’s particularly true for those like Karim who have determinedly followed the local sunrise and sunset.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: But, his efforts will be rewarded years from now, said Karim, thanks to the lunar calendar.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: Ramadan will eventually fall during winter, which, in Iqaluit, sees the sun rise and set within a few hours each day.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “I’ll follow those hours, too,” he said with a laugh.
label: ignore

title: item_p0035
complex: “Oh yes, definitely.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: It was not so much how hard people found the challenge but how far they would go to avoid it that left researchers gobsmacked.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: The task?
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: To sit in a chair and do nothing but think.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: So unbearable did some nd it that they took up the safe but alarming opportunity to give themselves mild electric shocks in an attempt to break the tedium.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: Two-thirds of men pressed a button to deliver a painful jolt during a 15-minute spell of solitude.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: Under the same conditions, a quarter of women pressed the shock button.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: The difference, scientists suspect, is that men tend to be more sensation-seeking than women.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: The report from psychologists at Virginia and Harvard Universities is one of a surprising few to tackle the question of why most of us nd it so hard to do nothing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: In more than 11 separate studies, the researchers showed that people hated being left to think, regardless of their age, education, income or the amount of time they spent using smartphones or social media.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: Timothy Wilson, who led the work, said the ndings were not necessarily a re ection of the pace of modern life or the spread of mobile devices and social media.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: Instead, those things might be popular because of our constant urge to do something rather than nothing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: The rst run of experiments began with students being ushered Ð alone, without phones, books or anything to write with Ð into an unadorned room and told to think.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: The only rules were they had to stay seated and not fall asleep.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: They were informed Ð speci cally or vaguely Ð that they would have six to 15 minutes alone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: The students were questioned when the time was up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: On average, they did not enjoy the experience.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: They struggled to concentrate.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: Their minds wandered even with nothing to distract them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: Even giving them time to think about what to think about did not help.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: In case the unfamiliar setting hampered the ability to think, the researchers ran the experiment again with people at home.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: They got much the same results, only people found the experience even more miserable and cheated by getting up from their chair or checking their phones.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: To see if the effect was found only in students, the scientists recruited more than 100 people, aged 18 to 77, from a church and a farmersÕ market.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: They, too, disliked being left to their thoughts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: But, the most staggering result was yet to come.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: To check whether people might actually prefer something bad to nothing at all, the students were given the option of administering a mild electric shock.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: They had been asked earlier to rate how unpleasant the shocks were, alongside other options, such as looking at pictures of cockroaches or hearing the sound of a knife rubbing against a bottle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: All the students picked for the test said they would pay to avoid mild electric shocks after receiving a demonstration.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: To the researchersÕ surprise, 12 of 18 men gave themselves up to four electric shocks, as did six of 24 women.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: ÒWhat is striking is that simply being alone with their thoughts was apparently so aversive that it drove many participants to self-administer an electric shock that they had earlier said they would pay to avoid,Ó the scientists write in Science.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: Jessica Andrews-Hanna at the University of Colorado said many students would probably zap themselves to cheer up a tedious lecture.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: But, she says more needs to be known about the motivation of the shockers in WilsonÕs study.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: ÒImagine the setup Ð a person is told to sit in a chair with wires attached to their skin and a button that will deliver a harmless but uncomfortable shock, and they are told to just sit there and entertain themselves with their thoughts,Ó she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: ÒAs they sit there, strapped to this machine, their mind starts to wander and it naturally goes to that shock Ð was it really that bad?
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: ÒWhat are the experimenters really interested in?
label: ignore

title: item_p0036
complex: Perhaps this is a case where curiosity killed the cat.Ó
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Not nearly enough exercise classes have a tea break halfway through.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: But Margaret Allen’s does.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: After a gentle warm-up and a few pulse-raising numbers, the 93-year-old great-grandmother lets her charges rehydrate with a cup of tea and a quick sit down.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Some of the eight-strong class look as if they need it more than others.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Allen herself, wearing a thick turquoise shirt, navy knitted waistcoat, black slacks and sensible shoes, has not broken into a sweat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Despite an “excruciating” trapped nerve in one leg and a knee in need of replacement, she looks as though she could go on for hours.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: The general rule is that eating directly before sport is not the best idea, let alone part way through.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: But, on the afternoon I visit Allen’s class at a church hall in Saltburn-by-the-Sea near Middlesbrough, slices of fruitcake are being passed around during the break.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: The cake has been baked in honour of Allen’s recent birthday by her 89-year-old sister, Joan, known locally as the “scone queen of Saltburn”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: The ladies have barely swallowed their last crumb when Allen is up again, leading the group through a jaunty Scottish number involving lots of toe pointing and leg kicking.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Forty-five minutes later, the class is finally over.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Allen, a former volunteer with the Red Cross, has been leading classes in the north-east seaside town for 45 years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Not particularly sporty at school, she started playing the piano for a keep-fit class during the second world war – “just for something to do during the blackouts, really” – and eventually took over in her 40s when the previous instructor retired.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: At its peak, Allen’s class had more than 18 regulars, each paying £1 a time.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: But, these days, her flock is diminishing fast – during the teabreak, the ladies discuss a funeral that most of them had attended that week for one of the younger members of the group who had just died, aged 68, from motor neurone disease.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Allen is the oldest, followed by her sister.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: The baby of the group is 60-year-old Jean Cunion, who credits the group with supporting her through a difficult time when her mother died.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: She is somewhat embarrassed to admit that she is perhaps the least fit of the group.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: “I remember, the first time I came, Margaret said: ‘Who’s that huffing and panting?’ and I had to admit it was me.” Ruth Steere, 76, marvels at how Allen never misses a trick, despite always having her back to the class: “She always shouts at us if we go wrong.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: She’s remarkably good at knowing what we are doing.” Allen, a keen dancer, has never done any formal training to be a fitness instructor.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Instead, she choreographs her own moves based on five tapes from the BBC’s first ever fitness guru, Eileen Fowler, who died in 2000 when she was 93, Allen’s age now.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Allen thinks her good health is largely down to keeping busy, especially since her husband Joe died in 1997.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: She took up writing poetry when she was 80.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: “I write poems about everything.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: I’m a prolific writer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: I just can’t stop,” she says, phoning me a few days after the interview to read out a ditty she has written about the joys of exercise.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: One of the class, 84-year-old former teacher Winnie Robertson, thinks the secret to staying fit is never letting yourself go: “Use it or lose it, that’s what I say.” Allen still plays the piano and gives speeches.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: She is president of the Women’s Fellowship at the local methodist church and is one of three 90-plus year olds at the scrabble club of the University of the Third Age.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: She did a computer course when she was 88 and tried to get online, but it didn’t work out.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Ageing is no fun, she admits, reading me a few lines from a poem she has written called ‘That Beast Called Age’.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: She happily recalls a doctor who saw her for the first time a few years back, who said she couldn’t possibly be more than 78: “I said, ‘Thank you, doctor.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: You can go now.’” She also has a no-nonsense attitude to weight gain: “I just think people shouldn’t eat too much.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Whenever I hear someone saying, ‘Oh, I can’t lose weight’, I say: ‘Sellotape.’” She mimes taping her mouth shut.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: “I said this just the other day to a big fat man.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: Everything in moderation is my motto.” Earlier in 2013, Allen was watching the news and saw a woman being given the British Empire Medal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: I think she means Margaret Chartwood from Horley, who was given the honour in January, at the age of 77.
label: ignore

title: item_p0037
complex: “She was saying: ‘I’m 80 and I’m the oldest fitness instructor in the country!’ I was thinking: ‘No, you’re not.’ But I shan’t be writing to Buckingham Palace.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: On the market square in Rjukan stands a statue of the town’s founder, a noted Norwegian engineer and industrialist called Sam Eyde.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: The great man stares northwards across the square at an almost sheer mountainside in front of him.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Behind him, to the south, rises the equally sheer 1,800-metre peak known as Gaustatoppen.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Between the mountains, along the narrow Vestfjord valley, lies the small, but once mighty, town that Eyde built in the early years of the last century, to house the workers for his factories.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Eyde harnessed the power of the 100- metre Rjukanfossen waterfall to generate hydroelectricity in what was, at the time, the world’s biggest power plant.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: But one thing he couldn’t do was change the elevation of the sun.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Deep in its east–west valley, surrounded by high mountains, Rjukan and its 3,400 inhabitants are in shadow for half the year.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: During the day, from late September to mid-March, the town, three hours north-west of Oslo, is not completely dark, but it’s certainly not bright, either.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Recently, however, Eyde’s statue has gazed out upon a sight that even he might have found startling.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: High on the mountain opposite, 450 metres above the town, three large, solar-powered, computer-controlled mirrors steadily track the movement of the sun across the sky, reflecting its rays down on to the square and bathing it in bright sunlight.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “It’s the sun!” grins Ingrid Sparbo, disbelievingly, lifting her face to the light and closing her eyes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: A retired secretary, Sparbo has lived all her life in Rjukan and says people “do sort of get used to the shade.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: You end up not thinking about it, really.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: But this ... this is so warming.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Not just physically, but mentally.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: It’s mentally warming.” Two young mothers wheel their children into the square and stand in the sun.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: On a freezing day, an elderly couple sit on one of the new benches, smiling at the warmth on their faces.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Children beam.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Lots of people take photographs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: A shop assistant, Silje Johansen, says it’s “awesome.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Just awesome.” Pushing his child’s buggy, electrical engineer Eivind Toreid is more cautious.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “It’s a funny thing,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “Not real sunlight, but very like it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Like a spotlight.” Heidi Fieldheim says she heard all about it on the radio.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “But it’s far more than I expected,” she says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “This will bring much happiness.” Across the road, in the Nye Tider café, sits the man responsible for this unexpected access to happiness.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Martin Andersen is a 40-year-old artist who moved to Rjukan in the summer of 2001.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: The first inkling of an artwork Andersen called the Solspeil, or Sun mirror, came to him as the month of September began to fade: “Every day, we would take our young child for a walk in the buggy,” he says, “and, every day, I realized we were having to go a little further down the valley to find the sun.” By 28 September, the sun completely disappears from Rjukan’s market square.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: It doesn’t reappear until 12 March.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Throughout the seemingly endless intervening months, Andersen says, “We’d look up and see blue sky above, and the sun high on the mountain slopes, but the only way we could get to it was to go out of town.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: It’s sad, a town that people have to leave in order to feel the sun.” Twelve years after he first dreamed of his Solspeil, a German company specializing in so-called CSP – concentrated solar power – helicoptered in the three 17-sq-m glass mirrors that now stand high above the market square in Rjukan.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “It took,” he says, “a bit longer than we’d imagined.” It really works.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: There were objectors – and plenty of them – petitions and letter-writing campaigns and a Facebook page organized against what a large number of locals saw initially as a vanity project and, above all, a waste of money.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: But even they now seem largely won over.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “I was strongly against it,” admits Nils Eggerud.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Like many others, he felt the money could have been better spent elsewhere – on a couple of extra carers to look after Rjukan’s old people, perhaps, or improved school facilities, cycle paths, a bit of rural road resurfacing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “And I still have my doubts about the ongoing maintenance costs,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “What will they be, who will pay them?
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: But ... well, it does feel nice, standing here.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: And, really, you just have to look at the people’s faces.” In his office overlooking the square, Rjukan’s energetic young mayor, Steinar Bergsland, is interested not so much in the cost but in the benefits the mirrors might bring to the town.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Already, Bergsland says, visitor numbers are up for the time of year and Rjukan’s shopkeepers have reported their takings following suit.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: A hi-tech company is interested in relocating to Rjukan, attracted by the cutting-edge technology on view at the top of the mountain and the publicity it has attracted.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: “This is a powerful symbol for Rjukan,” Bergsland says, and, helped by assorted government grants and a donation from a local business, the town needed to find just 1m krone – £100,000 – of the mirrors’ total 5m-krone cost.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: And, seen against the town’s 650m krone annual budget, he points out, 1m krone really wasn’t very much to pay for something that “gives us a far, far better chance of raising the money we need for better schools and more nursing care.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: And just look out of the window.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Look at those happy faces.
label: ignore

title: item_p0038
complex: Now it’s actually here, people love it.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Sleep deprivation used to be a badge of honour: a sign you were busy and important and very much in demand.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Snoozing was losing and sleep was for wimps.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Now, however, Arianna Huffington’s The Sleep Revolution, a ‘call to bed’ that promises to transform your life “one night at a time”, is a New York Times best-seller and Huffington is urging people to “sleep their way to the top”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Meanwhile, the sleep industry has woken up big time and a whole range of start-ups are reinventing where, when and how we sleep, as well as how much we’re prepared to pay for it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: For the more upmarket snoozer, luxury hotels are offering “sleep retreats”; more than $1,000 gets you dinner and a movie about sleep.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: And, if you’re staying home, you can upgrade your bedroom with everything from a mattress cover with a sensor that tracks your sleep ($249) to a brainwave-monitoring sleeping mask that lets you nap more efficiently ($299).
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Sleep hasn’t just been corporatized – it has infiltrated corporations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: A number of companies already have nap pods and Huffington predicts that nap rooms in offices are going to become “as common as conference rooms” in the next two years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: So, how did this happen?
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: How did sleep, something humans have done since long before Huffington awoke to it, suddenly become so fashionable?
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Getting enough sleep is a natural fit for the sort of lifestyle in which paying $10 for green juice and $34 for a SoulCycle class is the norm.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Then, there’s the rise of the quantified self through wearable technology.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Our bodies have become input/output devices that we monitor and optimize for greater efficiency and sleep has become another data set to be tracked.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: What Huffington emphasizes about sleep, after all, is not that it rests you but that it restores you.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Sleep, she says, is the ultimate performance enhancer and getting eight hours of rest has become the ultimate status symbol.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: You know how Arianna Huffington gets her eight hours?
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Well, for one thing, she has “nine or so” assistants, according to a recent New York Times profile.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Huffington calls them her “A-Team”; they do everything from running her errands to planning her travel to loading The Huffington Post on her computer in the morning.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: According to the Times, most of the A-Team can only endure about 12 months of the work because it’s so taxing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: The low pay also means many of them take second jobs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Basically, they don’t sleep so that Huffington can … and can sell books about it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Getting enough sleep isn’t just a question of valuing sleep enough to go to bed at the right time; it’s a question of going to bed in the right neighbourhood and in the right body.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Numerous studies show that you’re more likely to sleep poorly if you’re poor.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: It’s hard to sleep if you’re worried about your safety or haven’t had enough to eat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: It’s hard to sleep if you’re one of the 15 million American shift workers who work irregular hours.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Research has also found that there’s a black/white sleep gap.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: One study shows that, while white people sleep an average of 6.85 hours, African Americans sleep an average of 6.05 hours.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: They also have a lower quality of sleep.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Researchers have attributed this, in part, to the stress of discrimination.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Want to know who gets the most sleep and the best quality of sleep in America?
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Wealthy white women.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Which, if I’d hazard a guess, is probably the same demographic Huffington is targeting her book at.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Huffington describes her promotion of sleep as a “revolution” but, really, it’s a rebranding.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: The very real sleep crisis we face isn’t down to a few rich people thinking it’s a waste of time; it’s down to the 99% not being able to afford to spend time sleeping.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: While sleep is currently enjoying a moment, it will probably be short-lived.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Sleep may be a performance enhancer but it’s an inefficient one.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: The real prize is finding a way to negate sleep deprivation so humans can function on less sleep.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Unsurprisingly, the military is at the forefront of this research.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: In 2008, the Pentagon published a report called “Human Performance” which examined the possibility of a future in which soldiers could perform at their peak with only a couple of hours’ sleep.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: “Suppose a human could be engineered who slept for the same amount of time as a giraffe (1.9 hours per night).
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: This would lead to an approximately twofold decrease in the casualty rate.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: An adversary would need an approximately 40% increase in the troop level to compensate for this advantage.” The report goes on to look at the effects of ampakines, a class of drugs that modulate neurotransmitters in the brain, to remove the effects of sleep deprivation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Eventually, humans will figure out a way to get rid of sleep.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: Spending a third of your life unconscious won’t be a luxury anymore; it’ll be something only the poor will be forced to do.
label: ignore

title: item_p0039
complex: At which point, we may need a whole new sort of sleep revolution.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: For 85 years, it was little more than a featureless grey blob on classroom maps of the solar system, but, on 15 July, Pluto was seen in high resolution for the first time, revealing dramatic mountain ranges made from solid water ice on a scale to rival the Alps or the Rockies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The extraordinary images of the former ninth planet and its large moon, Charon, beamed 4bn miles back to Earth from the New Horizons spacecraft, mark the climax of a mission that has been quietly underway for nearly a decade.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: Alan Stern, the mission’s principal investigator, described the images as a “home run” for the team.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: “New Horizons is returning amazing results already.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The data look absolutely gorgeous, and Pluto and Charon are just mind-blowing.” One of the biggest surprises was the discovery that “there are mountains in the Kuiper belt”, the solar system’s mysterious “third zone” where Pluto sits amid around 100,000 smaller icy objects.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: John Spencer, a mission scientist, said the mountains appear to be around 11,000ft high and several hundred miles across.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: “These are pretty significant mountains.” The detailed image of one edge of the dwarf planet showed not a single crater, hinting that the surface has been recently “paved over” by geological activity, which could include dramatic geysers blasting plumes of ice into the atmosphere or cryo-volcanoes that erupt in explosions of ice rather than molten rock.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: In a nod to Pluto’s former status as the ninth planet, until it was downgraded to a dwarf planet in 2006, the NASA press conference began with a rundown of spectacular images of the sun and the eight official planets.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: “We’ve brought what was previously a blurred point of light into focus,” said Dwayne Brown, NASA spokesman, as scientists and journalists waited for the image to be unveiled.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: Stern described the images as “just skimming the surface” of what would be learnt about the planet during the coming year.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: They have already produced some surprises.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: Scientists believe the mountains are made from water ice with just a thin veneer of “exotic” ices, methane and nitrogen.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: “You can’t make mountains out of methane and nitrogen,” said Spencer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: “Water ice is strong enough to hold up big mountains and that’s what we think we’re seeing here.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: This is the first time we’ve seen this.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The methane and nitrogen are just a coating.” The mountains on Pluto are likely to have formed no more than 100m years ago – extremely recently given the 4.56bn-year-old solar system.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: This suggests the close-up region, which covers about 1% of Pluto’s surface, may still be geologically active.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The images are the first to show ice mountains outside of the moons of giant planets and raises the question of what kind of geological process could be generating the mountainous landscape.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The structures, together with the smoothness of Pluto’s surface, suggest that geological activity is taking place and smoothing over depressions caused by asteroid impacts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: Scientists believe this “paving” process could be the result of internal heat that softens rock and ice or from snowfalls that cover the surface.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: For scale, the images are so detailed that, if the craft were flying over London, we would be able to pick out the runways at Heathrow airport.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The distance to Pluto – 5bn km – means it takes New Horizons hours to send back a single picture and it will take 16 months to send all the data it has accumulated during the fly-by.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The team also announced that the heart-shaped feature visible on Pluto will now be known as the Tombaugh Regio, in honour of Clyde Tombaugh, who discovered the dwarf planet in 1930.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The new view of Charon reveals a varied, complex terrain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: An area of cliffs and troughs stretching about 1,000km suggests widespread fracturing of Charon’s surface, which could also be the result of geological activity.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The image also shows a dramatic canyon estimated to be 7 to 9km deep.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: Cathy Olkin, a mission scientist, said: “Charon just blew our socks off when we had the new image today.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: The team has just been abuzz.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: There is so much interesting science in this one image alone.” Pluto is thought to be composed of about two thirds rock encased in a lot of ice, with surface temperatures of about minus 230C.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: As the £460m mission travels onwards into the Kuiper belt, scientists hope that it will open up a window on the ancient solar system and the origins of planets, potentially helping to explain the formation of the Earth itself.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: Andrew Coates, head of planetary science at the Mullard Space Science Laboratory, said: “These Kuiper belt objects are the building blocks of the outer solar system.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: They’re all very cold – it’s like a cosmic deep freeze.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: It’s the best way of preserving solar system history.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: That is what is so fascinating about this.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: It’s a really thrilling time for solar system exploration.” In August 2015, mission scientists will choose which of two objects to visit next.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: NASA estimates that the spacecraft will be able to keep recording and transmitting until the mid-2030s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: Then, its plutonium power source will run out and it will shut down, drifting outwards towards the edge of the solar system and deep space beyond.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: New Horizons also observed the smaller members of the Pluto system, which includes four other moons: Nix, Hydra, Styx and Kerberos.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: A new sneak-peek image of Hydra is the first to reveal its apparent irregular shape and its size, estimated to be about 43 by 33km.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: “New Horizons is a true mission of exploration, showing us why basic scientific research is so important,” said John Grunsfeld, associate administrator for NASA’s Science Mission Directorate.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: “The mission has had nine years to build expectations about what we would see during closest approach to Pluto and Charon.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: Today, we get the first sampling of the scientific treasure collected during those critical moments and I can tell you it dramatically surpasses those high expectations.” The observations also indicate Hydra’s surface is probably coated with water ice.
label: ignore

title: item_p0040
complex: Future images will reveal more clues about the formation of this, and the other moon, billions of years ago.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: When the Taliban sent a gunman to shoot Malala Yousafzai in October 2012 as she rode home on a bus after school, they made clear their intention: to silence the teenager and kill off her campaign for girls’ education.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: Nine months and countless surgical operations later, she stood up at the United Nations on her 16th birthday on Friday to deliver a defiant riposte.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: “They thought that the bullet would silence us.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: But they failed,” she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: As 16th birthdays go, it was among the more unusual.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: Instead of blowing out candles on a cake, Malala sat in one of the main council chambers at the United Nations in the central seat usually reserved for world leaders.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: She listened quietly as Ban Ki-moon, the UN secretary-general, described her as “our hero, our champion”; and as the former British prime minister and now UN education envoy, Gordon Brown, uttered what he called “the words the Taliban never wanted her to hear: happy 16th birthday, Malala”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: The event, dubbed Malala Day, was the culmination of an extraordinary four years for the girl from Mingora, in the troubled Swat valley of Pakistan.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: She was thrust into the public glare after she wrote a blog for the BBC Urdu service describing her experiences struggling to get an education under the rising power of Taliban militants.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: By 11, she was showing exceptional determination, calling personally on the US special representative to Pakistan, Richard Holbrooke, to use his influence to combat the Taliban’s drive against education for girls.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043

label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: But such dizzying global attention came at a price.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: Death threats followed her growing recognition, and, on 9 October 2012, following a meeting of Pakistani Taliban leaders, the gunman was dispatched to remove what they called the “symbol of infidels and obscenity”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: Multiple operations in Pakistan and the UK followed the attack on the bus, including the fitting of a titanium plate on her left forehead and a cochlear implant to restore her hearing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0043
complex: She now lives with her family in Birmingham and does what the Taliban tried to stop her doing: goes to school every day.

title: item_p0043
complex: “Neither am I here to speak in terms of personal revenge against the Taliban or any other terrorist group.” Malala responded to the violence of the Taliban with her own countervailing force: words against bullets.

title: item_p0043
