title: item_p0044
complex: More than a third of all women worldwide – 35.6% – will experience physical or sexual violence in their lifetime, usually from a male partner, according to the first comprehensive study of its kind from the World Health Organization (WHO).
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: The report reveals the shocking extent of attacks on women from the men with whom they share their lives, with 30% of women being attacked by partners.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: It also finds that a large proportion of murders of women – 38% – are carried out by intimate partners.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: “These findings send a powerful message that violence against women is a global health problem of epidemic proportions,” said Dr Margaret Chan, director general of the WHO.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: “We also see that the world’s health systems can and must do more for women who experience violence.” The highest levels of violence against women are in Africa, where nearly half of all women – 45.6% – will suffer physical or sexual violence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: In low- and middle-income Europe, the proportion is 27.2%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: Yet, wealthier nations are not necessarily always safer for women – a third of women in high-income countries (32.7%) will experience violence at some stage in their lives.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: Of the women who suffer violence, 42% sustain injuries, which can bring them to the attention of healthcare staff.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: That, says the report, is often the first opportunity for violence in the home to be detected and for the woman to be offered help.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: Violence has a profound effect on women’s health.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: Some arrive at hospital with broken bones, while others suffer pregnancy-related complications and mental illness.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: The two reports from the WHO – one on the prevalence of violence, the other offering guidelines to healthcare staff on helping women – are the work of Dr Claudia Garcia-Moreno, lead specialist in gender, reproductive rights, sexual health and adolescence at WHO, and Professor Charlotte Watts, an epidemiologist who specializes in gender, violence and health, from the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: “For the first time, we have compared data from all over the world on the magnitude of partner violence and sexual violence by non-partners and the impact of these sorts of violence on health,” said Garcia-Moreno.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: These included HIV and other sexually transmitted infections, depression, women turning to alcohol, unwanted pregnancies and low-birthweight babies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: There were variations in the rates of violence against women in different regions of the world but, said Garcia-Moreno, “in whatever region we looked at, it is unacceptably high”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: Even in high-income countries, 23.2% of women will suffer physical and/or sexual violence from a partner in their lives, their data from 81 countries shows.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: The global figure for women attacked by partners was 30%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: More sexual assaults and rapes by acquaintances or strangers are reported in high-income countries than elsewhere – the report says that 12.6% of women in wealthy countries will be sexually attacked by a non-partner in their lives, which is higher than the African rate of 11.9%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: But, the data on such crimes is not well collected in all regions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: The authors say that their previous research shows that better-educated women are less likely to suffer violence, as are those who have jobs, although not in all regions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: There is a need to tackle social norms, said Watts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: “What is society’s attitude concerning the acceptability of certain forms of violence against women?” she asked.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: “In some societies, it is not OK – but not all.” “I think the numbers are a wake-up call for all of us to pay more attention to this issue,” said Garcia-Moreno.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: Over the past decade, there has been increasing recognition of the problem, she said, but “one has to recognize that it is a complex problem.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: We don’t have a vaccine or a pill”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: The new WHO clinical and policy guidelines recommend training for healthcare staff in recognizing the signs of domestic violence and sexual assault, but they rule out general screening – there is not a case for asking every woman who arrives in a clinic whether she has been subjected to violence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: “But, if you see a woman coming back several times with undisclosed injuries, you should be asking about domestic violence,” said Garcia-Moreno.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: “When I was training in medical school, it wasn’t something you learned or knew about.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: Years later, I was sometimes in a situation where I could tell there was something else going on in the woman I was interviewing, but didn’t have any sense that domestic violence was the issue.
label: ignore

title: item_p0044
complex: Now, I think I would handle the interview very differently.”

title: item_p0045
complex: Europe is to become the first place in the world to force ‘real-world’ emissions tests on car makers, opening up a new front in the fight to tackle air pollution.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: New regulations will introduce the tests to reveal what cars’ emissions are like when driving on roads and in traffic rather than in ideal, laboratory-like conditions, as is currently the case.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: Green lit by European Commission Vice President, Frans Timmermans, the tests are designed to enforce a limit of 80mg of nitrogen oxide per kilometre, a level met by only one car out of 16, according to researchers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: Other countries, such as China and Korea, which are also considering real-world emissions tests, will be watching what happens next closely.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: Pollutants from diesel engines such as nitrogen oxide, carbon monoxide and particulates are thought to be responsible for at least one quarter of the 29,000 annual pollution-related deaths in the UK alone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: That figure is likely to rise, when the committee on the medical effects of air pollutants publishes what it calls “strengthening evidence” of damage to public health from nitrogen oxide emissions later in 2015.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: But the current ‘New European Drive Cycle’ laboratory test for measuring these emissions is a quarter of a century old and has been outpaced by technological developments in the car industry.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: Studies have shown that the results of lab techniques to measure car emissions can easily be fixed by using techniques such as taping up doors and windows to minimize air resistance, driving on unrealistically smooth roads and testing at improbably high temperatures.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: Campaigners say that car makers also use tricks such as programming vehicles to go into a low emissions mode when their front wheels are spinning and their back wheels are stationary, as happens in such lab experiments.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: “The Commission is finalizing a proposal to introduce a new emissions testing procedure that will allow proper assessment of the vehicles in real driving,” said Lucia Caudet, a Commission spokesperson.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: The proposal still needs approval from other commissioners and a technical committee but “we don’t expect any major internal hurdles,” a European Union (EU) source added.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: “One key reason why air pollution kills 400,000 citizens annually is that car makers cheat the tests for diesel cars, causing many times more pollution on the road,” said Greg Archer, the clean vehicles manager for Transport and Environment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: “The development of a new, real-world driving emission test is an important step forward in tackling urban air pollution.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: EU states should now support the Commission’s proposals and ignore the whingeing from car makers that the rules are too tough.” According to research by the International Council on Clean Transportation in 2014, actual nitrogen oxide emissions from cars are seven times higher than the 80mg/km standard, with some models running at 22 times above the recommended limit.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: Only one car out of 16 met the 80g target.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: Around one third of all nitrogen oxide pollution comes from road transport – mostly diesel – and, in urban areas, concentrations can rise as high as 64%, European Environment Agency figures indicate.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: Campaigners say that the car industry has tried to delay reforms to car test cycles but industry groups deny this, arguing that a five-year lead-time is necessary for technical and economic reasons.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: “Real Driving Emissions (RDE) is a totally new regulation that will force significant emission control hardware changes that may be demanded in the middle of a vehicle’s production lifetime,” said Cara McLaughlin, a spokesperson for the European Automobile Manufacturers Association (ACEA).
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: “However, ACEA fully accepts that RDE will apply to new types of cars from September 2017.” In an unusual move, the car association sent Timmermans a draft regulation of their own for him to consider, after EU representatives finally agreed a regulation to implement the nitrogen oxide limits with beefed-up road trials and strict monitoring of exhaust fumes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: ACEA’s draft regulation would have covered fewer pollutants and further delayed the regulation’s phased introduction until 2020.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: Test distances would have been shortened from 1,300m to below 700m, minimum temperatures would have been raised from -7C to -3C and more rural roads would have been used.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: “The lobbyists were all over this,” Archer said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: “This was a real attempt at subversion of the legislative process.” After Timmerman’s apparent rejection of the ACEA proposal, the regulation will now pass to commissioners for a rubber stamp, and member states for final amendments, before an expected introduction in September.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: By 2017, the first real-world car emissions tests are expected to begin in earnest.
label: ignore

title: item_p0045
complex: With a similar battle already looming over the testing of CO2 emissions for cars at the end of 2015 – and with comparable debates in other countries – the introduction of the EU’s new emissions tests will be watched closely.

title: item_p0046
complex: If the amount of food wasted around the world were reduced by just 25%, there would be enough food to feed all the people who are malnourished, according to the UN.

title: item_p0046
complex: Each year, 1.3bn tonnes of food, about a third of all that is produced, is wasted, including about 45% of all fruit and vegetables, 35% of sh and seafood, 30% of cereals, 20% of dairy products and 20% of meat.

title: item_p0046
complex: Meanwhile, 795 million people suffer from severe hunger and malnutrition.

title: item_p0046
complex: Well-publicized attempts to combat the loss of food Ð such as recent laws in France that require supermarkets to distribute unsold food to charities Ð have highlighted the issue of food waste, identi ed by the UN as one of the great challenges to achieving food security.

title: item_p0046
complex: Estimates suggest that, by 2050, food production will need to have increased by 60% on 2005 levels to feed a growing global population.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: Reducing food wastage would ease the burden on resources as the world attempts to meet future demand.

title: item_p0046
complex: The problem is global but manifests itself in very different ways.

title: item_p0046
complex: In developing countries, there are high levels of what is known as Òfood lossÓ, which is unintentional wastage, often due to poor equipment, transportation and infrastructure.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: In wealthy countries, there are low levels of unintentional losses but high levels of Òfood wasteÓ, which involves food being thrown away by consumers because they have purchased too much or by retailers who reject food because of strict aesthetic standards.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: In developed countries, consumers and retailers throw away between 30% and 40% of all food purchased, whereas in poorer countries only 5% to 16% of food is thrown away.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: According to a 2011 report, in Europe and North America, each person wasted 95 to 115kg of otherwise edible food annually, whereas, in sub-Saharan Africa and south and south-east Asia, the equivalent waste was just 6 to 11kg.

title: item_p0046
complex: ÒIn the developing world, food waste is virtually non-existent,Ó says Robert van Otterdijk, coordinator of the UN Food and Agriculture OrganizationÕs Save Food programme.

title: item_p0046
complex: ÒFood waste is happening in countries where people can afford to throw away food.

title: item_p0046
complex: One statistic is that the amount of food wasted by consumers in industrialized countries [222m tonnes a year] is almost the same as the total net food production of sub-Saharan Africa [230m tonnes].

title: item_p0046
complex: But food losses, on the other hand, are really rampant in developing countries because of the underdeveloped conditions they have, from management of production to transportation and distribution.Ó The environmental impact of food loss and waste is high.

title: item_p0046
complex: The carbon footprint of food produced and not eaten is estimated at 3.3 gigatonnes of CO2, meaning that, if food waste were a country, it would rank as the third highest national emitter of greenhouse gases after the US and China.

title: item_p0046
complex: About 1.4bn hectares, or close to 30% of available agricultural land, is used to grow or farm food that is subsequently wasted.

title: item_p0046
complex: And more surface and groundwater, or Òblue waterÓ, is used to produce wasted food around the globe than is used for agriculture by any single country, including India and China.

title: item_p0046

title: item_p0046

title: item_p0046
complex: In the UK, 15m tonnes of food is lost or wasted each year.

title: item_p0046
complex: British consumers throw away 4.2m tonnes of edible food each year.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: This means that 11.7% of all food purchased is avoidably wasted, at an estimated cost to each family of £700 a year or almost £60 a month.

title: item_p0046
complex: The foods most commonly found in British bins are bread, vegetables, fruit and milk.

title: item_p0046
complex: According to a nationwide analysis of food waste in UK households, the most wasted food in the UK by weight is bread, with consumers throwing away 414,000 tonnes (22.4%) of all bread purchased.

title: item_p0046
complex: By percentage, the most wasted food is lettuce and leafy salads, of which consumers throw away 38% (64,000 tonnes) of all they buy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: The most wasted meat, by both percentage and weight, is poultry, of which 13% of all edible parts (not including bones) is wasted and the most wasted fruit by percentage is melon, with the equivalent of more than a quarter of each melon thrown in the bin.

title: item_p0046
complex: The UK has made progress in the past ten years, after a concerted campaign to reduce wastage.

title: item_p0046
complex: It is singled out by van Otterdijk as Òone of the great successesÓ in combating food waste.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: Between 2007 and 2012, the amount of avoidable food waste produced by UK households decreased by 21%, from 5.3m tonnes to 4.2m tonnes, largely due to greater awareness.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: Of the food that is not eaten, less is ending up in land ll.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: In 2000Ð01, only 14% of household waste was recycled or composted but, by 2011-12, the amount of composted food had risen to 43%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0046
complex: Van Otterdijk says there has been a Òvery encouraging, unexpected, continuing interestÓ in the subject of food waste, enabling grass-roots campaigns around the world to gain momentum.

title: item_p0046

title: item_p0048
complex: Coal is likely to rival oil as the world’s biggest source of energy in the next five years, with potentially disastrous consequences for the climate, according to the world’s leading authority on energy economics.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: One of the biggest factors behind the rise in coal use has been the massive increase in the use of shale gas in the US.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: New research from the International Energy Agency (IEA) shows that coal consumption is increasing all over the world – even in countries and regions with carbon-cutting targets – except in the US, where shale gas has displaced coal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: The decline of coal consumption in the US has helped to cut prices for coal globally.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: This has made it more attractive, even in Europe where coal use was supposed to be discouraged by the Emissions Trading Scheme.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: Maria van der Hoeven, Executive Director of the IEA, said: “Coal’s share of the global energy mix continues to grow each year and, if no changes are made to current policies, coal will catch oil within a decade.” Coal is abundant and found in most regions of the world, unlike conventional oil and gas, and can be cheaply extracted.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: As a result, coal was used to meet nearly half of the rise in demand for energy globally in the past decade.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: According to the IEA, demand from China and India will drive world coal use in the coming five years, with India likely to overtake the US as the world’s second biggest consumer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: China is the biggest coal importer, and Indonesia the biggest exporter, having temporarily overtaken Australia.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: According to the IEA’s Medium-Term Coal Market Report the world will burn 1.2bn more tonnes of coal per year by 2017 compared with today – the equivalent of the current coal consumption of Russia and the US combined.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: Global coal consumption is forecast to reach 4.3bn tonnes of oil equivalent by 2017, while oil consumption is forecast to reach 4.4bn tonnes by the same date.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: With the highest carbon emissions of any major fossil fuel, coal is a huge contributor to climate change, particularly when burned in old-fashioned, inefficient power stations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: When these are not equipped with special “scrubbing” equipment to remove chemicals, coal can also produce sulphur emissions – the leading cause of acid rain – and other pollutants such as mercury and soot particles.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: Van der Hoeven said that, without a high carbon price to discourage the growth in coal use and encourage cleaner technologies such as renewable power generation, only competition from lower-priced gas could realistically cut demand for coal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: This has happened in the US, owing to the extraordinary increase in the production of shale gas in that market in the past five years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: She said: “The US experience suggests that a more efficient gas market, marked by flexible pricing and fuelled by indigenous unconventional resources that are produced sustainably, can reduce coal use, carbon dioxide emissions and consumers’ electricity bills.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: Europe, China and other regions should take note.” That would mean producing much more shale gas, as conventional gas resources are running down in their easily accessible locations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: In Europe, the Emissions Trading Scheme was supposed to discourage high-carbon power generation by imposing a price on carbon dioxide emissions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: This was done through issuing generators and energy-intensive companies with a set quota of emissions permits, requiring them to buy extra permits if they needed to emit more than their allowance.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: But an over-allocation, coupled with the effects of the financial crisis and recession, has led to a large surplus of permits on the market, which has in turn led to a plunge in permit prices.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: At current levels – a few euros per tonne of carbon – there is little incentive to seek out lower carbon fuels, and coal is enjoying a renaissance in Europe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: That means one of the world’s only regulatory market mechanisms aimed at cutting greenhouse gas emissions is failing in its key goals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0048
complex: The world faces the likelihood of an increased risk of climate change as a result of this runaway consumption of the highest carbon fossil fuel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: When you see the word Amazon, what’s the first thing that springs to mind – the world’s biggest forest, the longest river or the largest internet retailer – and which do you consider most important?
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: These questions have risen to the fore in an arcane, but hugely important, debate about how to redraw the boundaries of the internet.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: Brazil and Peru have lodged objections to a bid made by the US e-commerce giant for a prime new piece of cyberspace: “.amazon”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: The Seattle-based company has applied for its brand to be a top-level domain name (currently “.com”), but the South American governments argue this would prevent the use of this internet address for environmental protection, the promotion of indigenous rights and other public interest uses.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: Along with dozens of other disputed claims to names, including “.patagonia” and “.shangrila”, the issue cuts to the heart of debates about the purpose and governance of the internet.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: Until now, the differences between commercial, governmental and other types of identity were easily distinguished in every internet address by “.com”, “.gov” and 20 other categories.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: But these categories – or generic top-level domains (gTLDs) as they are technically known – are about to undergo the biggest expansion since the start of the worldwide web.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: The Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers (ICANN) – a US-based non-profit organization that plays a key role in cyberspace governance – has received bids (each reportedly worth almost $200,000) for hundreds of new gTLDs to add to the existing 22.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: Amazon has applied for dozens of new domains, including “.shop”, “.song”, “.book” and “.kindle”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: But its most contentious application is for its own brand.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: Brazil and Peru have called for the “.amazon” application to be withdrawn, saying a private company should not be assigned a name that denotes an important geographical area that spans their territories and is also used for certain regions and cross-border organizations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: “Allowing private companies to register geographical names as gTLDs to reinforce their brand strategy or to profit from the meaning of these names does not serve, in our view, the public interest,” the Brazilian Ministry of Science and Technology said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: Brazil said its views were endorsed last month by other members of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty (Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, Guyana, Suriname and Venezuela).
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: Dozens of other protests have also been registered over proposed top-level domains that take geographical, cultural or contested brand names.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: Argentina has lodged an expression of its unhappiness that the US outdoor clothing retailer, Patagonia, is claiming a domain name that has been known far longer as a region of spectacular beauty that also has its own parliament.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: “Argentina rejects the ‘.patagonia’ request for a new generic top-level domain,” the government notes in an appeal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: “Patagonia is a relevant region for the country’s economy because it has oil, fishing, mining and agriculture resources.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: It is also a region with a vibrant local community and it is a major tourist destination.” Less convincingly, China has disputed the domain “.shangrila”, which is proposed by a hotel group of the same name.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: The authorities in Beijing say the “shangri-la” label belongs to a region in Yunnan province, although it was only renamed as such in 2001 (long after the hotel group was formed) so that the local community could cash in on the fame of the fictional paradise depicted in the novel, Lost Horizon, by British author James Hilton.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: At a conference in April in Beijing, ICANN’s Governmental Advisory Committee – the primary voice of national governments within the institution – recommended a freeze on disputed proposals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: They are expected to be discussed again at a meeting in Durban in July.
label: ignore

title: item_p0049
complex: The first approved domain names should be in use before the end of 2013.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: A mirror that sends heat into the frigid expanse of space has been designed by scientists to replace air-conditioning units that keep buildings cool on Earth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Researchers believe the mirror could slash the amount of energy used to control air temperatures in business premises and shopping centres by doing away with power-hungry cooling systems.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Around 15% of the energy used by buildings in the US goes on air conditioning, but the researchersÕ calculations suggest that, in some cases, the mirror could completely offset the need for extra cooling.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: In a rooftop comparison of the device in Stanford, California, scientists found that, while a surface painted black reached 60C more than ambient temperature in sunlight and bare aluminium reached 40C more, the mirror was up to 5C cooler than the surrounding air temperature.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: ÒIf you cover signi cant parts of the roof with this mirror, you can see how much power it can save.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: You can signi cantly offset the electricity used for air conditioning,Ó said Shanhui Fan, an expert in photonics at Stanford University, who led the development of the mirror.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: ÒIn some situations, the computations say you can completely offset the air conditioning.Ó Buildings warm up in a number of different ways.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Hot-water boilers and cooking facilities release heat into their immediate surroundings.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: In hot countries, warm air comes in through doors and windows.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Then, there is visible light and infrared radiation from the sun, which also heat up buildings.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: The Stanford mirror was designed in such a way that it re ects 97% of the visible light that falls on it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: But, more importantly, it works as a thermal radiator.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: When the mirror is warmed up, it releases heat at a speci c wavelength of infrared light that passes easily through the atmosphere and out into space.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: To make anything cool requires what engineers call a heat sink: somewhere to dump unwanted heat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: The heat sink has to be cooler than the object that needs cooling or it will not do its job.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: For example, a bucket of ice will cool a bottle of wine because it becomes a sink for heat in the liquid.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: The Stanford mirror relies on the ultimate heat sink: the universe itself.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: The mirror is built from several layers of wafer-thin materials.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: The rst layer is re ective silver.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: On top of this are alternating layers of silicon dioxide and hafnium oxide.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: These layers improve the re ectivity but, also, turn the mirror into a thermal radiator.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: When silicon dioxide heats up, it radiates the heat as infrared light at a wavelength of around ten micrometres.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Since there is very little in the atmosphere that absorbs at that wavelength, the heat passes straight out to space.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: The total thickness of the mirror is around two micrometres or two thousandths of a millimetre.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: ÒThe cold darkness of the universe can be used as a renewable thermodynamic resource, even during the hottest hours of the day,Ó the scientists write in Nature.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: In tests, the mirror had a cooling power of 40 watts per square metre at ambient temperature.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Writinginthejournal,Fanputstheinstalledcostof mirrors at between $20 and $70 per square metre and calculates an annual electricity saving of 100MWh on a three-storey building.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Fan said that the mirror could cool buildings Ð or other objects Ð simply by putting it in direct contact with them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Coating the roof of a building with the mirror would prevent heating from sunlight but do little to remove heat from its interior.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: More likely, the mirror would be used to cool water or some other uid that would then be pumped around the building.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Fan said that the mirror could cool buildings Ð or other objects Ð simply by putting it in direct contact with them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Coating the roof of a building with the mirror would prevent heating from sunlight but do little to remove heat from its interior.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: More likely, the mirror would be used to cool water or some other uid that would then be pumped around the building.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: He ruled out the idea of using the mirrors to slow down global warming.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: ÒRoof space accounts for only a small portion of the EarthÕs surface so, at this point, we donÕt think this would be a geoengineering solution.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: Rather, our contribution on the greenhouse-gas-emission issue is simply to reduce electricity consumption,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: ÒIÕm really excited by the potential it has and the applications for cooling,Ó said Marin Solja__i__, a physicist at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: ÒYou could use this on buildings so you have to spend much less on air conditioning or maybe you wouldnÕt need it at all.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: You could put it on top of shopping malls.
label: ignore

title: item_p0050
complex: With a large enough surface, you could get substantial cooling.Ó
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: David Mitchell, a regular contender for the Man Booker literary prize, is used to his novels being picked over by the critics.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: So, it’s something of a relief, says the British author, that his latest work – completed at 1am one Tuesday morning before a car arrived to take him to the airport to catch a flight to Norway – won’t be seen by anyone until 2114.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: Mitchell is the second contributor to the Scottish artist Katie Paterson’s Future Library project, for which 1,000 trees were planted in 2014 in Oslo’s Nordmarka forest.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: Starting with Margaret Atwood, who handed over the manuscript of a text called Scribbler Moon in 2015, each year for the next 100 years, an author will deliver a piece of writing that will only be read in 2114, when the trees are chopped down to make paper on which the 100 texts will be printed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: Each author – their names revealed year by year and chosen by a panel of experts and Paterson, while she is alive – will make the trek to the spot in the forest high above Oslo, where they will surrender their manuscripts in a short ceremony.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: “It’s a little glimmer of hope in a season of highly depressing news cycles, which affirms we are in with a chance of civilization in a hundred years,” said Mitchell.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: “Everything is telling us that we’re doomed but the Future Library is a candidate on the ballot paper for possible futures.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: It brings hope that we are more resilient than we think: that we will be here, that there will be trees, that there will be books and readers, and civilization.” Mitchell said he found writing the book “quite liberating because I won’t be around to take the consequences of this being good or bad ...
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: But, I’m sandwiched between Margaret Atwood and no doubt some other brilliant writer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: So, it better be good.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: What a historic fool of epochal proportions I’d look if they opened it in 2114 and it wasn’t any good.” Usually, says Mitchell, who was shortlisted for the Man Booker for his novels number9dream and Cloud Atlas, he “polishes and polishes” his writing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: “Actually, I over-polish.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: But, this was very different – I wrote up to the wire.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: So, the first two-thirds were polished and the final third I didn’t have time.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: And, it was a liberation.” Future Library creator, Paterson, whose past works have involved her mapping dead stars and compiling a slide archive of the history of darkness through the ages, asked the writers to tackle “the theme of imagination and time, which they can take in so many directions”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: Mitchell revealed only the name of the manuscript, From Me Flows What You Call Time, during a ceremony in the Norwegian woods next to where Paterson’s 1,000 trees are planted.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: The title is taken from a piece of music by Japanese composer Toru Takemitsu but, other than admitting that “it’s somewhat more substantial a thing than I was expecting”, the author would say nothing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: Handing over his text in the forest, sheltered from the intermittent rain by an umbrella and amid the foot-high shoots of 1,000 pine trees, Mitchell read his damp audience of children and adults a short story and William Wordsworth’s A slumber did my spirit seal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: Its ending, “Rolled round in earth’s diurnal course / With rocks, and stones, and trees”, felt appropriate in this small section of forest, carpeted with blueberry bushes, which will be carefully tended to for the next 98 years before it is turned into Future Library’s manuscripts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: “How vain to suppose the scribblings of little old me will be of enduring interest to future generations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: Yet, how low-key and understated, to slave over a manuscript that nobody will ever pat you on the back for and say: ‘Nice one’ or ‘God, I loved the bit where she did that and he did this ...’” Mitchell wrote in a piece for the Future Library.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: His manuscript, now delivered, will be sealed and placed alongside Atwood’s in a wood-lined room in Oslo’s new public library, which will open in 2019.
label: ignore

title: item_p0051
complex: Watched over by a trust of experts until it is finally printed, it is now, says the novelist, “as gone from me as a coin dropped in a river”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: The beginning of the year is probably prime-time for feeling glum about work: it’s ages until the next holiday, and it’s dark in the morning and when you get home.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: And, if you’re stuck in a job you don’t like, it could be enough to have you reaching for your CV.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: But, before you start hunting through the job ads, try to put things in perspective.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: So, what else could you be doing instead?
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: We asked fi ve people doing some unusual jobs how much they are paid, what the worst parts are and why they enjoy their work.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Dog-food taster The job: Tasting dog food to make sure it meets a premium brand’s quality standards What it involves: Opening sample tins of each freshly made batch of dog (or cat) food, smelling it and eating it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “Although dogs’ palates are different from ours, taste is an important quality check to ensure each different ingredient is perfectly balanced in just the right way,” says Philip Wells, the chief taster for Lily’s Kitchen pet food.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “Trying the food is also a good way to pick up on the nuances of the cooking; this works especially well on the dry food.” Typical salary: £20,000 for an entry-level job in the quality department.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: However, Wells says £50,000 or more is “easily achievable” for an experienced technical director who, as well as tasting products, is also likely to be responsible for developing new recipes and advising the business on technical and regulatory matters.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Worst part of the job: The deadlines, for Wells, who admits he quite likes the food.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: The meat used in pet food has to be derived from animals passed as fi t for human consumption, under the Animal Feed Regulations 2010, and he says the fi rm uses “human-grade freshly prepared raw food” in its recipes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: He adds: “There are some pretty gruesome pet foods out there and, although I don’t taste them, the smell is enough to turn the stomach when I do a bit of market research.” Job satisfaction: “No two days are ever the same.” It’s rewarding, Wells says, that a project he has worked on will “help pets to become happier and healthier”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: However, he acknowledges that some of the credit must go to another “key member” of the tasting team: Lily, the border terrier.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Hygiene technician The job: Disinfecting areas that have potentially been exposed to bio-hazardous situations What it involves: Cleaning up crime scenes, road accidents and suicides.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Clearing hoarders’ houses full of rubbish, rats and excrement … among other things.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “The job is about keeping people safe,” says Richard Lewis, a hygiene technician for Rentokil.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “We deal with some very disturbingly dirty sites.” Typical salary: The entry-level salary is usually around £14,500 and a top salary can be up to £22,000.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Worst part of the job: Cleaning up after suicides.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “You get used to the job being disgusting but the emotional side of it is still hard.” You learn not to take your work home with you, he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “You also need to have a sense of humour, as some days can be tough.” Job satisfaction: Lewis fi nds the variety of tasks exciting.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “One day, I’m cleaning up after a dead body; another day, I’m in a prison cell or 100 feet in the air being lowered down into a silo to clean it.” He also takes pride in the transformation he brings about: “It’s satisfying to return a potentially hazardous site back to a safe environment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: And it benefi ts society.” 3.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Biogas engineer The job: Setting up biogas plants in developing countries What it involves: Linking a system of digesters – which can be fi lled with human excrement, animal dung and other waste products – to toilets to produce a biogas that can be used for cooking and lighting.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “You have to know what size and shape the mixing pit needs to be, how to create the optimum temperature for digestion and where to situate the biogas plant,” says Baburam Paudel, chief technical offi cer in Nepal for the charity Renewable World.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “You also have to convince poor communities that poo can be productive – many are repelled by the idea of connecting their toilets to their kitchens.” Typical salary: An entry-level salary is around £10,000, while a typical salary for a chief technical offi cer is £30,000.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Worst part of the job: For Paudel, it’s seeing people struggling to survive on very little income.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: But, he admits, anyone who won’t change a nappy would struggle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “You have to be willing to get your hands dirty during the build process and inspections.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Unsurprisingly, the anaerobic digestion (the process that takes place when bacteria eat the decomposing waste and produce methane) smells like rotten eggs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: It can be disgusting and there is no room for mistakes.” Job satisfaction: “I fi nd it immensely satisfying to know that I am helping people to increase their incomes and allowing girls to attend school by replacing the need to collect fi rewood,” says Paudel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “My work improves the health and hygiene of whole communities.” 4.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Eel ecologist The job: Conserving the critically endangered European eel What it involves: To monitor the size of the endangered eels, ecologists wade into the Thames and other London rivers and marshes full of eels, sometimes up to their armpits, and reach into a net fi lled with up to 20 adult eels to grab one with their bare hands.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “Adult eels can be a metre long, or even larger, and weigh up to 2kg.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: They’re not at all dangerous but they are almost pure muscle and they can be a little bit slimy,” says Stephen Mowat, an eel conservationist and ecologist for the Zoological Society of London.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “We have to weigh and measure them, and they wriggle … a lot.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: It’s diffi cult to look professional while crawling on the ground chasing an eel across the grass.” Worst part of the job: “Eels are really tricky creatures to work with – and getting outsmarted by an eel can be quite embarrassing,” says Mowat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “You also have to be ready to jump from one project to the next.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: I once had to dissect a tub of dead eels to examine parasites living in them, moments before jumping into a suit for a meeting with government offi cials.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: I remembered to wash my hands.” But, for Mowat, the worst part of the job is defi nitely not handling the eels – he believes baby eels (known as elvers) are “as cute as pandas”: “The worst thing about the job is regularly learning how much damage we, the British population, are doing to the environment.” Job satisfaction: “Getting to work outdoors and seeing British wildlife up close is the best part of the job,” says Mowat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “Eels are beautiful creatures and working with eels doesn’t just benefi t the eel: it helps whole river systems, estuaries and coastal habitats.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: That is something worth working on.” 5.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Shopping channel presenter The job: Selling and demonstrating a wide range of products on live TV What it involves: Presenting hours and hours of monotonous content, while simultaneously demonstrating the products and appearing to be enthusiastic and knowledgeable about everything that you’re selling.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “I prepare and research as much technical and practical information as possible on every single product beforehand,” says Shaun Ryan, presenter for Ideal World TV.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “But you also need the ability to relate to every genre of products and to every viewer.” Typical salary: A trainee presenter would start on a minimum of £30,000, while an experienced presenter can expect over £55,000.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: Worst part of the job: “The unsociable hours,” says Ryan.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “An experienced presenter like myself generally gets to work prime-time hours, which means all weekends, bank holidays and very late evenings, plus the occasional 5am shift.” His worst task ever, he says, was singlehandedly having to sell some female slimming pants: “It was a very tricky hour and not my fi nest.” Job satisfaction: “I love the rush of live presenting and having to think on my feet every second,” says Ryan.
label: ignore

title: item_p0052
complex: “I also get an adrenaline rush from knowing that, at times, I have thousands of viewers ordering the product that I have just been presenting.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: Two mothers in South Africa have discovered they are raising each otherÕs daughters after they were mistakenly switched at birth in a hospital in 2010.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: But, while one of the women wants to correct the error and reclaim her biological child, the other is refusing to give back the girl she has raised as her own, posing a huge legal dilemma.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: Henk Strydom, a lawyer for one of the mothers, who cannot be identified because of a court order, described the inadvertent swap as a travesty and tragedy that is unlikely to have a happy ending.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: Both mothers gave birth at the Tambo Memorial Hospital in Boksburg, east of Johannesburg, on the same day in 2010.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: ÒNobody suspected anything,Ó Strydom said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: But, in 2013, one of the mothers, who is 33 and unemployed, sued her ex-partner for maintenance for her daughter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: Strydom continued: ÒThe man denied he was the father.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: A DNA test was done and it was found it was not his baby and not her baby.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: She was devastated.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: She didnÕt know what to do.Ó Eventually, she met the other mother and, since December 2013, they have been attending joint counselling sessions, arranged by the hospital.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: This has included meeting their biological daughters.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: Strydom said of his client: ÒShe said there are resemblances to herself.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: She conveyed to me that it was traumatic.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: You can see itÕs not easy for her.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: She has to care for a child that is not hers on her own while her child is with someone else.Ó The woman reportedly became unhappy with the process and approached the childrenÕs court in a bid to gain custody of her biological child, but the other mother refused.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: Strydom agreed to represent the woman, who has one elder child, pro bono.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: ÒItÕs a tragedy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: She wants the baby back but it seems the other mother is reluctant.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: ItÕs four years later: you can understand she doesnÕt want to give up her baby.Ó The High Court in Pretoria has appointed the University of PretoriaÕs Centre for Child Law to investigate what will now be in the best interests of the children, which is the guiding principle under South African law.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: It must report back within 90 days.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: Strydom added: ÒYour guess is as good as mine what the court may decide.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: ItÕs a travesty.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: How do you rectify it after four years?
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: The longer you wait, the more traumatic it will be.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: But, whatever happens, someone wonÕt be happy.Ó He said at this point, he and his client do not want to sue the hospital or government health department, which is currently helping with the case and providing counselling.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: The Centre for Child Law will now interview the mothers and fathers, as well as any other person with a Òsignificant relationshipÓ with either of the girls.
label: ignore

title: item_p0053
complex: The children and mothers will undergo Òfull and thoroughÓ clinical assessments and may be seen by a psychologist.

title: item_p0053

title: item_p0055
complex: JMW Turner, one of Britain’s greatest painters, is to be the face of the new £20 note, following a nationwide vote.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: It will be the first time an artist has appeared on a British banknote, after the governor of the Bank of England, Mark Carney, asked the public to nominate a deceased cultural figure they felt deserved the high honour.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Turner, renowned for his dramatic seascapes, beat off competition from 590 painters, sculptors, fashion designers, photographers, film-makers and actors put forward by 30,000 members of the public.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: The list included Alfred Hitchcock, Alexander McQueen, Derek Jarman, Laura Ashley, William Morris and Vanessa Bell, which was then narrowed down by a panel of artists, critics and historians to a final choice of five.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: The final five – Barbara Hepworth, Charlie Chaplin, Josiah Wedgwood, William Hogarth and Turner – were selected on the basis of their “unquestioned” contribution to both the visual arts and British society as a whole, as well as their enduring influence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Fittingly, the announcement of the new banknote was made at the Turner Contemporary gallery in Margate, which stands on the former site of Mrs Booth’s lodging house, where Turner would always stay when he visited.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: The announcement was made jointly by Carney and the artist Tracey Emin, who grew up in the town.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Carney said it had been “so important to get this right and have a proper process that involved the public,” adding that, far from banknotes being purely a practical necessity, “if done properly, they can be a piece of art in everyone’s pocket”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: “Money is memory for a country and its people,” said the Bank of England governor.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: “Banknotes of the Bank of England are a celebration of the UK’s heritage, a salute to its culture, a testament to its great achievements, including those of its most notable citizens.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: In short, money has not just economic value, it has cultural value as well.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: “The fact that we will have Turner on the £20 note shows now that the British people are a nation of people who appreciate creativity and appreciate the arts,” said Emin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: The note will feature Turner’s 1799 self-portrait, which currently hangs in Tate Britain, as well as one of his most recognizable works, The Fighting Temeraire, a tribute to the ship that played a distinguished role in Nelson’s victory at the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Emblazoned on the note will also be a quote from the artist – “light is therefore colour” – as well as his signature, taken from his will, in which he bequeathed many of his works to the nation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: The new £20 note, which replaces the one featuring social philosopher and economic theorist Adam Smith, will enter circulation by 2020.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: This is the first time the public have been given a say over whose face appears on a British banknote.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Carney overhauled the process in 2013, following a high-profile campaign to ensure that women, apart from the Queen, remained on banknotes, after the bank decided to phase out the Elizabeth Fry £5 note.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Historical figures were first introduced onto banknotes in 1970.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Turner joins Winston Churchill and Jane Austen as the significant figures who will feature on the new polymer notes – a plastic-type material that the new notes will all be made from – Churchill on the £5 and Austen on the £10 note.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Following in the footsteps of Florence Nightingale and Elizabeth Fry, the Pride and Prejudice author is only the third woman to be the face of British currency.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Turner was born in 1775 in London, the son of a barber, and he entered the Royal Academy Schools at the age of 14.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: It was 1786, when he was sent to Margate, that his love affair with painting and drawing the north-east Kent coast began.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: He would return to that coast throughout his life and it was where he painted some of his most dramatic oils and watercolours, describing its skies as “the loveliest in all Europe”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Turner was a particularly prolific artist, producing more than 550 oil paintings and 2,000 watercolours over his lifetime.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: His life was also the subject of a film, in 2014, by Mike Leigh, with Timothy Spall taking on the role of the complex artist.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Victoria Pomery, the director of Turner Contemporary, said: “The decision to celebrate JMW Turner, one of the greatest technical pioneers in the history of British art, is extraordinary, not least because it has proven by a public vote that Turner is the nation’s favourite artist.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: Brilliantly, it is an official acknowledgment of the immense historical value of the visual arts to the UK.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: “But it is also a stark reminder that we must place increasing value on our future as a nation of creative minds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0055
complex: We must invest to support the next great artists and innovators.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0056
complex: In typical bad-boyfriend style, Dan Sullivan was late to breakfast with the Guardian because he got pulled over on his motorcycle.

complex: Sullivan works too much, he says.

title: item_p0056
complex: He misses dinner dates.

title: item_p0056
complex: And so, like seemingly everyone in Silicon Valley, the 27-year-old venture capitalist has come up with a start-up to fix it: BetterBoyfriend.me, a service that sends girlfriends and wives a present every month for about $70.

title: item_p0056
complex: Sullivan, who works in finance, has been quietly testing the program and has signed up about 350 boyfriends generating about $17,000 revenue every month.

title: item_p0056
complex: Most members, he says, are from his collection of Harvard and investor friends: venture capitalists, founders and employees of companies like Apple, Google and Facebook, whose girlfriends have been getting presents from Sullivan for the last year.

complex: Each month, Sullivan’s members choose from a list of seven possible gifts (chocolate, tea sets, manicures).
label: ignore

title: item_p0056
complex: The package is sent to the boyfriend unbranded and unlabelled.
label: ignore

title: item_p0056
complex: He tests his ideas by buying Facebook ads just to see what kind of traction they get.
label: ignore

title: item_p0056
complex: BetterBoyfriend.me took off.
label: ignore

title: item_p0056
complex: “BetterBoyfriend hit something,” Sullivan said.

complex: “It’s a pain point everyone has.” BetterBoyfriend.me was not Sullivan’s first idea.

complex: Founder Kyle Tabor launched it to give users a faux boyfriend to deter unwanted advances but has found people have grown attached to their algorithmic partners: “Many more users are seeking companionship through conversation rather than external ‘proof’ of a relationship to get people off their back.” For Sullivan and Tabor, the surprise was the real relationships that have formed with their customers.

title: item_p0056
complex: Sullivan says he has begun to see himself as a sort of relationship consultant for the boyfriends.

title: item_p0056

title: item_p0056
complex: Early on, the packages included receipts that had his name on them, Dan Sullivan.

title: item_p0056
complex: “One of the boyfriends wrote to me and said, ‘Listen, dude, she’s not mad but Cynthia found out’."

title: item_p0056

label: ignore

title: item_p0056

label: ignore

complex: It made sense, though.

title: item_p0057
complex: Facebook has lost millions of users per month in its biggest markets, independent data suggests, as alternative social networks attract the attention of those looking for fresh online playgrounds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: As Facebook prepares to update investors on its performance in the first three months of the year, with analysts forecasting revenues up 36% on last year, studies suggest that its expansion in the US, UK and other major European countries has peaked.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: In the last month, the world’s largest social network has lost 6m US visitors, a 4% fall, according to analysis firm Socialbakers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: In the UK, 1.4m fewer users checked in in March, a fall of 4.5%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: The declines are sustained.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: In the last six months, Facebook has lost nearly 9m monthly visitors in the US and 2m in the UK.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: Users are also switching off in Canada, Spain, France, Germany and Japan, where Facebook has some of its biggest followings.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: A spokeswoman for Facebook declined to comment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: “The problem is that, in the US and UK, most people who want to sign up for Facebook have already done it,” said new media specialist Ian Maude at Enders Analysis.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: “There is a boredom factor where people like to try something new.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: Is Facebook going to go the way of MySpace?
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: The risk is relatively small, but that is not to say it isn’t there.” Alternative social networks such as Instagram, the photo-sharing site that won 30m users in 18 months before Facebook acquired the business, have seen surges in popularity with younger age groups.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: Path, the mobile phone-based social network founded by former Facebook employee Dave Morin, which restricts its users to 150 friends, is gaining 1m users a week.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: It has recently topped 9m users, with 500,000 Venezuelans downloading the app in a single weekend.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: Facebook is still growing fast in South America.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: Monthly visitors in Brazil were up 6% in the last month to 70m, according to Socialbakers, whose information is used by Facebook advertisers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: India has seen a 4% rise to 64m – still a fraction of the country’s population, leaving room for further growth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: But in developed markets, other Facebook trackers are reporting declines.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: Analysts at Jefferies bank have developed an algorithm that interfaces directly with Facebook software and it “suggests user levels in [the first quarter] may have declined from peak”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: Jefferies saw global numbers peak at 1.05bn a month in January, before falling by 20m in February.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: Numbers rose again in April.

title: item_p0057
complex: The network has now lost nearly 2m visitors in the UK since December, according to research firm Nielsen, with its 27m total flat on a year before.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: The number of minutes Americans spend on Facebook appears to be falling, too.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: The total was 121 billion minutes in December 2012, but that fell to 115 billion minutes in February, according to comScore.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: As Facebook itself has warned, the time spent on its pages from those sitting in front of personal computers is declining rapidly because we are switching our screen time to smartphones and tablets.
label: ignore

title: item_p0057
complex: While smartphone minutes have doubled in a year, to 69 a month, that growth is not guaranteed to compensate for dwindling desktop usage.

complex: Facebook is the most authoritative source on its own user numbers, and the firm will update investors on its performance for the quarter.

complex: Wall Street expects revenues of about $1.44bn, up from $1.06bn in 2012.

title: item_p0057
complex: Shareholders will be particularly keen to learn how fast Facebook’s mobile user base is growing, and whether advertising revenues are increasing at the same rate.

title: item_p0057
complex: The company warned in recent stockmarket filings that it might be losing “younger users” to “other products and services similar to, or as a substitute for, Facebook”.

title: item_p0057
complex: Wary of competition from services that were invented for the mobile phone rather than the PC, founder Mark Zuckerberg has recently driven through a series of new initiatives designed to appeal to smartphone users.

complex: The most significant is Facebook Home, software that can be downloaded onto certain Android phones to feed news and photos from friends – and advertising – directly to the owner’s locked home screen.

title: item_p0058
complex: Back in 2010, the old city in Srinagar was the sort of place police would only venture into wearing body armour.

complex: A stronghold for violent separatists agitating for an independent Kashmir, it was at the centre of uprisings that left more than 100 people dead, buried along with dreams of peace in the mountainous north-Indian region.

complex: How quickly things change.

title: item_p0058
complex: Now carefree tourists line up in the same streets for barbecued mutton tikka and steaming plates of rogan josh.

title: item_p0058

complex: Down by Dal Lake, houseboats have been booked out months in advance.

complex: In the stunning gardens lining the lake’s green slopes, visitors can have their picture taken against one of Asia’s prettiest backdrops.

title: item_p0058
complex: Omar Abdullah, the UK-born Chief Minister of Jammu and Kashmir, has lobbied the British High Commission in Delhi to relax the guidelines, but to no avail.
label: ignore

title: item_p0058
complex: “It’s a source of frustration,” admitted Abdullah, who has been in charge of India’s most sensitive state since the start of 2009.
label: ignore

title: item_p0058
complex: “Today, unfortunately, as a result of that travel advisory, people’s insurance is null and void when they visit here.” The last publicized case of foreign tourists being murdered in J&K was in 1995, when six westerners including two Britons were kidnapped by Al-Faran, a Kashmiri militant Islamist group.

title: item_p0058
complex: Only one escaped with his life.

title: item_p0058
complex: “You’ve had British citizens killed more recently in countries that you still haven’t stopped people from visiting.

label: ignore

title: item_p0058
complex: Did you stop people from visiting New York?

title: item_p0058
complex: “We’ve lost Indians in London.

complex: There is no reason to single out Jammu and Kashmir, or even Srinagar, as an unsafe destination.” Germany relaxed its guidelines for those thinking of travelling to the region in 2011.

title: item_p0058
complex: “Foreigners are generally not direct targets of clashes,” counselled Germany’s amended advice.

title: item_p0058
complex: At the time, it was viewed by many outsiders as a bold move, coming less than a year after the 2010 disturbances finally died down.
label: ignore

complex: A nationwide holiday on 15 August marked 65 years of Indian independence – in the past, a day fraught with peril in a state where many do not feel part of the world’s biggest democracy.

title: item_p0058
complex: But this time at the independence celebrations there was no trouble.

complex: Abdullah insists tourists are safe in the state, “as long as you take the sort of precautions that one normally would”.
