label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: Stratford remained hugely important throughout ShakespeareÕs life, argues Paul Edmondson, the head of learning and research at the Shakespeare Birthplace Trust.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: ÒPeople have seen Shakespeare as someone who turns his back on Stratford and his family, goes to London to earn his fortune and only comes back to die,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: ÒBut Stratford is where he bought land and property, where he kept his library, where he lived and read and thought.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: We are going to spend the year re-emphasizing the importance of Shakespeare, the man of Stratford.Ó The seveneenth-century diarist, antiquarian and gossip John Aubrey, born 11 years after Shakespeare died, was at pains to point out there was nothing so very special about the bard.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: Aubrey, university educated, unlike Shakespeare, said that he acted Òexceedingly wellÓ and that Òhis Playes took wellÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The world has not agreed with Aubrey.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The anniversary of the death of the man from Stratford, the most famous and the most performed playwright in the world, will be marked across Britain and the globe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: Macbeth will open in Singapore, Romeo and Juliet in Brussels.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: ShakespeareÕs Globe is completing the rst world tour in the history of theatre, in which it has taken Hamlet to almost every country Ð North Korea is still holding out.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: In London, they are also creating a 37-screen pop-up cinema, one screen to showcase each of ShakespeareÕs plays, along the South Bank.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The National Theatre, the Royal Shakespeare Company and virtually every other theatre production company in the country will be marking the anniversary.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: Interpretations will range from the resolutely traditional to the Brighton-based SpymonkeyÕs Complete Deaths, a romp through the 74 deaths Ð 75 including a y squashed in Titus Andronicus Ð by stabbing, poisoning, smothering and smashing across the plays.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: There will also be hundreds of lectures, recitals, international academic conferences, lms, concerts, operas and major exhibitions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: For a man famous in his own lifetime, there is little documentary evidence for ShakespeareÕs life and times.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The plays would scarcely have survived if his friends and fellow actors had not gathered together every scrap of every play they could nd Ð drafts, prompt scripts, scribbled actorsÕ parts and 17 plays not known in any other version Ð into the precious First Folio published in 1623, seven years after ShakespeareÕs death.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The actor Mark Rylance has called it his favourite book in the world and most of the surviving First Folios will be on display Ð including those belonging to the British and Bodleian libraries, and a tattered copy recently discovered in France.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: Some of the most precious surviving documents will be gathered together in an exhibition at Somerset House in London, jointly organized by the National Archives and KingÕs College London, including four of his six known signatures, which are all slightly different.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The exhibition, By Me, William Shakespeare, will include his will, the court papers relating to the audacious move when Shakespeare and his fellow actors dismantled a theatre on the north side of the Thames and rebuilt it as the Globe on the South Bank, and accounts showing payments from the royal treasury for Boxing Day performances for James I and Queen Anne.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The outgoing Globe director, Dominic Dromgoole, recently jokily claimed Shakespeare as a true Londoner Ð albeit conceding Òsome spurious claimÓ by Stratford-upon-Avon.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: Stratford, however, will be insisting that the town made and educated Shakespeare.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: His old school room is being restored with a £1.4m Heritage Lottery grant and will open as a permanent visitor attraction.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: Shakespeare bought the splendid New Place, the second best house in the town, where he died, according to literary legend, on St GeorgeÕs Day, 23 April, the same day as his birth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: ÒYou donÕt buy a house like New Place and not live there,Ó Paul Edmondson said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: ÒThe general public and many academics have consistently underestimated the importance of Stratford to Shakespeare.Ó Edmondson believes that, after Shakespeare bought the house in 1597, all his thinking time was spent there and that the late plays, including The Tempest, were at least planned in his library and probably written there.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The Shakespeare Birthplace Trust describes New Place as Òthe jewel in the crown of the 400th anniversary celebrationsÓ but, in truth, it is more of a gaping hole where the gem should be.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: ShakespeareÕs house was demolished 300 years ago and the house that replaced it, probably incorporating some of the original fabric, was attened in 1759 by an irascible clergyman, Francis Gastrell, in a row over taxes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: He had already cut down ShakespeareÕs mulberry tree, under which the writer is said to have sat and worked, because he was irritated by all the tourists peering into his garden.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The gap in the Stratford streetscape has never been lled but a ve-year archaeology project has peeled back the years and the news that ShakespeareÕs kitchen had been found in the partly surviving cellars went round the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: The whole site is being redisplayed for the anniversary, with the foundations marked and the garden restored.
label: ignore

title: item_p0059
complex: ÒWithout Stratford,Ó Edmondson said, Òthere would have been no Shakespeare.Ó
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Back in 2005, when BlackBerry brought instant messaging to the mobile phone, the company was just entering its boom times.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: While the iPhone was still a gleam in Steve Jobs’s eye, BlackBerry’s innovations ensured its smartphone was one of Canada’s biggest exports.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Six years later, in the summer of 2011, as violence engulfed London and spread to Birmingham, Nottingham, Liverpool and Manchester, so effective was BlackBerry Messenger (BBM) at mobilizing the rioters that politicians called for the service to be temporarily shut down.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: But two years later, it is the users themselves who are pulling the plug.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: As demand for BlackBerry handsets fades, the once noisy BBM grapevine is falling silent.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Dozens of alternatives have sprung up to take its place, from Facebook’s and Apple’s own-brand instant messaging applications to independent start-ups such as WhatsApp and Kik (which is also Canadian).
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Free to download and use, they use the internet to swap text messages, pictures, voice clips, ‘stickers’, and even videos in WhatsApp’s case, between most types of phones.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: In an attempt to retain its following, BBM has been released on Android and Apple phones.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Despite the competition, the response has been overwhelming, with an announcement that there have been more than 20 million downloads.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: But, despite the initial interest, many believe BBM’s wider release will do little to save the service.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “The move to bring BlackBerry to the iPhone is four or five years too late,” says James Gooderson, an 18-year-old student who blogs on technology.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “WhatsApp has removed the reason why young people would use a BlackBerry.” BBM claims 80 million monthly users after its upgrade, but WhatsApp has 300 million.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Other services expose BBM’s limitations: unlike Skype and Viber, it does not yet offer video or voice calls; unlike Path, it does not do location sharing; there is no video sharing, as on iMessage; and the stickers (a more sophisticated version of the smiley face), adored by kids the world over, are also unforgivably absent.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Even the contacts and calendar sharing that BBM made possible on BlackBerry handsets have not migrated to the Apple and Android versions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Messaging is moving from verbal to visual.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Photos uploaded to Instagram trigger a wave of comments and Snapchat’s pictures, which self-delete after ten seconds, have opened a world of other possibilities.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Like BBM, all of these services are free for any phone with an internet connection.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Yet as recently as 2011, BBM was so powerful it was credited with starting a revolution in Egypt; and, at the time of the London riots, it was a more urgent source of news than the television screen.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “We could see on our BlackBerry messages where the rioters were going next; TV news would catch up four hours later,” said Jean- Pierre Moore, 28.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: He manages a youth club in Stockwell, south London, an area with some of the highest levels of crime and economic deprivation in Britain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Moore mainly communicates on an iPad now.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: He dismisses the notion that the BBM curfew urged by some MPs would have stopped the looting.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “The social networking wasn’t the reason,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “I know a lot of people who were out rioting.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: People had been angry for a long time.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Mention the words ‘stop and search’ around here and you immediately have a room full of angry young men.” Nearly 80% of young smartphone owners regularly use a social networking application, says the research firm Enders Analysis, but two-thirds use more than one.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Among 16- to 24-year-olds, 60% use Facebook every day but 46% use alternatives.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “It’s a much more complex, multifaceted environment,” says Benedict Evans, a digital media specialist at Enders.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “The smartphone itself has become the platform.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: All of these apps plug into your phone book and your photo library.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Apps rise and fall like fireworks.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Some, like Instagram, last; others disappear into thin air.” Thirteen-year-old Bennett has three devices, all hand-me-downs from family members.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: He keeps his BlackBerry for messaging, uses an iPhone over wi-fi to play games and makes phone calls on an HTC-branded Android phone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: His friends are still on BBM – the four phone thefts at his school so far this term were all BlackBerrys.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: At the touch of a few buttons, a single BlackBerry message can be sent to the phone owner’s entire contacts book – several hundred people in some cases; on WhatsApp, the limit for a broadcast message is 50.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: But, for Bennett, Instagram is now a major social network.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “Instagram is Facebook without parents,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “Facebook has been taken over by the older generation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Once I saw my mum on Facebook, I deleted my account.” For families that may struggle to pay their heating bills this winter, the low price tag attached to buying and communicating on a BlackBerry retains its appeal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Unlimited BBM messages are available to anyone with a second-hand device and a £7-a-month deal from T-Mobile.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: But trust in the privacy of BBM’s system has been eroded.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Part of the attraction to business people, revolutionaries, demonstrators and rioters was a belief that encrypted words sent over the company’s secure servers could not be traced back to their writers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Prosecutions after the riots put an end to that belief.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: Across town from Stockwell, outside the gates of a private school in well-heeled South Kensington, the older pupils all have Apple logos on their handsets.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: They all use WhatsApp.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: For many, BBM is a distant memory.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “I still have a Blackberry, but I’m the only one,” says a teenager standing with a circle of friends.
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: And how does that make him feel?
label: ignore

title: item_p0060
complex: “Isolated,” he replies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: Himalayan lakes, spacewalks and the presidential primaries helped Scott Kelly keep his wits over 340 days in space, the astronaut told journalists, after he landed back on Earth from a record-setting mission.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒIt seemed like I lived there forever,Ó Kelly said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: The veteran of past missions said that his biggest surprise was simply how long this one felt.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒMaybe, occasionally, you do go bananas,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: Kelly and a Russian counterpart, Mikhail Kornienko, spent nearly a year on the International Space Station (ISS) in order to study the effects of weightlessness, radiation and the cramped quarters of space ight on humans Ð research NASA considers essential for an eventual mission to Mars.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: Kelly said the length of the mission was its biggest challenge and that he felt signi cantly more sore on returning to gravity than after shorter trips.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: Kelly and his twin brother, Mark, a retired astronaut, have spent the last year taking physical and mental tests.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: The tests will continue, to help NASA learn about how the body copes with the severe strains of space ight.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: Kelly said he felt aches and had extremely sensitive skin but, so far, his balance has felt mostly decent.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: However, he said, Òthe rst thing I tried to throw on a table I missedÓ because Òyou tend to underestimate the effects of gravityÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: He said the discomfort of returning to gravity Ð which shrank him back down to normal height after he stretched by 1.5 inches in orbit Ð took nothing away from the awe he felt after his capsule landed back on Earth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: When the Russian capsule opened on to the cool air of Kazakhstan, Kelly said, he smelled Òa fragrance like a plant was blooming in that areaÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: It was the fresh air mingled with the charred, Òkind of sweetÓ smell of a spacecraft that had survived re-entry through the atmosphere.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: Backing away from the vessel, he said, the scope of the mission began to sink in: 340 days on a 15-year-old space station which is Òa million pounds, the size of a football eld, the internal volume, some say, of a six-bedroom houseÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: The ISS, he said, is a place that uses the power of the sun and was built with the help of an international team and millions of taxpayers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒThere are things weÕre going to discover about our experience in space in the space station that we donÕt even know now,Ó Kelly said, comparing the research of more than 450 missions there to the work done by computer scientists at NASA in the 1960s and 1970s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: He added: ÒThe view is great, too.Ó Kelly made the most of that view, posting spectacular photos on social media of the EarthÕs cities, landscapes, oceans and atmosphere.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒThe Earth is a beautiful planet,Ó he said, describing the striking colours of the waters around the Bahamas and the rainbow hues of lakes that dot the northern Himalayas.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: He said he would like to visit that region, though he would rst need to learn Òwhat country actually owns themÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: But, Òpredominantly, you just notice how thin the atmosphere is, how fragile it looks,Ó Kelly added.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒThat, combined with these large swathes of pollution, is kind of alarming.Ó The astronaut said he could see entire systems of pollution: smoke clouds from wild res that covered parts of the US, sections of Asia with continuous, visible pollution nearly all year round.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: He said the message Òwe need to save the planetÓ slightly missed the point: ÒThe planet will get better; itÕs us that wonÕt be here because weÕll destroy the environment.Ó The worldÕs thin shield of atmosphere Òmakes you more of an environmentalist after spending so much time looking downÓ, he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒItÕs for us to take care of the air we breathe and the water we drink.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: And I do believe we have an impact on that and we do have the ability to change it, if we make the decision to.Ó KellyÕs active social- media life, which he said was a joint effort with his girlfriend, earned him a huge online following.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: But he said he was unaware of it, instead he was watching the chaotic drama of the 2016 presidential election.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: One of the rst questions he asked the crew who lifted him out of a space capsule was ÒHow did Super Tuesday go?Ó But the astronaut demurred when asked for his thoughts on the likes of Donald Trump and Bernie Sanders.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒI would say that, as a government employee, I am subject to the Hatch Act,Ó he said, referring to a law that prohibits some political activity for federal employees.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒSo I canÕt say how I think of all the news stories of all the year.Ó Besides the news, he said, steady work helped keep him sane: ÒI tried to have milestones that were close, like when is the next crew arriving, the next spacewalk, the next robotics, the next science experiment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: That made a difference to me, keeping my sanity.Ó Being back on Earth with the rest of humanity had not quite sunk in, he added, recalling how shocked he had been to see a crowd of people after a previous mission.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒThereÕll be a point here pretty soon where IÕll start feeling that kind of culture shock,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: Although a NASA scientist said Kelly embodied the qualities needed for a mission to Mars, the astronaut himself predicted that he would not y again with the space agency.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒBut I donÕt think I would ever say IÕm absolutely, 100% done,Ó he added, noting the sudden successes of private space ight companies such as SpaceX.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒThey might need a guy like me someday,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0061
complex: ÒMaybe, in the next 20 years, youÕll be able to buy a cheap ticket, just go for a little visit.Ó
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: Sometimes life just isnÕt fair.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: Mark Zuckerberg created Facebook and is now worth an estimated $48bn.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: James Goodfellow also invented something used by millions of people around the world every day Ð the cash machine Ð but it didnÕt make him rich.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: In fact, he earned just £10 from the patent and has not made a penny more from it since.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: ÒYou can imagine how I feel when I see bankers getting £1m bonuses.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: I wonder what they contributed to the banking industry more than I did to merit a £1m bonus.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: It doesnÕt make much sense to me but thatÕs the way of the world,Ó Goodfellow says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: It also annoys him that heÕs not seen as a good role model for inventors and engineers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: He came up with a groundbreaking invention that generated billions of pounds Òand I got nothing, so whoÕs going to want to follow in James GoodfellowÕs footsteps and get £10 if they have a fantastic success?Ó There have been arguments for years over who should of cially go down in history as Òthe inventor of the ATMÓ and, in 2005, a man called John Shepherd-Barron received a UK honour for services to banking as the Òinventor of the automatic cash dispenserÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: But, the UK government is now saying it was Goodfellow who invented the ATM Ð so it would seem that, after all the squabbling, his place in history is now assured.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: Back in the mid-1960s, Goodfellow was working as a development engineer and had been asked to devise a way to enable customers to withdraw cash from banks on Saturdays.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: ÒMost people working during the week couldnÕt get to the bank.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: They wanted a solution.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: The solution was a machine which would issue cash on demand to a recognized customer,Ó he recalls.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: ÒI set out to develop a cash-issuing machine and, to make this a reality, I invented the PIN [personal identi cation number] and an associated coded token.Ó This token took the form of a plastic card with holes punched in it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: The patent documents proposed a system incorporating a card reader and buttons mounted in an external wall of the bank and stated: ÒWhen the customer wishes to withdraw a pack of banknotes from the system, he simply inserts his punched card in the card reader of the system and operates the set of ten push-buttons in accordance with his personal identification number.Ó Aside from the cards with punched holes, that pretty much describes todayÕs ATM.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: After Goodfellow successfully demonstrated the methodology by producing a model, prototypes were built and the rst machines were installed in 1967.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: At around the same time, Shepherd-Barron was developing a rival cash-dispensing device.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: His machine didnÕt use plastic cards Ð instead, it used cheques impregnated with carbon-14, a mildly radioactive substance.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: The machine detected the carbon-14, matched the cheque against a PIN and paid out the cash.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: It is widely accepted that the Shepherd-Barron ATM was the ÒworldÕs rstÓ when it comes to being installed and used by the public; the rst one, at a bank in north London, was opened on 27 June, 1967 Ð a month before GoodfellowÕs ATM made its public debut.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: However, the patent for GoodfellowÕs machine was lodged on 2 May, 1966, 14 months before the London ATM machine came into service.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: The rivalry between the two men bubbled up when Shepherd-Barron received an of cial honour for his achievement.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: Goodfellow says: ÒMy one big regret is that I never said anything about it until John Shepherd-Barron received the OBE in 2005.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: This honour was granted on the basis that he was the inventor of the automatic cash dispenser.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: That really stuck in my throat and I kicked up a bit of a fuss.Ó Shepherd-Barron is no longer alive to put his case but, in a 2005 interview, he was fairly withering about his rival: ÒI donÕt know him but itÕs clear that the difference between Goodfellow and us was that we thought through the whole system concept and that was important to the banks who bought it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: His invention reminds me of the hovercraft, an elegant failure.Ó The cash machine has become a world- conquering piece of technology and nothing Ð the contactless revolution, bitcoin, wearable technology, etc Ð seems to be slowing its growth: there are now 3m ATMs worldwide, with the number forecast to hit 4m by 2020.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: Goodfellow accepts he didnÕt invent the concept of a cash-issuing machine Òbut I did invent a way of doing it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: When people talk about the Wright brothers, they didnÕt invent the concept of ying Ð everyone was trying to do it Ð but they did it and got the credit for inventing the aeroplane so I think I should get the credit for inventing the cash dispenser.Ó The good news for Goodfellow is that this is now starting to happen.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: The website ATMInventor.com concludes: ÒWho invented the idea of an ATM?
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: We believe it was Luther George Simjian.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: Who invented the ATM as we know it?
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: We have to think it was James Goodfellow in Scotland for holding a patent date of 1966. Who invented the ATM design we recognize today?
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: We think it was John D White for Docutel in the US.Ó Even better for Goodfellow, his achievement has been of cially recognized in the latest edition of a 180-page guidebook called Life in the United Kingdom.
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: In the section about Ògreat British inventions of the twentieth centuryÓ, it states: ÒIn the 1960s, James Goodfellow (1937-) invented the cash-dispensing automatic teller machine (ATM) or ÔcashpointÕ.Ó So after all these years, Goodfellow nally nds himself being talked about in the same breath as John Logie Baird (the television), Alan Turing (the Turing machine), Sir Frank Whittle (the jet engine) and Sir Tim Berners-Lee (the World Wide Web).
label: ignore

title: item_p0062
complex: Asked what he did with the £10 he received back in the 1960s, Goodfellow says he thinks he blew it on a wild night out, adding: ÒIt didnÕt change my life.Ó But, he concludes, itÕs been a good working life: ÒI was very happy doing the job I was doing.Ó
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: When Larry Pizzi, a veteran bicycle industry executive, first heard about electric bikes nearly 20 years ago, he asked: “Why would anyone want to screw up a bike by putting a motor and batteries on it?” It’s a question that still puzzles traditionalists.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: Bicycle shops have been slow to stock e-bikes, even though they have been around since the late 1990s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: Sales in the US have been modest.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: Pizzi, who is now CEO of Currie Technologies, the number one seller of e-bikes in the US, believes that’s about to change.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: Others in the industry agree.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: Familiar brands including Trek, Raleigh and Specialized all offer electric models and they’re betting the market is about to take off.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: “We’re on the cusp of mainstream adoption,” Pizzi said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: “There are more players entering the category, it seems, with every passing month.” The US is an outlier when it comes to electric bikes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: Nearly 32m e-bikes were sold in 2014, the vast majority in China, where they are primarily used for transportation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: They are popular in much of Europe, too.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: They’re common in the Netherlands and Switzerland; German postal workers use them to get around and BMW offers one for about $3,000.
label: ignore

title: item_p0063
complex: Electric bikes are different from motorcycles or mopeds, which rely on motorized power; they are bicycles that can be pedalled with – or without – assistance from an electric motor.

title: item_p0065
complex: Carle, 26, set out, in 2013, to see if it was possible to live using only French-made products for ten months as part of a television documentary.

complex: The idea was triggered by the Minister for Economic Renewal Arnaud Montebourg’s call for the public to buy French to save the country’s industrial production sector.

complex: Fortunately, his long-suffering girlfriend, Anaïs, and cat, Loon, (both French) stuck with him.

title: item_p0065
complex: “Politicians say all sorts of things and expect us to go along with it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0065
complex: I wanted to see if it was possible and feasible to do what the minister was asking us to do; to hold him to account for his words,” Carle told the Guardian over a non-French coffee in a Parisian café after finishing his documentary.

title: item_p0065
complex: He set just three rules: eat only foods produced in France, eliminate contact with foreign-made goods and do so on €1,800 a month (above the minimum wage of €1,430 to cover the extra expense of living in Paris).

complex: The journalist was shocked to find out at the start of the experiment that only 4.5% of the contents of his flat were made nationally – and that the rest would have to go, including the lightbulbs (China) and green beans (Kenya).

complex: The removal men left his home almost bare.

label: ignore

title: item_p0065
complex: His foreign-made clothes, down to his underwear, were replaced with more expensive alternatives: French-produced underpants (€26), socks (€9), polo shirt (€75), espadrille sandals (€26), but no jeans as none are produced in France.

title: item_p0065
complex: During the experiment, Carle scoured supermarket shelves for 100% French-made products, learned to cook seasonal fruit and vegetables grown in France, proudly brushed his teeth with the last toothbrush made in France by a company in Picardie employing 29 people and hand-washed his smalls until he found the last French-made washing machine (which, being top opening, would not fit under the kitchen counter).

title: item_p0069
complex: The Senate Intelligence Committee has approved a bill that would provide for increased transparency of the National Security Agency’s bulk collection of US phone records but allow the controversial practice to continue.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: Sponsored by Chairwoman Dianne Feinstein, the bill lets the NSA continue to collect phone metadata of millions of Americans for renewable 90-day periods and allows the government to retain it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: Some legislators have alternatively proposed letting phone companies hold the metadata.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: It passed the committee by an 11-4 vote, paving the way for a full Senate vote.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: The bill allows analysts to search through the data if they suspect there is a ‘reasonable suspicion’ that a suspect is associated with international terrorism.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: Additionally, the bill allows the NSA to continue surveillance begun on foreigners outside the US if they enter the country ‘for a transitory period not to exceed 72 hours’.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: The bill is a direct challenge to one introduced by Senator Patrick Leahy that would end domestic phone-records collection.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: It was also opposed by leading Intelligence Committee member Mark Udall, who said it did not go far enough.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: “The NSA’s invasive surveillance of Americans’ private information does not respect our constitutional values and needs fundamental reform, not incidental changes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: Unfortunately, the bill passed by the Senate Intelligence Committee does not go far enough to address the NSA’s overreaching domestic surveillance programmes,” Udall said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: Another Democratic member of the committee, Ron Wyden, said the bill maintains “business as usual” and “remains far from anything that could be considered meaningful reform”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: Feinstein defended the NSA bulk collection programme, but said there was a need to rebuild public trust.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: “The NSA call-records programme is legal and subject to extensive congressional and judicial oversight, and I believe it contributes to our national security,” she said in a statement.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: “But more can, and should, be done to increase transparency and build public support for privacy protections in place.” In her statement, Feinstein said the bill would also make a number of improvements to transparency and oversight on the NSA, including: • requiring an annual public report of the total number of queries of NSA’s telephone metadata database and the number of times the programme leads to an FBI investigation or probable cause order; • requiring that the foreign intelligence surveillance court impose limits on the number of people at NSA who may authorize or query the call-records database; • establishing criminal penalties of up to ten years in prison for intentional unauthorized access to data acquired under the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA) by the United States; • mandating the FISA court impose a limit on the number of contacts an analyst can receive in response to a query of bulk communication records.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: After the committee’s hearing had ended, Feinstein strongly endorsed the NSA’s main domestic programme.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: “I think there’s huge misunderstanding about this NSA database programme, and how vital it is to protecting this country,” she told reporters.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: Concern over the Intelligence Committee’s bill was expressed by independent legal experts, who said the stage was now set for a showdown with the USA Freedom Act, a bill introduced by Leahy and Jim Sensenbrenner that would prohibit bulk collection of Americans’ telephone records.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: Elizabeth Goitein of the Brennan Center for Justice said: “The Intelligence Committee bill and the USA Freedom Act present two opposing visions of the relationship between law-abiding Americans and the national security state.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: The fundamental question is: should the government have some reason to suspect wrongdoing before sweeping up Americans’ most personal information to feed into its databases?
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: Leahy and Sensenbrenner say yes; Feinstein says no.” Wyden suggested that recent concern about NSA spying on foreign leaders had distracted from the real focus on mass domestic surveillance in the US.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: “The statements that American intelligence officials have made about collecting on the intentions of foreign leadership, that’s consistent with the understanding I’ve had for years, as a member of the Intelligence Committee,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: “That has implications for foreign policy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: My top priority is ending the mass surveillance, digital surveillance, of millions and millions of lawabiding Americans.” Feinstein unexpectedly announced that she was “totally opposed” to the foreign leader spying of the sort the NSA conducts of German Chancellor Angela Merkel.

title: item_p0069
complex: Feinstein has been a staunch supporter of the NSA’s bulk collection of Americans’ phone records.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: “Americans are making it clear, that they never – repeat, never – agreed to give up their constitutional liberties for the appearance of security,” Wyden said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0069
complex: “We’re just going to keep fighting this battle.

title: item_p0069
complex: It’s going to be a long one.” Separately, Feinstein said that James Clapper, the Director of National Intelligence, had agreed to provide her in writing with a statement about a Washington Post story that alleged the NSA had intercepted data in transmission between Google and Yahoo data centres.

complex: Wyden would not comment on the Washington Post report on the Google and Yahoo intercepts.

title: item_p0070
complex: An atmosphere of melancholy and changing times pervades the opening to the final series of Downton Abbey.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: The year is 1925 and there are already the first rumblings of the economic storms that will blight the end of the decade.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: The neighbours are selling up their own stately home, while Lord Grantham seeks to cut back on servants after declaring that under-butlers are no longer affordable.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: But at the real Downton Abbey, Highclere Castle – a stately home owned by George “Geordie” Herbert, 8th Earl of Carnarvon – the financial outlook has rarely been brighter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: According to Lady Fiona Carnarvon, the huge global success of Downton has funded a rolling programme of building repairs aimed at safeguarding Highclere for the next generation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: “It’s been an amazing magic carpet ride for all of us,” she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: “It’s given us a wonderful marketing platform, an international profile.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: I’m hugely grateful.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: My husband and I love the house, and the people here.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Now, without doubt, it is loved by millions of other people.” Currently, only the ground and first floors of Highclere, on the borders of Hampshire, are used.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: But, a restoration project of derelict tower rooms has begun that will eventually allow visitors to climb up into the tower to an exhibition showcasing the work of the architect of the Houses of Parliament, Sir Charles Barry, who rebuilt the house between 1839 and 1842.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: When the Downton Abbey producers first approached Highclere in 2009, the family faced a near £12m repair bill, with urgent work priced at £1.8m.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: But, by 2012, the Downton effect had begun to take the pressure off.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Lord Carnarvon said then: “It was just after the banking crisis and it was gloom in all directions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: We had been doing corporate functions but it all became pretty sparse after that.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Then, Downton came along and it became a major tourist attraction.” Visitor numbers doubled, to 1,200 a day, as Downton Abbey, scripted by Julian Fellowes, came to be screened around the world after becoming a hit in the UK in 2010 and, then, in the US.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: It is now broadcast in 250 countries.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: The formerly somewhat basic ticketing policy has become a computerized advance booking system, helping to guarantee foreign visitors admission.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: The accounts of Highclere Enterprises for 2014-15 show current assets have almost trebled to around £1m since 2012.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Gareth Neame, the executive producer for the series, said: “I think Downton Abbey secured Highclere’s future.” Peter Fincham, ITV’s director of television, recalls the moment when Highclere was booked.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: “I thought, ‘So what?’, because I had never heard of Highclere Castle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: One stately home looks much the same as another.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: How wrong I was.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: The castle has been one enormous character as well.” The Downton tourists are part of a growing phenomenon.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: VisitBritain estimates that nearly 30% of foreign visitors, or nearly nine million people, include castles and historic houses on their itineraries.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Almost half of potential visitors to Britain now say they want to indulge in “set jetting”, visiting places featured in films or on TV.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: More than a million embark on a tour of historic buildings each year, spending in excess of £1bn.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: From the biggest emerging tourist markets, 51% of Brazilians, 42% of Russians and Chinese, and 35% of Indian visitors are likely to include a visit to a site of interest in their trips.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: VisitBritain’s director, Patricia Yates, said: “The links between tourism, films and TV are potent ones.” She added that period dramas have also raised the popularity of regions outside of London.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Neame is now an ambassador for the GREAT Britain campaign, which is backed by government departments and the British Council, using it to promote the UK around the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Events include special Downton-themed receptions at British embassies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Neame said: “They approached me because of the reach.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: A lot of people here think of it as soapy entertainment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: In other parts of the world, people revere our actors, our writing and production talent.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: It is something I am passionate about; I am a really strong believer in soft power.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: We are not nearly as proud of our achievements as we should be.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: “Downton Abbey is iconic for expressing Britishness.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Really, it is a fantasy world, based in a particular time in history.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: It’s the first TV period drama that has really leapt out of the screen and become part of popular culture.” Lady Carnarvon is still keen to emphasize that the long-term future of Highclere is not necessarily secure.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: “The bottom line is quite thin,” she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: “The programme has allowed us to spend faster on the buildings, have the follies restored.” In the pipeline is a Tutankhamun centenary event in 2022, 100 years after the 5th Earl of Carnarvon, together with Howard Carter, discovered the tomb that revolutionized our understanding of Egyptology.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Another opportunity to keep Highclere in the public mind is the 300th anniversary of the birth of Lancelot “Capability” Brown, who designed the grounds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: “What you do is never sit on your laurels.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Every single day, don’t take anything for granted,” said Lady Carnarvon.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: “For all these great houses, you have to invest in them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: And, there has been a deficit since the 1930s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: Perhaps, in the past, an estate and house defined and supported the family and their lifestyle but, today, it is quite the reverse: the challenge is how Geordie and I seek to support and look after Highclere.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: “From my point of view, I’ve tried to persuade people it is fun and have specific events they can engage with, not just a wander around a dusty house.
label: ignore

title: item_p0070
complex: We have to compete with attractions like the London Dungeon.”

title: item_p0071
complex: Amsterdam still looks liberal to tourists, who were recently assured by the Labour Mayor that the city’s marijuana-selling coffee shops would stay open despite a new national law tackling drug tourism.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: But the Dutch capital may lose its reputation for tolerance over plans to dispatch nuisance neighbours to “scum villages” made from shipping containers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: The Mayor, Eberhard van der Laan, insists his controversial new £810,000 policy to tackle antisocial behaviour is to protect victims of abuse and homophobia from harassment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: The camps where antisocial tenants will be rehoused for three to six months have been called “scum villages” because the policy echoes proposals from Geert Wilders, the far-right populist, who last year demanded that “repeat offenders” be “sent to a village for scum”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: But Bartho Boer, a spokesman for the Mayor, denies that the plans are illiberal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: “We want to defend the liberal values of Amsterdam,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: “We want everyone to be who he and she is – whether they are gay and lesbian or stand up to violence and are then victims of harassment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: We as a society want to defend them.” According to Boer, the villages are not for “the regular nuisance between two neighbours where one has the stereo too loud on Saturday night” but “people who are extremely violent and intimidating, and in a clear situation where a victim is being repeatedly harassed”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: Those deemed guilty of causing “extreme havoc” will be evicted and placed in temporary homes of a “basic” nature, including converted shipping containers in industrial areas of the city.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: “We call it a living container,” says Boer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: Housing antisocial tenants in these units, which have showers and kitchens and have been used as student accommodation, will ensure that they are not “rewarded” by being relocated to better accommodation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: Dutch newspaper the Parool has pointed out that in the 19th century troublemakers were moved to villages in Drenthe and Overijssel, which rapidly became slums.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: But Boer insists that the administration has learned from past mistakes and is not planning to house the antisocial together.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: It would be more accurate to call them “scum houses” than scum villages, says Boer, “because we don’t want to put more than one of these families in the same area”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: After up to six months in these houses, scattered around the city, the tenants will be found permanent homes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: The city government anticipates moving around ten families a year into this programme, which starts in 2013.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: The temporary dwellings will be heavily policed, but antisocial tenants will also have access to doctors, social workers and parole officers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0071
complex: “They are taken care of so the whole situation is not going to repeat at the new house they are in,” says Boer.

title: item_p0072
complex: Is this the moment when streaming goes truly mainstream?
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: According to the International Federation of the Phonographic Industry (IFPI), there were just 41m subscribers using music streaming services globally in 2014.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: It might be the biggest revenue growth area for the record business but it is still incredibly niche.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Not only that, but a signi cant number of those subscribers come from bundled deals with mobile phone operators so it is debatable just how ÒactiveÓ its users are.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: AppleÕs greatest conjuring trick is to take something that already exists in the market Ð downloads (iTunes), digital music players (the iPod) and smartphones (the iPhone) Ð and adapt it to make it irresistible to the mainstream consumer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Leaked information recently suggested that Apple is aiming to reach 100m subscribers, which, based on a subscription fee of $120 per year, would generate $12bn annually.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: To put that in context, the entire global worth of recorded music in 2014 was just under $15bn.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple is good at mainstreaming products but itÕs not that good.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Is this the end of downloading?
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: The iTunes Store arrived in 2003 (2004 in Europe) at a time when MP3 piracy seemed insurmountable.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple managed to persuade consumers to pay for downloads and grew a huge business, which it dominated, with an estimated 70% market share.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Downloads still hold the biggest share of digital income for recorded music, making up 52% of total digital income in 2014 according to IFPI numbers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple holds the lionÕs share of this, making it the single biggest music retailer in the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: But download revenue peaked in 2013 in the UK at £283m and fell to £249m in 2014.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: The decline in download sales hit the US in 2013 so Apple bought Beats in 2014 for $3bn, not only to get into the premium headphone market, but also to make the transition from music ownership (downloads) to music access (subscription streaming).
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple, and the record industry, cannot afford to get rid of the download market just yet Ð so streaming and downloading will have to coexist under the Apple brand, one representing the future as the other gets progressively slower and sicker.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: The vast majority of people out there like music but donÕt love it enough to pay $120 a year to listen to it; a sporadic download purchase here and there will suit them just ne.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: The average spend of a music buyer in the UK in 2014, for example, was just £39.52, according to research.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Expecting most of them to triple their annual spend on recorded music is something that even Apple will seriously struggle with.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Has Apple Connect made Apple the most artist-friendly service?
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple have previously tried to build an artist-friendly platform via iTunes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: They called it Ping and it didnÕt work.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple Connect is something very different, somewhere in the middle of YouTube, Facebook and SoundCloud, letting artists post music, videos, photos and more to their pro le pages.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: It couldnÕt be any more 2015 if it tried.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple, partly because of its scale and also partly because it treats music as ÒartÓ rather than ÒcontentÓ, has generally had strong relations with not just the music industry but artists themselves and, generally, it has a good reputation among artists.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Compare that to Spotify, which has been criticized by artists from RadioheadÕs Thom Yorke to Taylor Swift.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: In contrast, Apple comes across as a benevolent uncle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: There is the smell of revolution in the air and Apple is making sure itÕs on the right side of the battle lines when they are drawn up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Where are the artist exclusives?
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: This is going to be the interesting bit when the service actually goes live.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Getting exclusives for big albums is going to become crucial to streaming.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Spotify paid a lot of money to get Led Zeppelin and Metallica exclusively.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple will have been watching this carefully and making its own notes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: It already has AC/DC and the Beatles catalogues exclusively for download on iTunes but it remains to be seen if it can persuade these two to make the jump into streaming.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: It also managed to get the surprise Beyonce album in 2013 before anyone else so it is inevitable that it will want more like that.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: There is plenty of speculation about Apple looking to get exclusive rights to the new Adele album.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Giving iTunes the download exclusive on an album was not that big a leap of faith for acts given just how much of the download market it controls but trying to do that in streaming is not the same thing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Add into this the fact that streaming now counts towards the album chart in markets like the UK and US and artists, who still see the chart as a measure of success, will not want to limit their audience by restricting themselves to one service.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Is this going to kill Spotify?
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Some people are already saying that Apple Music will destroy rivals like Spotify.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: However, itÕs not that simple.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple is entering a market where others have been working and gaining experience for many years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: It has a lot of catching up to do.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: The winner of this battle will not be the company with the best service; it will be the company with the most money.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: AppleÕs competitors have a head start in the market but they are losing huge amounts of money.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Spotify, for example, lost Û93.1m in 2013.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: Apple, on the other hand, started 2015 by becoming the most pro table company in corporate history, with $178bn in the bank.
label: ignore

title: item_p0072
complex: If Apple Music loses Apple money, the company will not continue it for long but it will not stop investment without at least trying to beat the competition.
