label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: The gang, with Gao the alleged ringleader, stands accused of laundering up to €300m a year, as well as selling counterfeit goods and toys with fake safety marks.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: The government prosecutor said Gao’s illegal business was so big it was damaging the competitiveness of Spain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Gao is on bail; the case has not yet come to trial.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Law enforcement officials have long had concerns about €500 notes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Small and easy to transport relative to their value, they are the payment method of choice for tax dodgers, money launderers and drug barons.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: The sum of €1m in €500 notes fits easily into a small laptop bag, where the same amount in €50 notes would require a small suitcase.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Cash mules have been known to fold the notes into plastic pellets and swallow them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: A less dangerous method of concealment is to stuff the banknotes into a car chassis.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: The UK stopped distribution of the €500 note in 2010 on the grounds that demand for it was “almost entirely for criminal purposes”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: In 2009, Italy’s central bank warned that the notes were widely used by mafia money launderers and terrorists.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Other countries have limited their own highdenomination notes due to links to organized crime – Canada scrapped its $1,000 note in 2000 on the advice of law enforcement officers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: In an age of electronic payment systems and contactless cards, more are questioning whether printing these notes can be justified.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Peter Sands, the former head of Standard Chartered Bank, has called for the abolition of high-denomination notes, including the €500, the $100, the 1,000 Swiss Franc note and the £50.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: In a report for the Harvard Kennedy School of Government, Sands argued it was time to get rid of high-value notes that make life easier for “bad guys” pursuing tax evasion, financial crime, terrorist finance and corruption.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Although criminals would switch to smallerdenomination bills, or gold or diamonds, these substitutes are bulkier and more traceable, making it more likely they will get caught, he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: At a conference on terrorist financing in London, the Head of Europol, Rob Wainwright, called on the European Central Bank (ECB) to look at whether it “should continue to produce and circulate these notes that make it easier for criminals and terrorists to hide their business and to fund illegal activities”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: According to Europol, the purple €500 note accounts for 30% of the value of all the euro notes in circulation, although most people have never seen one.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: The €500 note was introduced in 2002 when the euro was born: it replaced the 1,000 Deutschmark, the 10,000 Belgian franc and the 500,000 Italian lira.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Several European countries favoured highvalue banknotes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: “It is definitely a preference that has been there for a long time,” says Pia Hüttl, an affiliate fellow at the Bruegel thinktank.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: “The preference is based on the idea that cash has a lower cost and is accepted everywhere.” Cash remains king in Germany and Austria, where more than half of all transactions are made with paper money and coins.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: The former president of Germany’s constitutional court, Hans-Jürgen Papier, told Germany’s Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung that restrictions on cash were at odds with individual freedom, while tabloid newspaper Bild has launched a petition in defence of paper money, including the €500 note – “hands off my cash”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Law enforcement authorities are less convinced, amid a steady stream of reports of suspicious bundles of cash.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: In one case that has caught the attention of police, two men walked into a bank and tried to deposit €200,000 of torn and muddy €500 notes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: In the same week, €1.3m in €500 notes was found stuffed in the false bottom of suitcase.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: But, a suspicion of criminality is not enough to keep people in custody.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: “Our frustration from a law enforcement perspective is that, in many jurisdictions, it is impossible to provide sufficient evidence to satisfy judicial authorities of a link between suspicious cash detections and criminality,” says Jennifer MacLeod, a specialist in Europol’s financial intelligence group.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: “The search for these links is complicated further through time constraints and fragmented cooperation and information exchange.” The agency would like to see central banks take more responsibility for the “striking anomalies” in the use of €500 notes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Luxembourg, for example, issued more than twice its annual GDP in banknotes in 2013 alone, despite being one of the most cash-averse countries in Europe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Europol asked Luxembourg’s central bank to explain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: “The reply we had from Luxembourg is that they simply issue the notes requested and have no explanation for the reasons behind the demand,” MacLeod says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: “I find it surprising that a central bank does not consider itself to have a responsibility in this area.” This could be changing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Mario Draghi, the head of the ECB, has said he is determined that the income the bank generates from issuing the notes should not be “a comfort for criminals”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: Other members of the ECB’s top team, such as Yves Mersch, contend there is no evidence about the criminal uses of the €500 note.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: But, amid heightened fears about terrorism, this argument may no longer cut any ice.
label: ignore

title: item_p0084
complex: EU finance ministers have called on policymakers to explore “appropriate restrictions” on high-value notes and report back by 1 May 2016.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: The Duke and Duchess of Cambridge won the first round in their battle for privacy on Tuesday when a French magazine was banned from selling or reusing images taken of the couple at a private chateau in Provence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: But the war is far from over as French prosecutors must now decide if criminal proceedings are to be brought against the magazine editor and the photographer or photographers responsible for taking pictures of the duchess sunbathing topless while on holiday in the south of France.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: The Tribunal de Grande Instance in Nanterre, Paris granted an injunction ordering the gossip magazine Closer to hand over digital files of the pictures within 24 hours and preventing it disseminating them any further, including on its website and tablet app.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: The four-page ruling, which only affects Mondadori Magazines France, Closer’s publisher, also ordered it to pay €2,000 in legal costs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: The magazine faces a €10,000 fine for every day it fails to comply with the order.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: No damages were sought by the couple.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: “These snapshots, which showed the intimacy of a couple, partially naked on the terrace of a private home, surrounded by a park several hundred metres from a public road, and being able to legitimately assume that they are protected from passersby, are by nature particularly intrusive,” it said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: The magistrates ruled that every photograph published in France by Mondadori, the publishing company owned by former Italian Prime Minister Silvio Berlusconi, in future would carry a fine, also of €10,000 per breach.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: But the ruling refers only to the 14 pictures that have already been published.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: Closer’s editor has hinted she has other, more intimate pictures.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: St James’s Palace said the couple “welcome the judge’s ruling”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: A source said: “They always believed the law was broken and that they were entitled to their privacy.” Maud Sobel, a lawyer for the royal couple in Paris, described it as “a wonderful decision,” adding: “We’ve been vindicated.” Though pleased their civil action has succeeded, the couple have taken the rare step of seeking to have a much more public criminal prosecution for breach of privacy brought against the magazine and photographer or photographers responsible.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: The prosecutor will have to decide the targets for any criminal proceedings and the complaint cites “persons unknown”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: But it is understood the couple want proceedings brought against the editor of Closer, which published the photos on Friday, and whoever took the images of the couple sunbathing at the chateau, which belongs to Lord Linley, son of the late Princess Margaret.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: A preliminary investigation was launched on Tuesday by the Paris police.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: Under French law breach of privacy carries a maximum sentence of one year in prison and a fine of €45,000.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: This is the legal action that will truly lay down a marker, and by pursuing it the couple indicate a determination to convey a wider message to the world and, they hope, deter paparazzi in the future.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: Their lawyers had not asked for copies of Closer magazine to be removed from shelves.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: On Saturday the Irish Daily Star published the photos, leading to the editor being suspended on Monday night pending the outcome of an internal investigation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: Also on Monday, the Mondadori-owned Italian celebrity magazine Chi rushed out a special edition with 26 pages devoted to the candid photos of the future queen.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: The couple’s lawyer, Aurélien Hamelle, had told the Paris court it was necessary to block the “highly intimate” images of the duchess without her bikini top as she was a “young woman, not an object”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: But Delphine Pando, defending Closer, said the action was a “disproportionate response” to publication of the photographs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: She added that the magazine could not control their resale as it did not own the original images.
label: ignore

title: item_p0085
complex: Copies of Closer magazine were doing brisk business on online auction site eBay, with one selling for £31.01, until the site removed all listings following “strong feedback” from its community.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: The view from the visitors centre at the southern edge of Donana National Park in southern Spain is striking, to say the least.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: This is an ornithologists dream: 200,000 hectares of salt marsh of unrivalled importance to the birdlife of western Europe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Dozens of Britains most loved migratory birds, including house martins, swallows, cuckoos and warblers, rest here on their annual migrations from Africa.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Donana, a UN World Heritage Site, is also home to some of Europes rarest birds, including the Spanish imperial eagle, while its mammalian inhabitants include the highly endangered Iberian lynx.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: It is a glorious, vibrant landscape.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Yet it exists on a knife-edge, a point illustrated dramatically in 1998 when almost two billion gallons of contaminated, highly acidic water, mixed with waste metals, poured into the park from a dam that had burst its bank at Los Frailes mine 45km to the north.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: A toxic tsunami of waste poured down the Guadiamar river and over its banks, leaving a thick metallic crust over a vast stretch of parkland.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: More than 25,000 kilos of dead sh were collected in the aftermath and nearly 2,000 adult birds, chicks, eggs and nests were killed or destroyed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: It was Spains worst environmental disaster and the clean-up cost ¬90m.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Suddenly aware of Donanas status as the nations most important natural site, Spain decided to spend a further ¬360m, some of it EU money, on restoring the landscape, which, in the 1950s and 60s, had been drained in places to create rice and cotton elds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Some of this farmland is now being returned to its original wetland state.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: It has been a costly but encouraging process.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Yet the fate of Donana still hangs in the balance thanks to the increasing pressures of modern life.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Plans have been outlined to build an oil pipeline through Donana, while other developers have announced proposals to expand local tourist resorts whose new hotels and golf courses would demand water supplies that would further erode the local table.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Silt washed from nearby farms is also choking the channels that criss-cross Donana.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: However, the real body blow for conservationists has been the recent decision of the Andalucian government to reopen the Frailes mine that so very nearly destroyed Donana in 1998.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: This is Europes most precious bird sanctuary, both in terms of indigenous species and also as a resting place for birds that migrate between Africa and Britain and other parts of north-west Europe, says Laurence Rose of the RSPB (Royal Society for the Protection of Birds).
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Donana already faces a great number of threats, but now they want to bring back the very cause of its near-undoing 16 years ago.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: It is extremely worrying.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Having spent so much restoring Donana to its past glories, it might seem strange that the local government should choose to announce that it wants mining companies to tender bids to rework Los Frailes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: However, a brief examination of the state of the local economy provides an explanation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: The crash of Spains banks in 2008 hit the region catastrophically and unemployment in some parts of Andalucia is now more than 30%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Reopening the mine would provide more than 1,000 precious jobs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: "There are riches here, riches that are badly needed by local inhabitants, said Vicente Fernandez Guerrero, secretary-general of Innovation, Industry and Energy for Andalucia.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: We think mining is a good way to make it possible to allow local people to continue to live in the area.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: This is a mining area.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: People have been digging metals and ores here since Roman times, after all.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: More to the point, added Fernandez, the mine licence would stipulate that only modern mining techniques, which avoid the creation of poisonous wet waste, would be allowed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: (It has also stipulated that the Canadian company that ran Los Frailes cannot bid for the contract.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Sixteen years after the accident, the two sides remained locked over compensation claims.)
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: The best technology in the world will be used here, Fernandez insisted.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Liquid will not be used.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: We are going to insist on that.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Our tender makes that clear.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: The proposal has some support in the area, but it also has a lot of opposition.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: One road sign I passed was liberally daubed with obscenities about the workers union UGT, which supports the mines reopening.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: For his part, Carlos Davila, who works for the Spanish Ornithological Society in Donana, was also alarmed at the proposal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: This is a very, very bad idea indeed, he told me.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: They say the new mine will be safe, but they said it was safe in 1998 and look what happened.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: We got the worst ecological disaster in the history of Spain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: What alarms people such as Davila is the threat that a new mine poses to the intense investment in eco-tourism that has been made in Donana in recent years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: An example is provided at the restaurant Dehesa de Abajo, where you can have a drink or a meal surrounded by trees in which storks and black kites are nesting.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Virtually every visitor was equipped with a camera and telescopic lens or a pair of binoculars.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: There is a clear tourist trade to be made from the birdlife of Don_ana.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Nor should this be surprising, for this is a truly special place.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: A vast hemisphere of sky hangs over this utterly at but certainly not featureless landscape.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Birds of every shape and size ll the air and sometimes the road.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: At one point on my visit, a stork calmly stood in front of our car until it felt ready to y off.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: The trouble is that Spain does not have the public resources it possessed 16 years ago.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: A repeat of the toxin spill today would have a much, much more damaging impact, said Rose.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: This point is backed by Davila.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: After the disaster, Spain woke up to the fact that it possessed a place of real ecological importance and did a lot to clean it up and protect it, he added.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: Now, we seem to be forgetting that lesson.
label: ignore

title: item_p0086
complex: It is very depressing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Brazil experienced one of its biggest nights of protest in decades as more than 100,000 people took to the streets nationwide to express their frustration at heavy-handed policing, poor public services and high costs for the World Cup.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: The major demonstrations in Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo, Brasilia, Belem, Belo Horizonte, Salvador and elsewhere started peacefully, but several led to clashes with police and arson attacks on cars and buses.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: The large turnout and geographic spread marked a rapid escalation after previous, smaller protests against bus price increases led to complaints that police responded disproportionately with rubber bullets, tear gas and violent beatings.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Coinciding with the start of the Confederations Cup – a World Cup test event – the rallies brought together a wide coalition of people frustrated with the escalating costs and persistently poor quality of public services, lavish investment in international sporting events, low standards of health care and wider unease about inequality and corruption.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: While the vast majority of demonstrations in Rio were peaceful, several police were injured in clashes at the city’s legislative assembly, at least one car was overturned and burned, and windows were smashed in the offices of banks and notary offices.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: The unrest escalated during the night as a large crowd set several fires outside the legislative assembly, smashed the building’s windows and painted graffiti on the walls proclaiming “Revolution”, “Down with Paes, down with Cabral [the mayor and state governor]” and “Hate police”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Police inside responded with pepper spray and perhaps more – the Guardian saw one protester passed out and bleeding heavily from a wound in the upper arm.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: The causes pursued by the protesters varied widely.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: “We are here because we hate the government.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: They do nothing for us,” said Oscar José Santos, a 19 year old who was with a group of hooded youths from the Rocinha favela.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: “I’m an architect but I have been unemployed for six months.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: There must be something wrong with this country,” said Nadia al Husin, holding up a banner calling on the government to do more for education.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: At a far smaller rally in Brasilia, demonstrators broke through police lines to enter the high-security area of the national congress.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Several climbed onto the roof.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: In Belo Horizonte, police clashed with protesters who tried to break through a cordon around a football stadium hosting a Confederations Cup match between Nigeria and Tahiti.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: In Porto Alegre, demonstrators set fire to a bus and, in Curitiba, protesters attempted to force their way into the office of the state governor.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: There were also rallies in Belem, Salvador and elsewhere.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: In São Paulo, which had seen the fiercest clashes the previous week and the main allegations of police violence, large crowds gathered once again but initial reports suggested the marches passed peacefully.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Reflecting the importance of social networks in spreading the message about the protests, some in São Paulo – where numbers were estimated at between 30,000 and 100,000 – carried banners declaring “We come from Facebook”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Most protesters were young and, for many, it was their first experience of such a giant rally.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: “My generation has never experienced this,” said Thiago Firbida, a student.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: “Since the dictatorship, Brazilians never bothered to take over the streets.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: They did not believe they had a reason to.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: But now Brazil is once again in crisis, with a constant rise in prices, so people are finally reacting.” Comparisons have been drawn with rallies in Turkey and elsewhere.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Another global link was evident in the handful of demonstrators who wore Guy Fawkes masks, associated with Anonymous and the Occupy Wall Street protests.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Brazil’s demonstrations are being referred to as the “vinegar revolution” (after police arrested people for carrying vinegar to counter tear gas), as well as the “20-cent revolution” (due to the bus price rise) and the Passe Livre (after the demand for free public transport).
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Some said the protests felt un-Brazilian but liberating.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: “Our politicians need to see the strength we have as one people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Brazilians tend to be too nice sometimes – they enjoy partying rather than protesting – but something is changing,” said Deli Borsari, a 53-year-old yoga instructor.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Following widespread news coverage of the costs of new and refurbished stadiums, the Confederations Cup football tournament has been one of the focuses of the protests.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Before the opening match in Brasilia, crowds of demonstrators were dispersed by riot police.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Footage showed frightened Japanese supporters rushing from the area holding their children, as the sound of shots – perhaps rubber bullets or tear gas – was heard.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Another protest march, near Rio’s Maracana Stadium, was met with a similarly heavy police response.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: Most of the rallies appeared to start peacefully until they confronted the security forces, who are largely organized at a regional level.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: President Dilma Rousseff condones the protests, according to her aides.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: “The president believes peaceful protests are legitimate and proper for a democracy, and that it is natural for young people to demonstrate,” said Helena Chagas of the president’s office.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: However, the president was booed at the opening ceremony for the Confederations Cup.
label: ignore

title: item_p0087
complex: With the economy in bad shape and social unrest on the rise, she faces a serious political challenge, both now and in 2014, when Brazil will not only host the World Cup but also have a presidential election.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: In homes and cafes across the country, a cup of tea, baked beans on toast and fish and chips have long played a key role in the British dining experience.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: But, the extent of a change in tastes over the generations has been captured in a dataset published recently in the National Food Survey, which was set up in 1940 by the government after concerns about health and access to food.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Despite the apparent British love of tea, consumption has more than halved since the 1970s, falling from 68g of tea per person per week to only 25g.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: With a teabag or portion of loose tea weighing around 3g, that means Britons are drinking on average only eight cups of tea a week today, down from 23 cups in 1974.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: And, while tea remains the most drunk hot drink in the UK, households now spend more on coffee.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: The data, published by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs as part of its “open data” scheme, is from 150,000 households who took part in the survey between 1974 and 2000, combined with information from 2000 to 2014.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: It shows some moves towards healthier diets in recent decades, with shifts to low-calorie soft drinks, from whole to skimmed milk and increasing consumption of fresh fruit.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: But, weekly consumption of chips, pizza, crisps and ready meals has soared.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: There has also been a dramatic shift from white to brown, wholemeal and other bread but the figures suggest the amount people are eating has fallen from 25 to 15 slices a week over the past four decades, based on a 40g slice from a medium sliced loaf.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: The consumption of baked beans in sauce has dropped by a fifth despite a rise in other types of convenience food, particularly Italian dishes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Adults in the UK now eat an average of 75g of pizza every week compared with none in 1974, while the consumption of pasta has almost tripled over the same period.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Fresh potatoes are also becoming less essential with a 67% decrease from 1974, when adults ate the equivalent of 188g every day.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Other vegetables such as cucumbers, courgettes, aubergines and mushrooms have gained space on the table.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Consumption of takeaway food has almost doubled since 1974, from 80g per person per week to 150g.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Around 33g of this amount is chips and 56g is meat, with kebabs (10g), chicken (7g), burgers (5g) and “meat-based meals” (32g) particularly popular.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Some trends suggest that British people are becoming more prudent in what they put on their plates, with the average consumption of fruit, both fresh and processed, increasing by 50% since 1974.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: In 2014, UK adults ate an average of 157g of fruit per day, equivalent to almost two portions of the five-a-day recommendation from the government.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Bananas have been the most popular fruit in the UK since 1996, reaching 221g per adult per week in 2014, well above apples (131g) and oranges (48g).
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Low-calorie soft drinks represented half of all soft drinks consumed in 2014 for the first time.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088
complex: Other social changes emerge from the survey, with questions about owning chickens and getting your own eggs being dropped in 1991 and a somewhat belated end in the same year to asking the “housewife” to fill out the questionnaire.
label: ignore

title: item_p0088

title: item_p0090
complex: Lots of us know we are sleep-deprived but imagine if we could x it with a fairly simple solution: getting up later.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: In a speech at the British Science Festival, Dr Paul Kelley, clinical research associate at the Sleep and Circadian Neuroscience Institute at Oxford University, called for schools to stagger their starting times to work with the natural biological rhythms of their students.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: It would improve cognitive performance, exam results and studentsÕ health (sleep deprivation has been linked with diabetes, depression, obesity and an impaired immune system).
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: It follows a paper, published in 2014, in which he noted that, when children are around ten, their biological wake-up time is about 6.30am; at 16, this rises to 8am; and, at 18, someone you may think of as a lazy teenager actually has a natural waking hour of 9am.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: The conventional school starting time works for 10-year-olds but not 16- to 18-year-olds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: For the older teenagers, it might be more sensible to start the school day at 11am or even later.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: ÒA 7am alarm call for older adolescents,Ó Kelley and his colleagues pointed out in the paper, Òis the equivalent of a 4.30am start for a teacher in their 50s.Ó He says itÕs not as simple as persuading teenagers to go to bed earlier.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: ÒThe bodyÕs natural rhythm is controlled by a particular kind of light,Ó says Kelley.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: ÒThe eye doesnÕt just contain rods and cones; it contains cells that then report to the suprachiasmatic nuclei in the hypothalamus.Ó This part of the brain controls our circadian rhythms over a 24-hour cycle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: ÒItÕs the light that controls it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: ItÕs like saying: ÔWhy canÕt you control your heartbeat?ÕÓ But it isnÕt just students who would bene t from a later start.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: Kelley says the working day should be more forgiving of our natural rhythms.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: Describing the average sleep loss per night for different age groups, he says: ÒBetween 14 and 24, itÕs more than two hours.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: For people aged between 24 and about 30 or 35, itÕs about an hour and a half.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: That can continue up until youÕre about 55 when itÕs in balance again.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: The 10-year-old and 55-year-old wake and sleep naturally at the same time.Ó This might be why, he adds, the traditional nine to ve is so ingrained; it is maintained by bosses, many of them in their mid-50s and upwards because Òit is best for themÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: So, should workplaces have staggered starting times, too?
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: Should those in their 50s and above come in at 8am, while those in their 30s start at 10am and the teenage intern or apprentice be encouraged to turn up at 11am?
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: Kelley says that synchronized hours could have Òmany positive consequences.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: The positive side of this is peopleÕs performance, mood and health will improve.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: ItÕs very uplifting in a way because itÕs a solution that will make people less ill, and happier and better at what they do.Ó There would probably be fewer accidents as drivers would be more alert, he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: It could spell the end of rush hour as people stagger their work and school-run times.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: A later start to the day for many, says Kelley, Òis something that would bene t everyone, particularly families.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: Parents go and try to wake up teenagers who are waking up three hours too early.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: It creates tensions for everybody.Ó So, what time does Kelley start work?
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: ÒI am 67 so that means IÕm back to being ten years old and I get up just after six.
label: ignore

title: item_p0090
complex: I wake naturally.Ó And, yes, he says he nds the start of his working day much easier now than he did when he was younger.

complex: More than one million British workers might be employed on zero-hours contracts, new gures reveal, suggesting that British business is using the controversial employment terms far more widely than previously thought.

complex: Vidhya Alakeson, from the Resolution Foundation, an independent think tank, added: ÒIf itÕs true that there are around one million people on zero-hours contracts, then that would be a substantial portion of the workforce Ð this could no longer be dismissed as an issue affecting only a tiny minority.

title: item_p0091
complex: ÒThe new estimate underlines the urgent need for a deep and thorough review of zero-hours by the government, which takes into account not only the scale of the problem but the effect these contracts have on workersÕ employment rights, earning capacity and personal well-being.Ó Unions and poverty campaign groups have accused employers of pressuring staff into signing the contracts as a way to evade their responsibilities and cut staff bene ts.

title: item_p0092

title: item_p0093
complex: James Hamblin, senior editor of American magazine The Atlantic, recently joined the unwashed masses.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: As part of his series, ÔIf Our Bodies Could TalkÕ, Hamblin, a relatively sane- looking man, took on a no-showering challenge to examine the effect of over-cleansing the body.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: He reduced the number of showers he had and eliminated shampoo and soap when he did.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: In doing so, he discovered what thousands of others have: the more we fervently try to clean ourselves with soaps, body washes and those silly little body polishers, the harder our skin works to restore equilibrium, encouraging us to begin the whole bewildering process again.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: Showering strips the skin of its own oil and bacteria Ð which, many would argue, is the whole point of showering Ð but, apparently, this sometimes works a little too well, especially when you add hot water and cleansing products to the mix.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: You know that feeling after a shower when you feel like youÕre stuck in a skin suit two sizes too small?
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: ThatÕs because much of your skinÕs natural moisture has been washed down the drain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: Additionally, our skin, much like our gut, plays host to millions of bene cial bacteria.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: Showering destroys these happy bacterial colonies; theyÕre completely wiped out by all of our frequent rubbing and scrubbing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: And, when the bacteria washed off by soap repopulate, they tend to favour microbes which produce an odour Ð yes, too-frequent showering may actually make you smell more.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: When you stop showering and using soap, however, your skin goes through an initial (likely gross) adjustment period, after which the skin typically restores balance, oil production slows and healthy bacteria ourish.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: After everything was said and done, Hamblin realized what other no-soap/no-shower devotees have known for years: that the human body, functioning on its own, is actually quite lovely.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: ItÕs not just scent or aesthetics, either Ð although dermatologists suggest that slowing down on soap use can improve acne and eczema.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: Reducing the frequency of showers (and the number of cleansing products used) has very real implications for our environment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: The average shower lasts seven minutes and uses 65 litres of water.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: ThatÕs 65 litres of clean, drinkable water that weÕre infusing with soap and washing down the drain each and every day Ð sometimes more than once.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: The vital importance of clean water is becoming harder and harder to ignore, as California enters another summer of drought.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: ItÕs becoming clear that clean water is one of the worldÕs most valuable commodities and one that will soon be in short supply.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: Add in the environmental effect of all those body wash bottles and youÕve got yourself a handful of very compelling reasons to let your body go au naturel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: If this whole thing is giving you the heebie-jeebies as you recall the last time you were cheek by jowl with those who evidently already skimp on the showers Ð and have clearly not reached the scent-free stage yet Ð relax.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: Many cleansing- reduction enthusiasts still use deodorant if they nd it necessary (everyoneÕs natural scent varies in intensity and can be affected by a host of factors including diet, hydration and exercise) and hand-washing with soap is still recommended as a vital way to reduce the spread of infectious diseases.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: You donÕt need to go cold turkey, as Hamblin did, but in between thrice-daily disinfecting sessions and giving up showers altogether there lies room for our familiar, simple advice.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: Reduce.
label: ignore

title: item_p0093
complex: Skip a few showers, put down the soap and let those lovely little bacteria ourish a little, would you?
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: Like veins carrying the lifeblood of a city, a subway system teems with billions of inhabitants: the bacteria of Swiss cheese and kimchi, of bubonic plague and drug-proof bugs and of human skin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: Now, for the rst time, scientists have started to catalogue and map the bacteria coursing through a cityÕs subway Ð and they have found a wealth of curious results.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: Dr Christopher Mason, a geneticist at Weill Cornell Medical College, led a team that, for 18 months, swabbed the New York City subway system for the microscopic life forms that cover its turnstiles, seats, ticket booths and stations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: In what Mason called Òthe rst city-scale genetic pro le everÓ, his team found meningitis at Times Square, a trace of anthrax on the handhold of a train carriage and bacteria that cause bubonic plague on a rubbish bin and ticket machine at stations in uptown Manhattan.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: In research published in the journal Cell Systems, the team strongly downplayed the ndings of plague and anthrax, noting the extremely small trace of the latter, that rats likely carried the former and that no one has fallen ill with plague in or around New York for years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: ÒThe results do not suggest that plague or anthrax is prevalent,Ó the study says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: ÒNor do they suggest that New York residents are at risk.Ó In fact, most of the bacteria identi ed by the team are either harmless to humans or bene cial in the cityÕs thriving world of microorganisms, many of which process toxic hazards and waste in the same way that bacteria inside every human help with digestion and bodily functions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: Some of the results were expected, Mason said, including some bacteria associated with fecal matter, which he said Òshould be a gentle reminder for people to wash their handsÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: He also said that many bacteria of the same genus as those Òthat are bene cial and helpful, like the one used for making cheese,Ó also turned up around New York.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: Bacteria appeared to re ect the eating habits of various neighbourhoods.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: All around the subway, bacteria associated with cheeses Ð brie, cheddar, parmesan and the mozzarella of ubiquitous New York pizza Ð turned up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: The distinctive bacteria of Swiss cheese were more localized to midtown Manhattan and the nancial district, and the bacteria used to ferment cabbage for kimchi and sauerkraut showed up in the nancial district and Bay Ridge.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: The computer also identi ed cucumber DNA all over the city, Mason said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: Bacteria associated with illness and infections were extremely common.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: Species that cause diarrhoea and nausea, both benign and bad E.coli (mostly benign), and the bacteria that can cause skin infections and urinary-tract infections were common all over the city.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: The species that produces tetanus appeared in Soho and bacteria that cause dysentery appeared at a station in the Bronx and another in Harlem.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: With more than 1,000 samples collected at all of New YorkÕs 466 open subway stations, Mason and his team ran the organic materials through a DNA sequencer and, then, through a supercomputer armed with genetic databases.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: They identi ed 15,152 distinct species, nearly half of which were bacteria.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: The good news, the researchers wrote, is that these Òpotentially infectious agentsÓ are not spreading sickness or disease throughout New York but rather seem to be Ònormal co-habitantsÓ and Òmay even be essentialÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: They Òrepresent a normal, ÔhealthyÕ metagenome pro le of a cityÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: In short, the researchers conclude, the subway and city are about as safe as everyone thought.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: Mason said people should not be concerned about getting urinary-tract infections from subway seats.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: ÒYou should wash your hands,Ó he said, Òand probably get some sleep and eat salads and go to the gym, and thatÕs about the same today as it was yesterday.Ó ÒIf anything,Ó he added, ÒIÕve become much more con dent riding the subway.Ó Many ndings made sense: heavily traf cked stations like Grand Central and Times Square had more bacteria and more diversity among them; the subway was most enriched for bacteria associated with skin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: The Bronx, with its diverse neighbourhoods and stations, had the greatest diversity of bacteria; Staten Island, with just three stops, had the lowest.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: The researchers found marine bacteria at South Ferry, a station that ooded during Hurricane Sandy Ð but they were surprised to note the species included some normally associated with Antarctica and fish.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: The next steps, Mason said, are studies of other cities, which have begun in Paris, Sa_o Paolo and Shanghai, and continued studies of New York, for instance to see how the microbiome changes with the seasons.
label: ignore

title: item_p0094
complex: He said he hoped the research would provide Òa baselineÓ of research for health of cials and geneticists, and could help health of cials to be better prepared to prevent and track diseases and pathogens.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Maria is sitting on a black plastic chair in a community centre on a cold Tuesday afternoon waiting for her number to be called.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: She is number 34.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: When itÕs her turn, Maria is called forward to pick up a brown paper bag lled with essentials including pasta, eggs and corn akes, and is invited to choose between butternut squash or carrots as this weekÕs vegetables.

complex: Maria is the 34th ÒclientÓ so far today at East Hampton Food Pantry, a community initiative set up just streets away from some of the most expensive and exclusive properties in the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: By the end of the day, the food pantryÕs organizers expect more than 400 families to have followed Maria through the doors to collect their weekly food parcel to help them get through the cold, dark Long Island winter.

title: item_p0095
complex: In the summertime, the Hamptons, a collection of historic oceanfront towns and villages 100 miles from Manhattan, is a billionairesÕ playground.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: But, come Labor Day in early September, when the rich and famous shut up their mansions and head back to Manhattan or Beverly Hills, the glitz gives way to the gritty reality of life for the mostly immigrant community who live here all year.

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒThe people who come here are rich and famous but we who live here are not,Ó says Maria, who works 14-hour days in the summer cleaning mansions but goes months without any work at all in the winter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Maria laughs when asked if she has enough money.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒThere is no work in the winter, only in the summertime,Ó says Maria, who, like many of the workers in the Hamptons, is from Latin America.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒHere, lots of people live in a single room because they canÕt pay the rent.Ó She says some families with up to ve children are crammed into basements and still pay more than $1,000 a month in rent.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒPeople come here looking for work but, in the winter, there is nothing.Ó Lots of her friends canÕt pay for heating or medication and many would go hungry if it were not for the East Hampton Food Pantry, she says, which is just one of several food pantries in the town.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Vicki Littman, chairperson of the East Hampton Food Pantry, which provided more than 31,000 food parcels in 2015, says the number of people seeking out the food pantry is ever increasing.

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒOnce Labor Day comes and the season is over and peopleÕs hours start to be cut back, our numbers go up to about 400 families a week,Ó she says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒWhen they come to us on Tuesday, they get two to three daysÕ worth of food.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Without us, they would struggle that much more."
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Littman says it can be hard for outsiders to realize that there are people struggling to get by in a place known the world over for its excess.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒWhen I discuss with the summer community that comes out here about the food pantries, theyÕre always shocked because there is that glamorous side of the Hamptons where there are galas and the beaches and the mansions that are here.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒBut, what people donÕt realize is that there is that service industry.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ItÕs the landscapers, the nannies, the waitresses Ð they are all relying on that summer income to get them through the winter but people donÕt see that when theyÕre coming out on holiday.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒThere are seniors who have to sometimes pick between whether they are going to pay for their medications or pay their bills or buy food, and that shouldnÕt be the case.Ó Littman says the town has lost too many people working key jobs Ð such as teachers, police of cers and even doctors and dentists Ð because they canÕt afford to live in the community and the food pantry board is determined to do more to ensure people have a better shot at staying put.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Housing is, by far, the biggest cost in the Hamptons.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: At $147m, the nationÕs most expensive property is hedge fund manager Barry RosensteinÕs 18-acre beachfront estate at 67 Further Lane, a stoneÕs throw from Maidstone Golf Club, which is considered Òthe most elite, prestigious and dif cult to get intoÓ of all the Hamptons clubs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Larry Cantwell, East HamptonÕs town supervisor and lifelong resident, says homes regularly change hands at more than $25m and the rapid price in ation at the top end has trickled down to even the townÕs most modest ats.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒFinding your rst home is a challenge in an area like this,Ó Cantwell says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒNot just people who you would characterize as poor Ð working- and middle-class families are also having a hard time.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: If you can nd a home to buy anywhere in East Hampton for $500,000, youÕre very lucky.Ó Cantwell says more than half the townÕs homes are empty for most of the year, which causes the population to dwindle to as little as 10,000 in the winter months compared with 80,000 in August.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒItÕs kind of the tale of two cities.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ThereÕs certainly a lot of wealth here but almost all of that wealth is in second homes only used in the summer,Ó says Cantwell, the son of a sherman father and a house-cleaner mother.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒBut, the rest of us live here year round.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒThere are famous and very wealthy people but then you have hard-working and poor people struggling to get by.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: YouÕve got to remember that this community was founded as a farming and shing community of people who lived off the land and the water Ð a real working-class community.Ó Cantwell says saving up enough money to buy your rst house while working as a farmer or sherman is near-impossible in East Hampton today Òand itÕs not just the poor Ð police of cers, teachers, young professionals and others all struggle to nd a place to live here and many of them cannot afford to own their own home.Ó Being homeless in the Hamptons means spending a lot of time on a bus.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Various houses of worship have joined together to ensure there is somewhere for the homeless to spend the night over the winter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Churches up and down the north and south fork of Long Island take on the burden one night at a time and roughly 50 homeless people are bussed between them every day.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: Eddie Vallone, 22, is one of those on the bus every night.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒPeople look at the Hamptons as some sort of rich town and thereÕs no problems going on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: But there are a lot of problems here, especially drugs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: "ItÕs hard to really grasp Ð ÔOK, the summer is coming to an end.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: What am I going to do for the winter?ÕÓ Vallone says at MaureenÕs Haven, a charity that coordinates the homeless shelter programme.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒI want to work but thereÕs no work to be done.Ó Vallone, who works cleaning pools and doing odd jobs on luxury estates, says that, if he saves well and doesnÕt impulse-buy, he can make his summer earnings stretch out until November.
label: ignore

title: item_p0095
complex: ÒBut, work doesnÕt start again until May or the beginning of June.Ó

title: item_p0096
complex: That millennials rely heavily on technology is no secret.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: More than eight in ten say they sleep with a mobile phone by their bed, almost two thirds admit to texting while driving, one in ve has posted a video of themselves online and three quarters have created a pro le on a social networking site.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: Compared to other generations, millennials are the most active on social media, according to a 2010 report, with 75% of them having created at least one social media account.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: In contrast, only 50% of Generation X, 30% of baby boomers and 6% of those aged 65 and older use social media.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: But there is a small percentage of millennials who donÕt use social media at all.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: Meet the millennials bucking the trend.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: Celan Beausoleil, 31, Oakland, California Beausoleil is a social worker and has had an Òon and off, more off than onÓ relationship with Facebook.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: She last deactivated her account in December 2015 after nding the amount of personal information shared by others Òtoo heavyÓ to deal with on top of her work demands.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: ÒA lot of my job is listening to peopleÕs lives all day, every day and it started to feel so overwhelming to go on social media and see every single detail of everybodyÕs life, including people that I donÕt really have a relationship with,Ó she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: ÒIt feels almost like intimacy overload.Ó She added: ÒIÕm holding a lot in my work life for people and sometimes it felt like it was too heavy to do in my personal life also.Ó But Beausoleil does love the way social media connects the world in a truly unique way, citing it as one of her only reasons for staying on Facebook for as long as she did.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: ÒOne thing I really liked about Facebook was that I could sit for hours and click on a friend and then click on one of their friends and one of their friends and one of their friends and literally end up on someoneÕs Facebook page from the other side of the world,Ó she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: ÒI used to do that all the time.Ó Jason Mathias, 26, Baltimore, Maryland ÒOne day, I realized IÕm spending so much time doing this.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: These little seconds add up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: I wonder what it would be like if I didnÕt spend these seconds here and spent them doing something else.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: What if I was doing other things with these seconds?
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: What would they become?
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: Would I enjoy it?Ó Mathias, who works for the Baltimore City government, had Facebook and Twitter accounts for years before deleting them both in November 2012.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: But he Òquickly forgot that Facebook existedÓ after his impromptu decision to end his social media presence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: He can still appreciate the bene ts that come with having social media accounts, like when he met his girlfriendÕs friends for the rst time and realized Òhumanizing 20 people youÕre meeting at a partyÓ is much easier if you can connect their faces, hometowns and jobs to a photo later on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: Or how easy it is to organize large events online.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: Mathias relies on friends for party invites and is sure there are times he Òslips through the cracksÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: But, now, he relishes the time thatÕs freed up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: He spends his lift rides and spare moments at work reading news articles and books rather than scrolling through a newsfeed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: And with no friendsÕ accounts to follow online, he has to Òpick up the phone and call themÓ, something heÕs come to Òde nitely enjoyÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: Lauren Raskauskas, 22, Naples, Florida Raskauskas describes herself as a Òpretty privateÓ person.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: So social media, which can open you up to the scrutiny and analysis of others, is not that appealing to her.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: ÒIÕm more privacy-minded and have concerns about giving out my data,Ó said Raskauskas, who is currently looking for a job.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: She recently deleted her Twitter account and deactivated her Facebook account two years ago after realizing she ÒdidnÕt like everyone knowing what I was doingÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: But Raskauskas, who was late to the Facebook game because her Òparents were really strict with technologyÓ, can see the positive sides of social media.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: When a friend of hers that sheÕd lost track of moved to Naples for a month, Raskauskas didnÕt even realize she was there until after sheÕd left, which the 22-year-old said Òwas a bummerÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: But in the end, her privacy concerns outweighed any bene ts social media could provide and she saw a de nite upside when she went through a recent break-up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: The last time a relationship of hers ended and she was online, it was not pleasant.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: ÒOne time, I did break up with somebody while I was on Facebook and I was like ÔOh my gosh, should I change my pro le photo?
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: Should I change my status?Õ And, this time, I donÕt have to worry about any of that because that kind of stuff is pretty hard,Ó she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: Hariharan Rajagopalan, 18, Boston, Massachusetts Rajagopalan, a student at Boston College, doesnÕt see any drawbacks to abstaining from social media.

title: item_p0096
complex: He claims that he ÒhasnÕt seen any effect at this pointÓ.

title: item_p0096
complex: Even though classmates post about parties and events on Facebook, they make sure to send him a text message, too, he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: ÒSince I was young, I was always a step behind on that kind of thing so it never really mattered to me,Ó he said.

title: item_p0096
complex: In fact, the only time Rajagopalan made use of social media was when it was unavoidable: it was the only way to reach his new roommate at college.

title: item_p0096
complex: Before starting his rst year at college, he signed up for his rst, and only, social media account.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: He joined Facebook in order to contact his future roommate and talk about their plans for that year.

title: item_p0096
complex: Months later, he still has the account but he admits: ÒI donÕt use it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0096
complex: I donÕt check it or anything like that.Ó The most activity it sees is when his two sisters tag him in family photos.

title: item_p0096
complex: He has avoided social media accounts in all other situations, though he has felt the draw of Twitter.

title: item_p0096
complex: As a sports fan, he acknowledged that ÒitÕs where most of the news breaks outÓ.

title: item_p0097
complex: Some cities have pigeons.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Lima has black vultures or gallinazos.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: They circle in groups overhead and perch on the city’s most emblematic buildings – the decrepit, colonial-era churches and crumbling eighteenth-century piles in the city centre.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: In many ways, with their wrinkly heads and beady eyes, they remind Lima residents of the side of their city they would rather ignore: the neglect, poverty and filth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: But these carrion-eaters’ natural affinity for dead and decaying things is being turned into a virtue.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Environmental authorities are giving these muchmaligned birds a PR makeover, kitting them out with GoPro action video cameras and GPS trackers, and giving them a new mission in the fight against fly-tipping and illegal dumping.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Samuel is one of the project’s ten certified diseasefree Coragyps atratus that have been charged with doing what they do best: sniffing out rubbish.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Fitted with his tracker, he is set free above the city, where he identifies clandestine dumps and records the GPS coordinates on a live map.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: His trainer at Lima’s Huachipa Zoo, Alfredo Correa, beams with admiration.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: “They can eat dead animals because their metabolism protects them from viruses and bacteria,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: “They’ve got some of the strongest gut flora in the natural world.” The effort is a collaboration between USAID and the Peruvian Environment Ministry to tackle Lima’s rubbish problem.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Samuel’s other airborne companions have been given more evocative names: Capitan Huggin, Capitan Fenix (named after the mythological creature that rises from the ashes) and Capitana Aella (“Whirlwind”).
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: A tonguein- cheek video adds a melodramatic voiceover, in which the noble “carthatidae lineage – the vultures” are pitted against pestilence and disease, while “humanity is placidly ignoring the danger”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: The project makes a serious point.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: With just four landfills in a city of nearly ten million inhabitants, there are countless illegal dumps.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: A fifth of the rubbish ends up there, according to the Environment Ministry.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Run-off from the waste contaminates Lima’s main water source, the Rimac river, as well as the Chillon and Lurin rivers, which flow into the Bay of Lima.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: The environmental supervision agency, OEFA, says that three poorer neighbourhoods, despite having only 12% of Lima’s population, have by far the most fly-tipped rubbish: Villa Maria del Triunfo (39.4%), Villa El Salvador (25.3%) and El Agustino (18.3%).
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: The problem, in part, is unpaid taxes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Many residents, especially in the barrios, just don’t pay.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: That means some of the 43 district municipalities lack the resources for basic services such as rubbish collection.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: It also means nobody is necessarily going to clean up where the vultures identify illegal rubbish.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: “We share the vulture’s GPS coordinates with the municipalities,” says Javier Hernandez, the project director.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: “It’s their job to collect the rubbish and to try and change the habits of their residents.” The project aims to encourage citizens to be “vultures on the ground”: to report fly-tipping, cut back on their own waste and recycle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Some residents are responding, posting photos of illicit dumps on the Twitter feed and Facebook page.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: The idea was hatched at the 2014 United Nations Climate Change Conference, hosted in Lima.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: “We were looking for ways to involve not just the authorities but also citizens to generate environmental awareness,” Hernandez says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Artist Cristina Planas had placed huge, sculpted vulture heads on top of 25 dead palm trees in a wetland reserve in the south of the city.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: Rich residents hated it as an ugly imposition but Planas invited citizens to “adopt a vulture” in support of conservation and recycling.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: “We are a little scared of the vulture’s appearance,” she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: “But in reality, he is out there sacrificing himself for us.
label: ignore

title: item_p0097
complex: He is out there recycling, in the last place we threw out our rubbish.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: Tigers are more numerous in Nepal than at any time since the 1970s, a new census has revealed, giving conservationists hope that the big cats, whose numbers have been dropping across south Asia for 100 years, can be saved.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: The number of wild royal bengal tigers in Nepal has increased to 198 – a 63.6% rise in five years – the government survey showed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: “This is very encouraging,” said Maheshwar Dhakal, an ecologist with Nepal’s Department of National Parks and Wildlife Conservation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: The census is based on the examination of pictures from more than 500 cameras placed in five protected areas and three wildlife corridors.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: More than 250 conservationists and wildlife experts worked on the survey, which cost about £250,000.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: Dhakal said that a parallel survey was conducted in India and the results from both countries will be published later in 2013.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: “It will take a few more months for India, which now has 1,300 big cats in several huge protected areas, to finalize the data,” he added.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: Nepal has pledged to double the population of tigers by the year 2022 from 121 in 2009 when the last systematic tiger count took place.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: Increasing prosperity in Asia has pushed up prices for tiger skins and the body parts used in traditional Chinese medicines.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: International gangs pay poor local Nepali significant sums to kill the cats.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: The skin and bones are handed to middlemen, who pass easily through the porous border to India, where the major dealers are based.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: One major problem is complicity between senior officials and mafia networks involved in the trade.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: Conservation experts credit the increase in numbers to the effective policing of national parks, stronger anti-poaching drives and better management of tiger habitats in Nepal, where forests cover 29% of the land.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: But they say Nepal needs to do more to protect the habitat and animals on which tigers prey so the big cats have enough space to roam and food to eat.

title: item_p0098
complex: As the number of tigers has increased over the years, so have incidents of conflict with villagers.

title: item_p0098
complex: Seven people were killed in attacks by tigers around national parks in 2012 compared to four in 2011, park officials said.

title: item_p0098
complex: Villagers are also seeking better protection.

title: item_p0098
complex: “The government is making conservation plans for tigers.

complex: Wildlife authorities captured a tiger in Chitwan after it killed two people, including a villager who was pulled from his bed in May.

title: item_p0098
complex: Thousands of tigers once roamed the forests in Bangladesh, India and Nepal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: But their numbers have dropped to about 3,000, a 95% drop over a century.

title: item_p0098
complex: Chitwan National Park in central Nepal has the highest number of adult tigers, with 120, followed by Bardiya National Park (50) and Shukla Phanta Wildlife Reserve (17).
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: Diwakar Chapagain, who heads a World Wildlife Fund Nepal unit that monitors wildlife trade, said tiger skins were in demand in Tibet, where well-heeled people use them as festival costumes.

title: item_p0098
complex: In Nepal, kings used to stand on tiger skins in front of stuffed tigers for special occasions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: Some affluent Nepali have mounted tiger heads on the walls of their living rooms.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: Tiger bones are in high demand for use in traditional Chinese medicines.
label: ignore

title: item_p0098
complex: “The trade in tiger parts is lucrative and fetches thousands of dollars in illegal markets,” Chapagain said, highlighting the threat tigers face.

title: item_p0099
complex: After being told for the umpteenth time that the beer she wanted would be Òtoo dark and too strong for you, love Ð have something sweeterÓ, Rebecka Singerer had had enough.

title: item_p0099

complex: Women can drink whatever we want,Ó she says.

title: item_p0099
complex: Now Singerer, a childminder, has joined FemAle, a group of like-minded drinkers in Gothenburg, to launch SwedenÕs rst beer made by women.

title: item_p0099
complex: We Can Do It, a bottled pale ale, has just gone on sale in stores across Sweden.

title: item_p0099
complex: Its label is a take on Rosie the Riveter, the creation of a US Second World War propaganda campaign that went on to become a symbol of womenÕs power in the workplace.

title: item_p0099

title: item_p0099
complex: ÒItÕs nothing to do with feminism; itÕs about equality Ð we wanted to show we can do it.Ó FemAle is up against decades of prejudice in the beer world.

title: item_p0099
complex: In an irony not lost on FemAle, Carlsberg and other big brewers have spent millions in recent years trying to sell beer to women, attempting to pink it and shrink it to appeal to perceived feminine tastes.

title: item_p0099
complex: Carlsbergs Eve and Copenhagen offerings, Fosters Radler and Coorss Animee were among lighter, avoured and even bloat-resistant beers that failed to nd a market.

complex: FemAleÕs approach is different, with women- only tastings that allow potential customers to experiment with avours and styles of beer that they may not normally try.

title: item_p0099
complex: This education process is the way to Òget more girls into the beer worldÓ, the group says.

complex: We Can Do It was the brainchild of Felicia Nordstro_m, a bar worker who says she was fed up with bearded beer snobs telling her: ÒWhat do you know about beer, sweetie?Ó She approached FemAle and they teamed up with Ocean, a local independent micro-brewery.

complex: ÒThis is not a beer that is aimed at women Ð itÕs our hoppiest brew,Ó says Thomas Bingebo, the head brewer at Ocean.

complex: ÒGuinness tastes like water to me now.

complex: The standard stor stark (large strong) lager is now Òalmost extinctÓ in Gothenburg, the women say, as pubs and bars replace the big brands with a choice of specialist beers.

title: item_p0099
complex: ÒAll the girls are different Ð there is no typical woman beer-lover.

complex: Anyone can do it,Ó says Emma Henriksson, 22, a group member who works in a garden equipment company.

complex: ÒAnd Elin has found the way.
