title: item_p0100
complex: The Virunga National Park, home to rare mountain gorillas but targeted for oil exploration by a British company, could earn trouble-torn DR Congo $400m a year from tourism, hydropower and carbon credits, said a WWF report.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: But, if the UNESCO World Heritage Site that straddles the equator is exploited for oil, as the Congolese government and exploration firm SOCO International are hoping, it could lead to devastating pollution and permanent conflict in an already unstable region, says the conservation body.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: SOCO International is the only company seeking to explore inside the boundaries of the Virunga park.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: SOCO insist that their operations in Congo would be confined to an area in the park known as Block V, and would not affect the gorillas.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: SOCO Chairman Rui de Sousa said: “Despite the views of WWF, SOCO is extremely sensitive to the environmental significance of the Virunga National Park.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: It is irrefutable that oil companies still have a central role in today’s global energy supply and a successful oil project has the potential to transform the economic and social well-being of a whole country.’’ He added: “The park has sadly been in decline for many years, officially falling below the standards required for a World Heritage Site.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: The potential for development just might be the catalyst that reverses this trend.’’ However, Raymond Lumbuenamo, country director for WWF Democratic Republic of the Congo, based in Kinshassa, said that security in and around the park would deteriorate further if SOCO went ahead with its exploration plans.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “The security situation is already bad.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: The UN is involved with fighting units and the M23 rebel force is inside the park.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: Oil would be a curse.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: It always increases conflict.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: It would attract human sabotage.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: The park might become like the Niger Delta.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: Developing Virunga for oil will not make anything better.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “The population there is already very dense, with over 350 people per square kilometre.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: When you take part of the land (for oil), you put more pressure on the rest.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: Oil would not provide many jobs; people would flood in looking for work,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: One fear is that the area is seismically active and another eruption of one of the volcanoes in the park could damage oil company infrastructure and lead to oil spills in the lakes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “Virunga’s rich natural resources are for the benefit of the Congolese people, not for foreign oil prospectors to drain away.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: Our country’s future depends on sustainable economic development,” said Lumbuenamo.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “For me, choosing the conservation option is the best option.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: Once you have started drilling for oil, there’s no turning back,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: But Lumbuenamo accepted that, while the gorillas were safe at present, the chances of the park generating its potential of $400m a year were remote.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “It would be difficult to make the kind of money that the report talks of.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: Virunga used to be a very peaceful place and can be again.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: The security situation right now is bad.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: The UN is involved with fighting units.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: It’s not as quiet as it used to be.” According to the WWF report, ecosystems in the park could support hydropower generation, fishing and ecotourism, and play an important role in providing secure water supplies, regulating climate and preventing soil erosion.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: The park, Africa’s oldest and most diverse, is home to over 3,000 different kinds of animals, but is now heavily populated with desperately poor people, many of whom fled there after the Rwanda massacre in 1994.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “In all, the park could support in the region of 45,000 permanent jobs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: In addition, people around the world could get an immense value from simply knowing that the park is well managed and is safe for future generations,” says the report.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “Virunga represents a valuable asset to DR Congo and contributes to Africa’s heritage as the oldest and most biodiverse park on the continent,” the report continues.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “Plans to explore for oil and exploit oil reserves put Virunga’s potential value at risk,” it says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “This is where we draw the line.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: Oil companies are standing on the doorstep of one of the world’s most precious and fragile places, but we will not rest until Virunga is safe from this potential environmental disaster,” said Lasse Gustavsson, executive director of WWF International.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: “Virunga has snow fields and lava fields, but it should not have oil fields.” The UNESCO World Heritage Committee called for the cancellation of all Virunga oil permits and appealed to concession holders Total SA and SOCO International plc not to undertake exploration in World Heritage Sites.
label: ignore

title: item_p0100
complex: Total has committed to respecting Virunga’s current boundary, leaving UK-based SOCO as the only oil company with plans to explore inside the park.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: The inventor of a state-of-the-art computer-assisted autopsy system that is increasingly being used in European hospitals has claimed the technique could eventually mean there is no such thing as a ‘perfect murder’.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: The method, called ‘Virtopsy’, is now being used at selected forensic medical institutes in Europe, having been pioneered by a group of scientists at the University of Zurich.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: Instead of reaching for the scalpel and making the Y-shaped incision in the chest with which a traditional autopsy begins, pathologists are now able to examine the corpse in 3-D via computer screens.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: Michael Thali, the Director of Zurich’s Institute for Forensic Medicine in Europe, and one of the inventors of Virtopsy, said it had the potential to revolutionize criminal investigations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: “Basically there will be no such thing as the perfect murder any more as a virtual autopsy allows you to find every piece of evidence,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: Virtopsies combine the images from high-powered magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT) and surface scans of dead bodies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: Combined, the devices are referred to as a ‘Virtobot’.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: The technique allows the detection of injuries such as lesions and blows often undetectable during a traditional autopsy, as well as air pockets, heart attacks and even cancer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: “The Virtopsy has the potential to replace the autopsy one day,” Richard Dirndorfer, one of the pioneers of DNA analysis in criminology, and a founder developer of Virtopsy, told the German science magazine PM.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: “I think we’ll see it happen gradually, just like DNA analysis gradually replaced blood group analysis,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: The computer imaging techniques allow doctors to gain in-depth insights into the deepest interiors of dead bodies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: The method has already allowed the discovery of haemorrhages and fractures that were not picked up during conventional autopsies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: The initial aim is to use the new method to complement the traditional autopsy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: “It will enable forensic scientists to plan their autopsies far more efficiently,” Dominic Wichmann, an internal medicine specialist at Hamburg’s University Hospital, told Spiegel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: Criminologists from around the world have been travelling to Switzerland over the past few years to see the development of the new method for themselves.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: The US forensic medical drama CSI has already twice featured Virtopsies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: In one, the system was able to prove that a murder victim was killed by a bullet through the cheek; in another, a Virtopsy on a murdered man meant his wish to be cryogenically frozen could still go ahead as his body remained intact.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: The method has been under development for decades, with the scientists behind it first housed in an unheated laboratory on a university campus where they were considered a bit of a laughing stock.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: Later, a donation from a rich ophthalmologist enabled the project to take off.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: Even then it was initially rejected for its potential to undermine the traditional skills of forensic scientists and pathologists.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: But a new generation appears to be seeing it as less of a threat and rather as something that will complement and substantiate conventional methods and possibly even one day replace them, though probably not entirely.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: “In order to analyze the colour of the blood, the consistencies (of body fluids) or smells, we’ll need to keep on with the conventional cut,” said Lars Oesterhelweg, Deputy Director of the Institute of Forensic Medicine at the Charité Hospital in Berlin, which is using a version of the Virtopsy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: He added that the new method was particularly helpful in re-examining cases where the cause of death was unclear.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: “It means that third opinions can be gathered, investigations can be re-examined and cases can be reopened,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0101
complex: Scientists using the new method said that relatives of the dead, who are often reluctant for autopsies to be carried out because of the disfigurement they cause, were much keener on the non-invasive method.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: The roof is plastic held up by a crooked tree trunk and the desks just a jumble of cast-off chairs, but the students inside the Chemin des Dunes school are studying with the same intensity you would find in a seminar at Oxford University.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: At stake is the hope of a new life in France.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “The French language is very difficult but we try hard.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: If we come every day, maybe we can touch our dreams,” says Kamal, a refugee from Sudan’s war-torn Darfur district who comes to three or four hours of classes every day.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “It’s a good thing to keep your brain active.” The 29-year-old electrical engineer is one of dozens, perhaps hundreds, of refugees living in the “jungle” camp outside Calais who have applied for asylum in France and are eager to learn the language of what they hope will be their new home.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: Like many of his fellow students, he is frustrated that media coverage of the sprawling tentvillage has focused only on those who use it as a staging post for risky nightly bids to sneak on board cross-Channel lorries or trains.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “I need to tell people in the UK, they think that everyone wants to go there.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: But there are a lot of people here who want to stay in France,” Kamal said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: France is already home to more than a quarter of a million refugees, according to United Nations data – the country has taken in more than twice as many as the UK, even though the countries have similar populations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: There are a further 56,000 asylum seekers waiting for their claim to a French safe haven to be processed – the second highest number in Europe – while, in the UK, there are 36,000.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: While the applicants wait for an answer, though, France does not provide them with any financial support or allow them to work – and the slow process can take many months.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: The jungle camp offers a free meal a day and a plastic roof over their heads so many decide to endure the basic conditions for a few extra months, rather than potentially jeopardize their asylum bid by working illegally.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: The idea for the school was first touted by some of that group at the start of the summer, when they were bored with sitting around waiting and nervous about starting a new life in France totally unable to communicate.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: It was a reality within weeks, opening on 11 July.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “We did it to reunite the ‘brothers’ and, at the same time, they can learn French,” said Zimarco Jones, the school’s Nigerian founder, who arrived in Calais in 2013 and is still waiting for his asylum claim to be processed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “Now, we need to build another one,” he says with a grin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: At its busiest, the tiny classroom holds 30 pupils, crammed into five rows of desks in front of a big green chalkboard and pictures of cartoon animals for each letter of the French alphabet.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: There are also classes in English, art and t’ai chi but the biggest draw is the French lessons, provided by volunteers from Calais and beyond.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “French is not as easy as English but, two weeks ago, I decided there was no way to get to the UK,” says George, another Darfur refugee and dedicated student.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: He wanted to cross the Channel because he speaks fluent English but, with language classes, he says he is happy to settle in France.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “Anywhere there is peace, I can stay, no problem,” he says, already waiting at the classroom more than half an hour before his teachers arrive.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: He admits he doesn’t know much about France but says that the classes are slowly helping him understand the country as well as the language.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: Many of the volunteers at the jungle school are local teachers who are giving up their summer holidays.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: Jenny Flahaut, 33, who works at a children’s home, was inspired to volunteer after seeing an advertisement on Facebook.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “I saw these people in Calais every day and I wanted to do something for them,” she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: She is particularly frustrated by the depiction of migrants in the media and by politicians who have never visited the camp, most recently David Cameron, the British prime minister, who in a much criticized speech talked about “swarms” of people trying to reach the UK.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “They don’t know them and have a bad vision but they are not like that,” Flahaut said as she prepared for an afternoon lesson.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “Most of them are very good people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: They are welcoming and friendly.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: They want to improve their life and make it better, and learning is part of that.” The teachers and Zimarco are focused, now, on setting up a separate classroom to serve around 200 women and two dozen children.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: The women are outnumbered around ten to one by men in the Calais encampment and most feel uncomfortable attending classes with male students they don’t know, the volunteers say.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: When that is finished, the former hotel worker whose work getting the school off the ground is remembered in its unofficial name – everyone in the camp just calls it “Zimarco’s school” – has more dreams for making the camp a place to live, not just survive.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: He wants to set up a football team for migrants, he explains over an instant cappuccino in the immaculate tent he calls home, and even dreams of changing the camp name.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: He hates “the jungle” because he says it implies the residents aren’t people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “We have a discotheque, a house, a mosque, a school, shops,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0102
complex: “We are not animals.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: A few months before he died, Carl Sagan recorded a message of hope to would-be Mars explorers, telling them: “Whatever the reason you’re on Mars is, I’m glad you’re there.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: And I wish I was with you.” Seventeen years after the pioneering astronomer set out his hopeful vision of the future in 1996, a company from the Netherlands is proposing to turn Sagan’s dreams of reaching Mars into reality.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: The company, Mars One, plans to send four astronauts on a trip to the Red Planet to set up a human colony in 2023.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: But there are a couple of serious snags.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: Firstly, when on Mars their bodies will have to adapt to surface gravity that is 38% of that on Earth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: It is thought that this would cause such a total physiological change in their bone density, muscle strength and circulation that voyagers would no longer be able to survive in Earth’s conditions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: Secondly, and directly related to the first, they will have to say goodbye to all their family and friends, as the deal doesn’t include a return ticket.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: The Mars One website states that a return “cannot be anticipated nor expected”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: To return, they would need a fully assembled and fuelled rocket capable of escaping the gravitational field of Mars, on-board life support systems capable of up to a seven-month voyage and the capacity either to dock with a space station orbiting Earth or perform a safe re-entry and landing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: “Not one of these is a small endeavour,” the site notes, requiring “substantial technical capacity, weight and cost”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: Nevertheless, the project has already had 10,000 applicants, according to the company’s Medical Director, Norbert Kraft.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: He told The Guardian that the applicants so far ranged in age from 18 to at least 62 and, though they include women, they tended to be men.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: The reasons they gave for wanting to go were varied, he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: An American woman called Cynthia, who gave her age as 32, told the company that it was a “childhood imagining” of hers to go to Mars.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: She described a trip her mother had taken her on in the early 1990s to a lecture at the University of Wisconsin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: She said the lecturer had been Sagan and she had asked him if he thought humans would land on Mars in her lifetime.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: Cynthia said: “He in turn asked me if I wanted to be trapped in a ‘tin can spacecraft’ for the two years it would take to get there.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: I told him ‘yes’, he smiled, and told me in all seriousness, that yes, he absolutely believed that humans would reach Mars in my lifetime.” She told the project: “When I first heard about the Mars One project I thought, this is my chance – that childhood dream could become a reality.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: I could be one of the pioneers, building the first settlement on Mars and teaching people back home that there are still uncharted territories that humans can reach for.” The prime attributes Mars One is looking for in astronaut-settlers are resilience, adaptability, curiosity, ability to trust and resourcefulness, according to Kraft.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: They must also be over 18.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: Founded in 2010 by Bas Lansdorp, an engineer, Mars One says it has developed a realistic road map and financing plan for the project based on existing technologies and that the mission is perfectly feasible.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: The website states that the basic elements required for life are already present on the planet.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: For instance, water can be extracted from ice in the soil and Mars has sources of nitrogen, the primary element in the air we breathe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: The colony will be powered by specially adapted solar panels, it says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: Mars One said it had signed a contract with the American firm Paragon Space Development Corporation to take the first steps in developing the life support system and spacesuits fit for the mission.
label: ignore

title: item_p0103
complex: The project will cost a reported $6bn, a sum Lansdorp has said he hopes will be met partly by selling broadcasting rights.

title: item_p0104
complex: When two islanders spotted a small fibreglass boat washed up on a remote Pacific atoll, they decided to take a closer look.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: What they found inside was a tale of adventure and unlikely survival to rival the blockbuster book and film Life of Pi: an emaciated man with long hair and a beard, who claimed to have been drifting for 16 months after setting out from Mexico, more than 12,500km away.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: The man, dressed only in a ragged pair of underpants, told his rescuers that he had been adrift in the 7.3-metre fibreglass boat, whose engines were missing their propellers, since he left Mexico for El Salvador in September 2012.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: A companion had died at sea several months earlier, he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: “His condition isn’t good, but he’s getting better,” said Ola Fjeldstad, a Norwegian anthropology student doing research on the isolated Ebon Atoll, part of the Marshall Islands archipelago.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: The man had said his name was José Ivan and he had indicated that he survived by catching turtles and birds with his bare hands, but, because he spoke only Spanish, further details were sketchy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: There was no fishing equipment on the boat, but a turtle was inside when it washed up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: “The boat is really scratched up and looks like it has been in the water for a long time,” Fjeldstad told reporters.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: According to the researcher, the islanders who found the man took him to the main island in the atoll – which is so remote it has only one phone line and no internet – to meet the mayor, Ione de Brum, who contacted the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Majuro, the Marshall Islands capital.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: Officials at the ministry said that they were awaiting more details and expected the man to be taken to the capital.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: The government airline’s only plane that can land at Ebon is currently undergoing maintenance and is not expected to return to service for several days.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: Officials are considering sending a boat to pick up the castaway.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: “He’s staying at the local council house and a family is feeding him,” said Fjeldstad, who added that the man had a basic health check and was found to have low blood pressure, but did not appear to have any life-threatening conditions and was able to walk with the aid of men on the island.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: “We’ve been giving him a lot of water and he’s gaining strength.” Fraser Christian, who teaches maritime survival courses at his Coastal Survival School in Dorset, said the man’s story, if true, would be remarkable but far from unique.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: It was entirely possible to catch turtles or small fish by hand, he said, since “they are inquisitive and they will approach a small boat to shelter underneath it”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: Christian advises clients who find themselves forced to eat turtles to start with their eyes – “lots of fluid” – then move on to the blood.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: The major problems the man would have faced were exposure and dehydration.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: “The basic rule is: no water, no food.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: You need water to digest protein.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: If you have no fresh water and it doesn’t rain for a few days, so you can’t collect rainwater, you have basically had it.” Individual physiology also plays a part, he said, with some people better suited to survival than others.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: “The mental thing is key and that’s often down to people’s situation in life and how used they are to dealing mentally with hardship.” Stories of survival in the vast Pacific Ocean are not uncommon.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: In 2006, three Mexicans made international headlines when they were discovered drifting, also in a small fibreglass boat near the Marshall Islands.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: They claimed to have survived for nine months at sea on a diet of rainwater, raw fish and seabirds, with their hope kept alive by reading the Bible.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: But Cliff Downing, who teaches sea survival to sailors, said he was sceptical about the latest tale.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: “It just doesn’t sound right to me.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: There are 1,001 hazards that would make his survival for so long very unlikely.
label: ignore

title: item_p0104
complex: One would want to know a lot more.”

title: item_p0105
complex: An international agreement to improve safety in Bangladesh’s clothing factories is facing the threat of legal action as factory owners demand compensation for the cost of closures and repair work.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: With some repair programmes expected to take months, factory owners say they cannot shoulder the costs of paying staff while factories are closed, alongside the expense of some major works needed to ensure buildings are safe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: The building overhauls are being carried out in the wake of the collapse of the Rana Plaza complex in the capital of Bangladesh, Dhaka, in 2013, in which 1,138 people were killed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: The problems come as hundreds of Bangladeshi clothing factories per month are inspected for firesafety and structural problems under the Accord on Fire and Building Safety in Bangladesh, which is backed by over 170 international brands, including Primark and Marks & Spencer, and international trade unions, including IndustriALL.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: The owner of one Dhaka-based factory, Softex Cotton, has threatened legal action against the Accord after his factory was closed down due to structural problems, prompting a demand for a reported $100m in compensation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: Another factory owner, who declined to be named, said that once a factory closed its doors, even for a few months, it would lose orders and close permanently: “There is no such thing as temporary closure,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: The factory owner said that the Accord agreement had “pussyfooted” around the issue of who paid for factory closures amid efforts to get as many brands as possible to sign up to a deal in the wake of the Rana Plaza disaster.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: He said there was no clear process in place to handle the costs involved.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: Jenny Holdcroft, policy director for IndustriALL, which has been closely involved in the Accord, said that the agreement ensured that factories would not lose orders during closure because brands had committed to maintain orders with suppliers for two years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: While 12 factories have been identified so far by the Accord as needing significant work, Holdcroft said many of those only needed partial closure in order to reduce stress on the building so production could continue on other floors.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: The Accord also legally binds brands to ensure that workers are paid during factory closures.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: She said the detail on who would make payments had been left open in order to ensure that all those factory owners who could afford to pay for repairs and compensation for workers made the necessary contribution.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: “This was always going to be a topic of negotiation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: Brands don’t want to commit to paying so that rich factory owners who have just pocketed the profits and not been spending on their factories for years continue to do so.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: There was obviously going to be disruption.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: If there was no disruption, there would be no change,” she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0105
complex: A spokesman for the Accord said negotiations over payments and even legal action would not hold up its work to improve safety in factories.

complex: However, pressure on the Accord to contribute to the payment of displaced workers has ratcheted up after a rival factory-safety group backed by US retailers including Walmart and Gap, the Alliance for Bangladesh Worker Safety, set aside $5m to help pay factory workers for up to two months while work is carried out on the buildings it has identified as needing improvement.

title: item_p0106

title: item_p0108
complex: Music subscription services, including Spotify and Deezer, have broken through the $1bn sales barrier worldwide, as increasing numbers of fans choose to pay for music online.
label: ignore

title: item_p0108
complex: Streaming and subscription revenues rose by more than 50% in 2013 to reach $1.1bn, helping overall sales of recorded music in Europe grow for the rst time in 12 years, according to gures published in March 2014.
label: ignore

title: item_p0108
complex: There are now an estimated 450 music-rental services around the world, and, while many people still listen for free, a desire for more choice is persuading more music lovers to part with their cash.
label: ignore

title: item_p0108
complex: In a three-year period, the number of paying subscribers rose from 8 million to 28 million, according to the 2014 digital music report from the International Federation of the Phonographic Industry (IFPI).
label: ignore

title: item_p0108
complex: Easily accessible from smartphones and tablets, subscription services are popular with people looking to try out new music without committing to buying a download or a CD.
label: ignore

title: item_p0108
complex: Consumers say they are attracted by a cheap, user-friendly and legal alternative to pirated downloads.

title: item_p0108
complex: ÒIt is now clear that music streaming and subscription is a mainstream model for our business,Ó said IFPI Chief Executive, Frances Moore.

title: item_p0108

title: item_p0108
complex: Katy PerryÕs Prism was the best-selling album by a female artist, in sixth place behind Eminem, Justin Timberlake, Bruno Mars and Daft Punk.

title: item_p0108

title: item_p0108

title: item_p0108

title: item_p0109
complex: Why do it?
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Talking to the men earmarked to be the elite referees of the future, the question elicits a knowing smile.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: This season, criticism of referees has increased to the point where some former referees have felt compelled to complain about standards.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: That is quite striking because, the more you talk to referees, the more obvious it is that supporting each other through thick and thin is fundamental.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: So why do it?
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Why spend countless hours driving up and down the country to dole out rules, some of which are inevitably going to upset people, trying to climb the ladder until you get the chance to make decisions on television in front of millions who scrutinize you and your ability with the aid of umpteen different angles and slow-motion replays?
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: A glimpse of an answer appears on the face of Lee Swabey moments after he blows the final whistle of a 2–1 win for Grimsby over Woking, a match at level 5 of the English league system.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: He gets what all referees hope for every time they referee a match.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: “Twenty-two handshakes,” he explains afterwards, proudly.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Symbolically, a full set of handshakes, plus a “well done” from both managers, represents maximum satisfaction.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: “The buzz,” as he calls it, of a game that passes smoothly, is something he loves.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: “I wouldn’t spend so much time away from my family if this didn’t mean the world to me.” As one of the group that is highly regarded by the Professional Game Match Officials Limited (PGMOL) for his potential, Swabey knew he was being watched at that match.

title: item_p0109
complex: PGMOL’s chief, Mike Riley, was in attendance, along with Steve Dunn, who is the coach for this level, armed with notes and stopwatch to catalogue every significant move the officials make.

title: item_p0109

title: item_p0109
complex: “I hope to have the opportunity to get promoted to the Premier League and officiate some of the top games in this country,” Brooks says.

title: item_p0109
complex: It is all part of the experience Brooks needs to acquire before he is trusted with more high-profile games, the different problems that need dealing with – often, clubs are very reluctant to have a late postponement, particularly when they have to pay all the staff who have turned up but will not receive any gate money.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Brooks phoned his coach for advice and made the difficult but correct decision.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: A little later, the football club secretary arrived with envelopes to pay the officials for their time – the match fee at level 5 is £95 so it is safe to assume these men do not do it for the cash.

title: item_p0109

title: item_p0109
complex: He is under no illusions that developing a thick skin and perfecting strategies to deal with disappointments is a big part of that.

title: item_p0109

title: item_p0109
complex: “Erm … not great,” he admits.

label: ignore

complex: Your heart sinks.

title: item_p0109
complex: Riley, as a young referee, went out and bought himself books on psychology and nutrition as there was no information on offer to him at all.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: The former referees agree that the backup, education and tools that today’s referees have is a world away from what they experienced in their own days.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Riley, as a young referee, went out and bought himself books on psychology and nutrition as there was no information on offer to him at all.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Contrast this with Brooks, who has a coach at the end of the phone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: They consult weekly, discuss how his games have gone, study footage of key decisions and work out how to improve.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: He also has the support of a sports psychologist, Liam Slack, for regular guidance and an exercise regime to help him handle the 11km he runs during a game.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Brooks says psychology is vital in his development.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: “One of the things we have talked about is forgetting decisions and moving on,” he explains.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: “There may be a big decision to make in the first 30 seconds of the game.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Once you have made that, you need to stay focused for the next 89 minutes and not be wondering whether that was correct or worrying about that decision.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Liam has taught us some techniques for releasing that decision.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Working with the sports psychologist is really important for mental toughness.” Jones believes the whole approach can only help.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: “I refereed in professional football but, looking back, I was an amateur,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: “I was going to work – I worked for British Telecom – and I might referee at Newcastle on a Wednesday evening and, 9am the next morning, I was in Leicester trying to speak to customers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: I perhaps hadn’t slept.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Training was ad hoc.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: We were amateurs in a professional environment compared to now.” When the subject of technology comes up, the three former refs are unanimous in their support of it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: “We are all in favour of anything that makes the referee’s job better and makes them more effective on the field of play,” says Riley.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: Minimizing mistakes is the aim.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: After all, a bad decision can stick with you for a while.
label: ignore

title: item_p0109
complex: “The rest of your life,” notes Jones with a chuckle.
