title: item_p0110
complex: Our new international survey across 33 countries shows just how wrong the world is about a range of key social realities.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: British people think the top 1% wealthiest households own 59% of their countryÕs wealth, when they actually ÒonlyÓ own 23%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Americans think that 33% of their population are immigrants, when in fact it is only 14%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Brazilians think the average age in their country is 56, when it is only 31.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Russians think that 31% of their politicians are women, when it is only 14%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: In Britain, people think that an extraordinary 43% of young adults aged 25-34 still live at home with their parents, rather than the actual 14%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: In India, the online population think 60% of the whole country also has internet access, when in fact only 19% do.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Why are people across the world so often so clueless about these realities?
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: It is partly that we just struggle with basic maths and some of us clearly misunderstand the questions or interpret them differently.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: For example, most countries hugely overestimate how many people do not af liate themselves with a religion: across the 33 countries, 37% do not, according to respondents, but the average is actually just 18%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: This will be partly because we will be thinking of how many people practise their religion, rather than what they put on census forms.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: People also take mental shortcuts, where they grab for easily available information even if it doesnÕt quite t the question.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Our huge overestimates of the rural populations in most countries will be affected by how much of the physical landmass rural areas make up, rather than a careful calculation of how unoccupied it generally is.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: In Daniel KahnemanÕs terms, answers to these sorts of questions are classic examples of ÒfastÓ thinking, rather than ÒslowÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: We are tied to our own perspective and struggle to imagine the variety in our countries, as highlighted by our Indian sampleÕs massively overestimating their populationÕs access to the internet.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Our study was mostly carried out through an online survey Ð and, in developing countries, this will be representative of a more af uent, connected group rather than the population as a whole.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: In some ways, we may have expected this more educated sample to get closer to reality Ð those with higher education levels tend to be more accurate on these types of questions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: But what we nd, throughout the study, is that people grossly generalize from their own situations, forgetting how unrepresentative they are.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: We suffer from what social psychologists call Òemotional innumeracyÓ when estimating realities: this means we are sending a message about what is worrying us as much as trying to get the right answers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Cause and effect run both ways, with our concern leading to our misperceptions as much as our misperceptions creating our concern.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: For Britain, this is likely to be part of the explanation for peopleÕs huge overestimates of how much the wealthiest own, how many young people are still living at home and what proportion of the population are immigrants (the guess is 25%, when it is really only 13%, according to of cial estimates).
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: People are worried about the concentration of wealth, the housing pressures facing young people and immigration levels, and this is re ected in them overstating the scale of the issues.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: But, the survey suggests there are also some issues where people are not as worried as they should be.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: For example, most countries hugely underestimate how much of their population is overweight or obese.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: The worst case is Saudi Arabia, where people think only 28% are, when 71% are.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Britons think it is 44%, when it is actually nearly half as much again Ð 62% are either overweight or obese.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: And, in many ways, it is not our misperceptions but these realities across different countries that are the most interesting and important aspects of the study.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: The top 1% in Russia own 70% of the nationÕs wealth, while the top 1% in New Zealand only own 18%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Half of Italians aged 25-34 still live with their parents, when it is only 4% in Norway.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: The average age in India is 27; it is 47 in Japan.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: Only 10% of politicians are women in Brazil, Hungary and Japan, when 44% are in Sweden.
label: ignore

title: item_p0110
complex: When the reality is so strange and varied, it is no wonder weÕre so wrong.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: As colourful sh were swimming past him off the Greek coast, Cathal Redmond was convinced he had taken some great photos with his rst underwater camera.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: But, when he looked at the results on dry land, the images were brown and murky.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: Having taken the pictures while holding his breath underwater, he blamed the limited time he had to set up the shots.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: All he needed, the industrial designer thought, was a little more time to properly capture the sh in their natural environment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: He vowed to make the little extra time needed a reality and the result is his invention of the Express Dive Ð a re llable air storage device, held in the mouth, that lets users swim underwater for two minutes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: It is aimed at bridging the gap between snorkelling, with its limited scope, and scuba diving, which gives divers the freedom to breathe underwater but at the cost of using cumbersome and expensive gear.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: The prototype of the invention Ð which is still in the initial stages of testing individual parts Ð looks like a cross between a scuba mouthpiece and a water bottle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: ÒI wanted to enable people to do more.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: So, rather than just get in underwater and spend 30 seconds holding their breath, I wanted to do a little bit more than they were able to do,Ó says Redmond, 27.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: In 2006, the Irish designer completed a scuba-diving course and loved the feeling of being able to breathe underwater and observe sh in their natural environment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: Less enjoyable, however, were the ns, the weight belt, the wetsuit, air tank, mask and all the other equipment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: ÒI was very keenly aware of the fact that I had about 50kg strapped to me and getting into the water was quite foreign when you are used to trying to keep yourself at the surface.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: It was a very surreal experience,Ó he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: ÒThe real problem is that it is very limiting as to what you can do.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: Although it allows you to stay underwater for longer, you have to plan your whole day around it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: You have 20kg to 50kg of gear with you Ð you canÕt be walking on the beach and decide you want to go in.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: Planning is a very big part of it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: It demands a lot.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: There is a lot of relearning required.Ó It was during a nal-year project for his product design degree at the University of Limerick that Redmond produced the Express Dive.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: The device has two main parts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: When above the surface, the unit uses a fan to suck in air via a vent in the mouthpiece.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: The air, accelerated around the motor-driven turbine, is compressed through a series of valves and stored in the attached tank, which has a display light that ashes green when it is full.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: When air is no longer being taken in, the vent shuts off and, as the user dives, air is fed back via the mouthpiece.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: That display, which is in the userÕs eyeline, then acts as a health bar turning from green to red when the air runs low.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: The device can take in enough air for two minutes of diving and takes approximately the same amount of time to be re lled.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: ÒWhen you put the compressor into the unit with all of the rest of the stuff, the batteries and the electronics and the membranes for the scuba delivery, the big trick was to be able to get it small enough so that it would be able to be held through the teeth and out of the mouth,Ó Redmond says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: The electrics are shielded from the water in the casing and are recharged using inductive power transfer Ð a system using an electromagnetic eld, similar to the pads which can wirelessly charge mobile phones, so that there is no need for exposed wires.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: Redmond says the mouthpiece feels similar to using a snorkel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: He likens it to an extension of the lungs in that the user is taking a deep breath and then using it underwater.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: ÒIt is an extension of the bodyÕs capacity to store air,Ó he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: The prototype, made from high-density foam, aluminium and silicone, has been tested in parts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: Redmond says he has shown that the motor can compress two minutesÕ worth of air into the unit and that the design can be effectively held in the diverÕs mouth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: What he has not yet done is test the device on a diver, fully submerged for two minutes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: But, with enough testing, Redmond is con dent he can get a fully functioning device that will not endanger swimmers underwater.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: Redmond recently came runner-up for the International James Dyson Award, which will give him £5,000 to further develop the project.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: Early indications are that the device would be priced at £280, he says, and it is likely to weigh anywhere from 1kg to 3kg depending on the safety features needed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: To anyone who thinks two minutes of air is no more than a minor improvement on snorkelling, Redmond says it could make all the difference underwater.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: The typical swimmer can hold their breath for about 40 seconds while underwater, he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0111
complex: ÒTwo minutes is not a lot of time but it is a lot longer than that,Ó he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: Writing in the journal Nature, former president of the Royal Institute of Navigation Roger McKinlay argues that our reliance on GPS (global positioning system) technology is misplaced and could be eroding our innate way- nding abilities.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒIf we do not cherish them, our natural navigation abilities will deteriorate as we rely ever more on smart devices,Ó he wrote.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: McKinlay believes huge investment will be needed before navigation systems will be good enough to allow technologies such as autonomous vehicles to take off.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: In the meantime, he argues, we need better research into systems for navigation while children should be encouraged to learn how to nd their way around by more traditional means.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒSchools should teach navigation and map reading as life skills,Ó he wrote.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: According to Ofcom (the Of ce of Communications), around 66% of adults in the UK owned a smartphone in 2015, up from 39% in 2012, making GPS technology widely available.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: But McKinlay, a satellite communication and navigation consultant, believes that we should be wary of leaving our navigational needs to our devices.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒNavigation is a use-it-or-lose-it skill,Ó he wrote.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: While few scienti c studies have explored the issue, research from 2009 supports the notion.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒWhat we did was to look at a set of current London taxi drivers and a set of London taxi drivers that had been retired for about four years,Ó said neuroscientist Dr Hugo Spiers, head of the Spatial Cognition Group at University College London, who is an author of the study.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: The results showed that the retired taxi drivers performed worse on navigation tests than those still behind the wheel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒWe were able to show that their abilities did drop away if they werenÕt using their knowledge on that particular test.Ó Spiers also believes there is a danger in relying on technologies like GPS but he is quick to point out that the biggest risk lies in users being unwittingly led into perilous situations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: Among the fatalities blamed on satnavs (satellite navigation devices) was the death of a driver who, in 2010, plunged into a reservoir in Spain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒThere is a genuine potential for risk in relying on a satnav,Ó said Spiers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒBut the actual health risk of not using your brain effectively is not known.Ó The way in which navigational technology is used could also affect its impact on our own abilities, says Spiers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: While audio instructions to drivers remove the need to think about navigation, he says, the use of smartphone apps as digital maps is very different.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒIn the old days, you had to print out or take an A to Z map in your pocket Ð what we are doing now is just using computer- aided information and you are having to think really quite hard about where you are going and interact with this device,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒThe modern technology isnÕt just dumbing us down completely.Ó McKinlay remains convinced that navigation technologies have a long way to go before futuristic scenarios involving driverless cars and smart cities become a reality.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒFor really critical applications Ð safety applications like landing aircraft or navigating aircraft Ð GPS is still not good enough,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: Though Spiers believes the development of arti cial intelligence based on machine learning could lead to a new wave of navigational aids, McKinlay remains sceptical.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒWe will see ever- smarter machines which are very, very task speci c, but the big breakthrough will be when they are able to tune in to what you might be thinking and what you might be wanting to achieve,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: Ultimately, McKinlay believes, itÕs essential that humans remain able to take control of their navigation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒDo you really want to encourage people to get to a point where, when it disappears or when the battery goes at, they are in total shock and can do nothing?Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0112
complex: ÒTechnology isnÕt magic Ð it is just a tool.Ó
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: Throughout a momentous day at Liverpool’s Anglican Cathedral for the families of the 96 people who died so needlessly at Sheffield Wednesday’s Hillsborough football ground, one phrase dominated above all else: the truth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: These were the words most infamously abused by a headline in The Sun newspaper, above stories which we now know, in extraordinarily shocking detail, were fed by the South Yorkshire Police to deflect their own culpability for the disaster on to the innocent victims.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: Margaret Aspinall, whose son James, then 18, died at what should have been a joyful day out, an FA Cup semi-final between Liverpool and Nottingham Forest, said the families had been forced to fight, for 23 years, for just that: the truth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: Aspinall, Chair of the Hillsborough Family Support Group, said that, although the families’ loss would never fade, she was “delighted” at the unequivocal, “profound” apology given for Hillsborough’s savage failings by David Cameron.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The Hillsborough Independent Panel had inspected 450,000 documents generated by the police, Sheffield Wednesday and all other bodies responsible, and delivered its remarkable 395-page report indicting official failings and vindicating the victims and football supporters.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: Some of what happened to cause the disaster, and the police’s subsequent blame-shifting, has been exposed before.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: But the depth of what the families call a cover-up, in particular the deliberate police campaign to avoid its own responsibilities and falsely blame the supporters, was still startling.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: In a concerted campaign – led, the panel found, by the Chief Constable, Peter Wright – the South Yorkshire Police put out their story that drunken supporters or those without tickets had caused the disaster.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The victims had their blood tested for alcohol levels.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: This was “an exceptional decision”, the panel said, for which it found “no rationale”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: When victims had alcohol in their blood, the police then checked to find if they had criminal records.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The report, substantially authored by Professor Phil Scraton of Queen’s University, Belfast, and unanimously agreed by the panel of eight experts, found there was “no evidence … to verify the serious allegations of exceptional levels of drunkenness, ticketlessness or violence among Liverpool fans”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The report found that even as the nightmare began for the families of the victims, Wright was meeting his police federation in a Sheffield restaurant to prepare “a defence” and “a rock-solid story”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The Secretary of the South Yorkshire Police Federation branch, Constable Paul Middup, told the restaurant meeting before Wright turned up: “The Chief Constable had said the truth could not come from him, but had given the secretary a totally free hand and supported him,” as had many senior officers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The meeting was held just four days after the disaster.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: It was the day that The Sun splashed its headline “The Truth” over lies fed to it by four senior South Yorkshire police officers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: Middup was encouraged to continue this police campaign of defaming Liverpool supporters for supposed drunkenness and misbehaviour and “to get the message – togetherness – across to the force”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The panel’s report sustained the allegation made in parliament that the orchestrated changing of junior officers’ statements by senior South Yorkshire police officers amounted to a “black propaganda unit”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The officers’ statements, presented as official police accounts to the subsequent inquiry, were changed to delete criticism of the police themselves on the day, and, largely, emphasize misbehaviour by supporters.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The panel found that 116 of 164 statements were amended “to remove or alter comments unfavourable to South Yorkshire police”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The police had claimed this was done only to remove “conjecture” and “opinion” from the statements, but the panel had no doubt the operation, to craft a case rather than deliver truthful police accounts, went further.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: “It was done to remove criticism of the police,” Scraton said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: This propaganda did not convince the original inquiry, which ruled as quickly as August 1989 that the police stories of fan drunkenness and misbehaviour were false, and criticized the police for making the claims.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The report revealed that Sheffield Wednesday’s football ground was unsafe in crucial respects, that the Football Association had selected it as the venue for the match without even checking if Hillsborough had a valid safety certificate, which it did not.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: In that landscape of neglect, it was the mismanagement of the crowd by the police, commanded by an inexperienced Chief Superintendent David Duckenfield, that was “the prime cause” of the disaster.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The police lost control outside the ground, where 24,000 Liverpool fans had to be funnelled through just 23 turnstiles, so Duckenfield ordered a large exit gate to be opened and a large number of people to be allowed in.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: His “blunder of the first magnitude”, according to the inquiry, was the failure to close off the tunnel that led to the already overcrowded central section of the Leppings Lane terrace.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The inquiry report established this but the police, undaunted, repeated their claims to the subsequent inquest.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: Its procedure was marked by the coroner’s decision not to take evidence of what happened after 3.15pm on the day of the disaster, thereby excluding an emergency response the panel found to have been chaotic.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: The finding that 41 of the 96 who died could possibly have been saved had the police and ambulance service done their jobs decently is damning of those bodies and, Aspinall said, difficult for the families to contemplate.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: In the light of the panel’s report, the Attorney General will now consider whether to apply to the High Court for the inquest verdict of accidental death to be quashed and a new inquest held.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: There may be prosecutions too, after all these years, of Sheffield Wednesday, South Yorkshire Police and Sheffield City Council, which failed in its duty to oversee the safety of the football ground.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: Trevor Hicks, the President of the HFSG, both of whose teenage daughters, Sarah and Victoria, died in the crush, said: “The truth is out today.
label: ignore

title: item_p0113
complex: Tomorrow is for justice.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: The sight of colleagues and acquaintances taking a drag on an e-cigarette has become commonplace.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: But have we reached Òpeak vapeÓ?
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Statistics suggest that vaping among smokers and recent ex-smokers, who comprise the vast majority of vapers, may already be on the decline.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: The gures will be studied closely by the major e-cigarette rms, which have poured millions into promoting a technology that was thought to have been growing in popularity.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Figures released in 2014 by the health charity Action on Smoking and Health (ASH) reveal that usage among adults in Britain of electronic cigarettes Ð which do not contain tobacco and produce vapour, not smoke Ð tripled from an estimated 700,000 users in 2012 to 2.1 million in 2014.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: However, gures collated by the Smoking Toolkit Study, a research body backed by the Department of Health that provides quarterly updates on smoking trends, show vapingÕs appeal may be waning.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Vaping rates among smokers and ex-smokers rose steadily until the end of 2013, when some 22% of smokers and ex-smokers were vaping.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: But this proportion levelled out throughout 2014 before dropping to 19% during the nal quarter of 2014.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Early signs suggest the decline has continued into 2015.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: The drop is described as Òstatistically signi cantÓ by Professor Robert West, of UCLÕs Department of Epidemiology and Public Health, who collates the gures for the Toolkit.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Smokers are the key group for e-cigarette rms because seven out of ten vapers are smokers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Only around 1% of people who have never smoked have tried an electronic cigarette.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: ÒNumbers who use e-cigarettes while continuing to smoke are going down,Ó West said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: ÒWeÕve only been tracking vaping for just over a year, so itÕs a short time period, but we are not seeing growth in the number of long-term ex-smokers or ÔneverÕ smokers using e-cigarettes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: That is not to say vaping rates might not change but, at this stage, it looks like theyÕre staying the same.Ó The levelling off in popularity of vaping in the UK would appear to be at odds with what is happening in the US, where the technology has been promoted aggressively and where recent reports suggested it was growing in popularity.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: However, West questioned the interpretation of US data, which made little distinction between people who had once tried an e-cigarette and those who regularly vaped.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Fears that vaping could become fashionable among young non-smokers appear to be misplaced, according to experts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Only 1.8% of children are regular users, the ASH study found.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Instead, e-cigarettes seem to be most popular among adults seeking to quit.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: ÒWhile the gures published this month by Smoking In England show that the use of electronic cigarettes by smokers has levelled off, their data also shows the huge increase in use since May 2011,Ó said James Dunworth, director and co-founder of ecigarettedirect.co.uk.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: ÒOur customers are still very happy with the product, and technology and innovation in hardware is improving user experience and helping them to switch from traditional cigarettes.Ó ÒE-cigarettes are behaving like a souped-up nicotine patch,Ó West agreed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: ÒThey are more popular than nicotine patches and may or may not be more effective.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: One-third of quit attempts use e-cigarettes, which makes them by far the most popular method of stopping.Ó Hazel Cheeseman, director of policy at ASH, said it was too soon to say whether vaping had peaked.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: ÒAlthough there are indications that the market hasnÕt grown in the UK for about a year, there doesnÕt seem to be a decline in the number of people using electronic cigarettes to help them quit smoking.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: Using an electronic cigarette is safer than smoking; some, but not all, people nd them useful to help quit smoking and there is little evidence that they are leading to an increase in young people smoking.Ó It emerged recently that the European Commission (EC) is looking at increasing taxes on e-cigarettes, something that could have an impact on their popularity.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: A new EC tobacco directive comes into force in 2016 that will limit the amount of nicotine in e-cigarettes to below their current levels.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: This may mean vapers will have to increase their usage to obtain the same hit, again something that may make e-cigarettes more expensive.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: West suggested that policymakers should see e-cigarettes as a smoking cessation aid and not subject them to the same regulations as smoking.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: ÒThere is a tendency among some local authorities and organizations to treat e-cigarettes as cigarettes and ban them in public places and outdoors,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: ÒThe problem is that brings public health into disrepute.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: It just sounds like youÕre having a go at vapers and hitting everyone with a sledgehammer and that undermines the public health messages weÕre trying to get out.
label: ignore

title: item_p0114
complex: We have to be careful not to stigmatize e-cigarettes.Ó
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Passing clouds One of the pleasures of flying is seeing clouds close up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Even though they seem insubstantial they carry a considerable weight of water – around 500 tonnes in a small cumulus cloud.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: And water is denser than air.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: So why don’t clouds fall out of the sky like rain?
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: They do.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: But the droplets take a long time to sink.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: An average cloud would take a year to fall one metre.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: On cloud nine Most of us are happy to label clouds “fluffy ones” or “nasty black ones”, but meteorologists identify more than 50 cloud types based on shape and altitude.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: These fit into categories given numbers from one to nine.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Cloud nine is the vast, towering cumulonimbus, so to be “on cloud nine” implies being on top of the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Around the rainbow There’s no better place to see a rainbow than from a plane.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Rainbows are produced when sunlight hits raindrops.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: We see a bow because the Earth gets in the way, but, from a plane, a rainbow is a complete circle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: When passing over clouds, the plane’s shadow appears neatly in the centre of the effect.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Mr blue sky Sunlight is white, containing all the colours of the spectrum but, as it passes through air, some of the light is scattered when it interacts with the gas molecules.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Blue light scatters more than the lower-energy colours, so the blue appears to come from the sky.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: There’s life out there Apart from clouds and other planes, we don’t expect to see much directly outside a flying aircraft’s window, but the air is seething with bacterial life – as many as 1,800 different types of bacteria have been detected over cities and they can reach twice the cruising height of a plane.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Turbulence terror Even the most experienced flyer can be turned green by turbulence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: The outcome can be anything from repeated bumping to sudden, dramatic plunges.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: The good news for nervous flyers is that no modern airliner has ever been brought down by turbulence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: People have been injured and occasionally killed when they are not strapped in, or get struck by poorly secured luggage – but the plane is not going to be knocked out of the sky.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: In-flight radiation When body scanners were introduced at airports there were radiation scares but the level produced is the same as passengers receive in one minute of flight.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: The Earth is constantly bombarded by cosmic rays, natural radiation from space that has more impact at altitude.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: You can’t cure jet lag The world is divided into time zones.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: The result is that long-haul travel results in a difference between local time and your body’s time, causing jet lag.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: However, its effects can be minimized by keeping food bland for 24 hours before travel, drinking plenty of fluids and living on your destination time from the moment you reach the aircraft.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Supersonic 747s Many of us have travelled faster than sound.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: There are a number of jet streams in the upper atmosphere, notably on the journey from the US to Europe, where a temperature inversion causes a corridor of air to move as fast as 250mph.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: If an airliner with an airspeed of 550mph enters a jet stream, the result can be to fly at 800mph, above sound’s 740mph.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Flying through time Time zones provide an artificial journey through time – but special relativity means that a flight involves actual time travel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: It’s so minimal, though, that crossing the Atlantic weekly for 40 years would only move you 1/1,000th of a second into the future.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Terrible tea Don’t blame the cabin attendant if your tea isn’t great.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Water should be just under 100°C when it is poured on to tea leaves – but that isn’t possible on a plane.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: It’s impossible to get water beyond 90°C during flight – so choose coffee.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: I can’t hear my food Airline food has a reputation for being bland and tasteless.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Some of the problem may not be poor catering, though.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: A plane is a noisy environment and there is evidence that food loses some of its savour when we are exposed to loud noises.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Needle in a haystack With modern technology, it seems strange that Malaysian flight MH370 could disappear without a trace – yet, finding a missing aircraft is a needlein- a-haystack problem.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: The plane knows its location, both from GPS and inertial tracking, but this information is not relayed elsewhere in real time.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: That would be perfectly possible.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Oceangoing ships have had tracking since the 1980s – the limitation is not technology but a lack of legislation requiring it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Volcanic fallout Air travel can be cancelled by volcanic activity.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Glass-like ash particles melt in the heat of the engine, then solidify on the rotors.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: A clear-skies policy in an ash cloud may be inconvenient – but the risks of ignoring the ash are clear.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: The wing myth For many years, we taught the wrong explanation for the way wings keep planes in the air.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: In fact, almost all a plane’s lift comes from Newton’s Third Law of Motion.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: The wing is shaped to push air downwards.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: As the air is pushed down, the wing gets an equal and opposite push upwards, lifting the plane.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Forget electric planes When we see ultra-light, experimental, electric planes, it’s easy to assume there will soon be clean, green, electric airliners, but it won’t happen any time soon.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Aircraft fuel packs in a remarkable amount of energy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Batteries are much less efficient.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: To provide the same energy as a tonne of fuel would take 100 tonnes of batteries – and a 747 uses 150 to 200 tonnes of fuel.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Unless battery technology is made vastly more efficient, electric airliners won’t get off the ground.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Beware the vortex Pilots often wait a long time to get clearance.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: This is to allow the air to settle after a previous take-off, as a plane’s wingtips generate vortices in the air, which can take two or three minutes to disperse.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: If the following aircraft set off immediately, the rapidly moving air would make the plane difficult to handle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: The delay gives the air time to recover from the miniature whirlwinds caused by the preceding plane.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: The doors aren’t locked In practice, the doors on a plane don’t need to be locked.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: If you watch an aircraft door being opened, it swings in an unusual way.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: It first has to be opened inwards before manoeuvring it out of the way.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: Once the plane has taken off, a significant pressure difference soon builds up between the inside of the plane and the outside.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: This differential forces the door into place.
label: ignore

title: item_p0115
complex: To open it, you would have to pull against the air pressure, well beyond the capabilities of human muscles.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: Illegal downloading is a kind of “moral squalor” and theft, as much as reaching in to someone’s pocket and stealing their wallet is theft, says author Philip Pullman.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: In an article for Index on Censorship, Pullman, who is president of the Society of Authors, makes a robust defence of copyright laws.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: He is highly critical of internet users who think it is OK to download music or books without paying for them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: “The technical brilliance is so dazzling that people can’t see the moral squalor of what they’re doing,” he writes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: “It is outrageous that anyone can steal an artist’s work and get away with it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: It is theft, as surely as reaching into someone’s pocket and taking their wallet is theft.” His article comes after music industry leaders met British Prime Minister David Cameron in Downing Street, where the issue of web piracy was discussed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: Pullman, writer of the His Dark Materials trilogy, says authors and musicians work in poverty and obscurity for years to bring their work to the level “that gives delight to their audiences and, as soon as they achieve that, the possibility of making a living from it is taken away from them”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: He concludes: “The principle is simple, and unaltered by technology, science or magic: if we want to enjoy the work that someone does, we should pay for it.” Pullman is writing in the next issue of the campaign group’s magazine in a dialogue with Cathy Casserly, chief executive of Creative Commons, which offers open content licences “that lets creators take copyright into their own hands”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: Casserly argues that there is much wrong with copyright, which was created “in an analogue age”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: She writes: “By default, copyright closes the door on countless ways that people can share, build upon and remix each other’s work, possibilities that were unimaginable when those laws were established.” She says artists need to think creatively about how they distribute and monetize their work, quoting the science fiction writer Cory Doctorow, who said: “My problem is not piracy, it’s obscurity.” Index on Censorship agrees.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: The magazine’s editor, Rachael Jolley, said: “Existing copyright laws don’t work in the digital age and risk criminalizing consumers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: We need new models for how artists, writers and musicians earn a living from their work.” The debate is a lively one and the scale of illegal downloading vast.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: Data collected by Ofcom (the independent regulator and competition authority for the UK communications industries) suggests that, between November 2012 and January 2013 in the UK, 280 million music tracks were digitally pirated, along with 52 million TV shows, 29 million films, 18 million ebooks and 7 million software or games files.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: Ofcom has said 18% of internet users aged over 12 admit to having recently pirated content, and 9% say they fear getting caught.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: Pullman writes in his article: “The ease and swiftness with which music can be acquired in the form of MP3 downloads is still astonishing to those of us who have been building up our iTunes list for some time.” One thing to emerge from the Downing Street meeting was Cameron’s appointment of the Conservative MP Mike Weatherley to be his adviser on the subject.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: A spokesman for the BPI, the record industry trade body, said: “Mike Weatherley is a strong champion of copyright and the artists and creative producers it’s there to protect.
label: ignore

title: item_p0116
complex: We hope his influence and the prime minister’s endorsement of copyright will be brought to bear on the approach of the UK’s intellectual property office.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: Scientists have implanted a false memory in the brains of mice in an experiment that they hope will shed light on the well-documented phenomenon whereby people ‘remember’ events or experiences that have never happened.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: False memories are a major problem with witness statements in courts of law.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: Defendants have often been convicted of offences based on eyewitness testimony only to have their convictions later overturned when DNA, or some other corroborating evidence, is brought to bear.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: form in the human brain, Susumu Tonagawa, a neuroscientist at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), and his team encoded memories in the brains of mice by manipulating individual neurons.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: Memories of experiences we have had are made from several elements, including records of objects, space and time.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: These records, called engrams, are encoded in physical and chemical changes in brain cells and the connections between them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: According to Tonagawa, both false and genuine memories seem to rely on the same brain mechanisms.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: In their work, Tonagawa’s team used a technique known as optogenetics, which allows the fine control of individual brain cells.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: They engineered brain cells in the mouse hippocampus, a part of the brain known to be involved in forming memories, to express the gene for a protein called channelrhodopsin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: When cells that contain channelrhodopsin are exposed to blue light, they become activated.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: The researchers also modified the hippocampus cells so that the channelrhodopsin protein would be produced in whichever brain cells the mouse was using to encode its memory engrams.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: In the experiment, Tonagawa’s team placed the mice in a chamber and allowed them to explore it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: As they did so, relevant memory-encoding brain cells were producing the channelrhodopsin protein.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: The next day, the same mice were placed in a second chamber and given a small electric shock, to encode a fear response.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: At the same time, the researchers shone light into the mouse brains to activate their memories of the first chamber.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: That way, the mice learned to associate fear of the electric shock with the memory of the first chamber.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: In the final part of the experiment, the team placed the mice back in the first chamber.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: The mice froze, demonstrating a typical fear response, even though they had never been shocked while there.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “We call this ‘incepting’ or implanting false memories in a mouse brain,” Tonagawa told Science.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: A similar process may occur when powerful false memories are created in humans.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “Humans are very imaginative animals,” said Tonagawa.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “Independent of what is happening around you in the outside world, humans constantly have internal activity in the brain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: So, just like our mouse, it is quite possible we can associate what we happen to have in our mind with bad or good high-variance ongoing events.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: In other words, there could be a false association of what you have in your mind rather than what is happening to you.” He added: “Our study showed that the false memory and the genuine memory are based on very similar, almost identical, brain mechanisms.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: It is difficult for the false memory bearer to distinguish between them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: We hope our future findings along this line will further alert legislatures and legal experts to how unreliable memory can be.” Chris French, head of the Anomalistic Psychology Research Unit at Goldsmiths, University of London, is a leading researcher in false memories in people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: He said that the latest results were an important first step in understanding their neural basis.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “Memory researchers have always recognized that memory does not, as is often assumed, work like a video camera, faithfully recording all of the details of anything we experience.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: Instead, it is a reconstructive process, which involves building a specific memory from fragments of real memory traces of the original event, but also possibly including information from other sources.” He cautioned that the false memories created in the mice in the experiments were far simpler than the complex false memories that have generated controversy within psychology and psychiatry – for example, false memories of childhood sexual abuse, or even memories for bizarre ritualized satanic abuse, abduction by aliens, or “past lives”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “Such rich false memories will clearly involve many brain systems and we are still a long way from understanding the processes involved in their formation at the neuronal level,” said Professor French.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: Mark Stokes, a neuroscientist at Oxford University, said the experiments were a “tour de force” but that it was important to put them into perspective.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “Although the results seem to imply that new memories were formed by the artificial stimulation (rather than the actual environment), this kind of phenomenon is still a long way from most people’s idea of memory,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: Rather, he said, it was equivalent to implanting an association that perhaps someone cannot place but that makes them wary of a specific environment for no apparent reason.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “It is unlikely that this kind of pairing could lead to the rich set of associations related to normal memories, although it is possible that, over time, such pairing could be integrated with other memories to construct a more elaborate false narrative.” The mouse models created by the MIT team will help scientists ask ever more complex questions about memories in people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “Now that we can reactivate and change the contents of memories in the brain, we can begin asking questions that were once the realm of philosophy,” said Steve Ramirez, a colleague of Tonagawa’s at MIT.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “Are there multiple conditions that lead to the formation of false memories?
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: Can false memories be artificially created?
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: What about false memories for more than just contexts – false memories for objects, food or other mice?
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: These are the once seemingly sci-fi questions that can now be experimentally tackled in the lab.” As the technology develops, said French, scientists need to think about its uses carefully.
label: ignore

title: item_p0117
complex: “Whatever means are used to implant false memories, we need to be very aware of the ethical issues raised by such procedures – the potential for abuse of such techniques cannot be overstated.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: The customer next to you in the queue looks innocent enough.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: But, instead of a shopping list, you notice sheÕs carrying handwritten notes about the appearance and cleanliness of the store.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: SheÕs been timing the progression of the queue on her phone ... and is that a tiny camera lens peeking out from her purse?
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: The odds are youÕve just spotted a mystery shopper.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: There are approximately 50,000 mystery shopping trips carried out every month in the UK, according to the Mystery Shopping Providers Association, and, as more and more spending takes place online, the demand for mystery shoppers is growing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒRetailers are becoming increasingly aware that shoppers who are prepared to set foot in a physical store want a service and an experience they canÕt get online,Ó says Simon Boydell, spokesman for Marketforce, which has more than 300,000 mystery shoppers on its books.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒOur clients want to measure how well their stores are delivering on that experience.Ó ÒWe assign different store locations to each shopper and rotate them so that they never go back to the same shop within three months,Ó says Jill Spencer of mystery shopping company ABa.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒEach day, they typically spend up to eight hours visiting ve to ten stores, plus another hour or two ling detailed reports on every aspect of their visit.Ó For that, the mystery shoppers can earn up to £155 a day.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: They are also reimbursed for their petrol and hotel stays, and compensated for their car depreciation (the shoppers can be expected to drive as many as 20,000 miles a year).
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: Meanwhile, video mystery shoppers, who lm their visits with a hidden camera planted in a buttonhole or handbag, can earn even more Ð around £300 a day.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: Shoppers are usually repaid any money they spend in the stores and may also be allowed to keep the products they buy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒIÕm typically given between £5 and £20 to spend at each store, to assess the service I receive at the till,Ó says Laura, a 50-year-old mystery shopper from Devon, who has been paid to visit around 7,000 shops since 2001.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: The purchase usually has to be related to a service or a type of product that the retailer wants her to check.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒIÕm always given a scenario, such as buying something from a speci c department or a new product range, but, within that framework, I can often buy whatever I want Ð and keep it.Ó Like most full-time mystery shoppers, Laura is self-employed, taking jobs from ABa and other mystery shopping companies as and when they come up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: Her income is typically £30,000 to £40,000 a year and that doesnÕt include all the freebies she gets on the job.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒWith the perks, itÕs enough to live on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: But I donÕt do it because I love shopping.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: In fact, I hate shopping now.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: When IÕm not working, it pains me to have to go out and buy a pint of milk.Ó She does, however, nd it satisfying to return to a store she has previously mystery shopped and see standards have improved.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒI know it must be because of my feedback or why would they pay me to give it?
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: Some of the retailers I shop at win awards for customer service and I think that is down to us mystery shoppers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: I feel IÕm not just doing a service to my company; IÕm doing a service to all shoppers everywhere.Ó Sadly, regular mystery shopping assignments that pay like LauraÕs are few and far between.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: In fact, competition is so erce, she keeps her job a closely guarded secret and even her friends and family donÕt know who she works for (Laura is not her real name).
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ItÕs estimated that more than 500,000 people have registered as mystery shoppers in the UK, but just 10% or less manage to get regular work each month and this has led to a dramatic reduction in compensation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒWhere once you got a fee, reimbursement for your purchase and mileage, you now often just receive a contribution towards a purchase,Ó say Val, a 51-year-old former mystery shopper.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒI worked for 40 different mystery shopping companies for almost 20 years but I gave up entirely three years ago because I had bills to pay and very few assignments paid what I considered to be an acceptable rate.Ó Nowadays, mystery shopping companies mostly rely on the promise of freebies to incentivize their workers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒMarketforce shoppers typically get a couple of pounds for a visit as a token gesture for their time and effort,Ó says Boydell.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒAt the most, weÕll pay £15 to £25 plus reimbursement for, say, a meal for two or a hotel stay.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: We donÕt directly employ any shoppers so we donÕt have to pay them the minimum wage.Ó ÒIÕd go on a cruise for nothing,Ó says Laura.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: ÒBut I think mystery shopping companies that pay you a nominal fee to travel to a restaurant and eat a meal are exploiting people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: I wonÕt touch those jobs anymore.Ó There are plenty of people, however, that would.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: Hannah, a 41-year-old City lawyer, has conducted nearly 500 visits for the Mystery Dining Company in her spare time without receiving remuneration or travel expenses.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: As a prestigious Òplatinum dinerÓ, she is regularly hand-picked by the Mystery Dining Company to carry out their most exclusive assignments, enjoying £200 meals at Michelin-starred Mayfair restaurants, ve-star hospitality at Ascot and overnight stays at boutique hotels.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: But thereÕs no such thing as a free lunch, even if you work for a mystery dining company.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: Hannah says she typically spends two to four hours after each visit writing detailed reports on everything from the quality of the food to speci c interactions with staff, whom she always needs to be able to name or ÒsubtlyÓ describe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0118
complex: Trickiest of all, she must memorize all these details while eating her meal Ð unable, of course, to openly write anything down.

title: item_p0118

title: item_p0119

title: item_p0120
complex: Organic food has more of the antioxidant compounds linked to better health than regular food, and lower levels of toxic metals and pesticides, according to the most comprehensive scienti c analysis to date.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The international team behind the work suggests that switching to organic fruit and vegetables could give the same bene ts as adding one or two portions of the recommended Ô ve a dayÕ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The team, led by Professor Carlo Leifert at Newcastle University, concludes that there are Òstatistically signi cant, meaningfulÓ differences, with a range of antioxidants being Òsubstantially higherÓ Ð between 19% and 69% Ð in organic food.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: It is the rst study to demonstrate clear and wide-ranging differences between organic and conventional fruits, vegetables and cereals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The researchers say the increased levels of antioxidants are equivalent to Òone to two of the ve portions of fruits and vegetables recommended to be consumed daily and would therefore be signi cant and meaningful in terms of human nutrition, if information linking these compounds to the health bene ts associated with increased fruit, vegetable and wholegrain consumption is con rmedÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The ndings will add to the controversy over organic food and whether it is better for people, with one expert saying that the ndings were exaggerated.

title: item_p0120
complex: Tom Sanders, a professor of nutrition at KingÕs College London, said the research did show some differences.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: ÒBut the question is are they within natural variation?
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: And are they nutritionally relevant?
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: I am not convinced.Ó He said LeifertÕs work had caused controversy in the past.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: ÒLeifert has had a lot of disagreements with a lot of people.Ó Sanders added the research showed organic cereals have less protein than conventional crops.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The results of the research are based on an analysis of 343 peer-reviewed studies from around the world Ð more than ever before Ð which examine differences between organic and conventional fruit, vegetables and cereals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: ÒThe crucially important thing about this research is that it shatters the myth that how we farm does not affect the quality of the food we eat,Ó said Helen Browning, chief executive of the Soil Association, which campaigns for organic farming.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: Plants produce many of their antioxidant compounds to ght back against pest attacks, so the higher levels in organic crops may result from their lack of protection by chemical sprays.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: But, the scientists say other reasons may be important, such as organic varieties being bred for toughness and not being overfed with arti cial fertilizers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: Leifert and his colleagues conclude that many antioxidants Òhave previously been linked to a reduced risk of chronic diseases, including cardiovascular diseases, neurodegenerative diseases and certain cancersÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: But, they also note that no long-term studies showing health bene ts from a broad organic diet have yet been conducted.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The researchers found much higher levels of cadmium, a toxic metal, in conventional crops.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: Pesticide residues were found on conventional crops four times more often than on organic food.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The research was funded by the EU and an organic farming charity.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The research is certain to be criticized: the inclusion of so many studies in the analysis could mean poor-quality work skews the results, although the team did Òsensitivity analysesÓ and found that excluding weaker work did not signi cantly change the outcome.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: Also, the higher levels of cadmium and pesticides in conventional produce were still well below regulatory limits.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: But, the researchers say cadmium accumulates over time in the body and that some people may wish to avoid this, and that pesticide limits are set individually, not for the cocktail of chemicals used on crops.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: A further criticism is that the differences seen may result from different climates, soil types and crop varieties, and not from organic farming, though the researchers argue that combining many studies should average out these other differences.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The greatest criticism, however, will be over the suggestions of potential health bene ts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The most recent major analysis, which took in 223 studies in 2012, found little evidence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: ÒThe published literature lacks strong evidence that organic foods are signi cantly more nutritious than conventional foods,Ó it found.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: This was also the conclusion of earlier, smaller studies published in 2009 in a scienti c journal and by the UK Food Standards Agency, though the latter considered just 11 studies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: The 2012 study did note that eating organic food might help people avoid pesticide residues.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: Sanders said he was not persuaded by the new work.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: ÒYou are not going to be better nourished if you eat organic food,Ó he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: ÒWhat is most important is what you eat, not whether itÕs organic or conventional.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120
complex: ItÕs whether you eat fruit and vegetables at all.

title: item_p0120
complex: People are buying into a lifestyle system.

title: item_p0120
complex: They get an assurance it is not being grown with chemicals and is not grown by big business.Ó Opinion polls show healthy eating (55%) and avoiding chemical residues (53%) are key reasons cited by shoppers for buying organic produce.

title: item_p0120
complex: But, many also say care for the environment (44%) and animal welfare (31%) are important, as is taste (35%).

title: item_p0120
complex: Browning said: ÒThis research backs up what people think about organic food.

title: item_p0120
complex: In other countries, there have long been much higher levels of support and acceptance of the bene ts of organic food and farming.
label: ignore

title: item_p0120

complex: For three-and-a-half years, all pupils at St NinianÕs Primary have walked or run a mile each day.

title: item_p0122
complex: They do so at random times during the day, apparently happily, and, despite the rise in childhood obesity across the UK, none of the children at the school are overweight.

title: item_p0122
complex: The daily mile has done so much to improve these childrenÕs tness, behaviour and concentration in lessons that scores of nursery and primary schools across Britain are following suit and getting pupils to get up from their desks and take 15 minutes to walk or run round the school or local park.
