title: item_p0123
complex: Police and intelligence agencies around the world have, for almost 100 years, relied on lie detectors to help convict criminals or unearth spies and traitors.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The polygraph is beloved of the movies, with countless dramatic moments showing the guilty sweating profusely as they are hooked up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: But the invention could soon be defunct.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: Researchers in Britain and the Netherlands have made a breakthrough, developing a method with a success rate in tests of over 70% that could be in use in police stations around the world within a decade.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: Rather than relying on facial tics, talking too much or waving of arms – all seen as tell-tale signs of lying – the new method involves monitoring full-body motions to provide an indicator of signs of guilty feelings.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The polygraph is widely used in the US in criminal and other cases and for security clearance for the FBI and CIA but is much less popular in Europe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: There has been a lot of scepticism in the scientific and legal communities about its reliability.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: By contrast, the new method developed by the researchers has performed well in experiments.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The basic premise is that liars fidget more and so the use of an all-body motion suit – the kind used in films to create computer-generated characters – will pick this up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The suit contains 17 sensors that register movement up to 120 times per second in three dimensions for 23 joints.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: One of the research team, Ross Anderson, professor of security engineering at Cambridge University, said: “Decades of deception research show that the interviewer will tell truth from lies only slightly better than random, about 55 out of 100.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: “The polygraph has been around since the 1920s and, by measuring physiological stress induced by anxiety, you can get to 60.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: However, it can easily be abused as an interrogation prop and many people are anxious anyway facing a polygraph on which their job or liberty depends.” He said the new method, by contrast, achieved a reliability rating of over 70% and he was confident they would be able to do better.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: In some tests, the team has already achieved more than 80%.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: Anderson said: “The takeaway message is that guilty people fidget more and we can measure this robustly.” Anderson added that the research had a special significance at this time, against the background of the US Senate report on torture by the CIA.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: Apart from the moral case against torture, Anderson pointed out that it was a very unreliable way of gathering accurate information.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: “We have known for a long time that torture does not work,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The new method offers a pragmatic, scientifically backed alternative to conducting interviews.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The research paper was written by Dr Sophie van der Zee of Cambridge University, Professor Ronald Poppe of Utrecht University, Professor Paul Taylor of Lancaster University and Anderson.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The polygraph was created in 1921 by policeman John Larson, based on research by the psychologist William Marston.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: It records changes in pulse, blood pressure, sweating and breathing to ascertain whether a subject is lying.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: While cinema depictions suggest the device is near-infallible, the US Supreme Court ruled, in 1998, that there was no consensus that the polygraph was reliable, a finding supported by the US National Academy of Scientists in 2003.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The experiment carried out by Anderson and his colleagues involved 180 students and employees at Lancaster University, of which half were told to tell the truth and half to lie.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: They were each paid £7.50 for their participation in the 70-minute experiment, involving two tests.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: Some were interviewed about a computer game Never End, which they played for seven minutes, while others lied about playing it, having only been shown notes about it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The second test involved a lost wallet containing £5.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: Some were asked to bring the wallet to a lost-and-found box while others hid it and lied about it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: “Overall, we correctly classified 82.2% (truths: 88.9%; lies: 75.6%) of the interviewees as either being truthful or deceptive based on the combined movement in their individual limbs,” the report says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: Anderson said: “Our first attempt looked at the extent to which different body parts and body signals indicated deception.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: It turned out that liars wave their arms more but, again, this is only at the 60% level that you can get from a conventional polygraph.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: “The pay dirt was when we considered total body motion.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: That turns out to tell truth from lies over 70% of the time and we believe it can be improved still further by combining it with optimal questioning techniques.” Another advantage is that total body motion is relatively unaffected by cultural background, anxiety and cognitive load (how much you are thinking), which confound other lie-detection technologies, Anderson said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: The use of all-body suits is expensive – they cost about £30,000 – and can be uncomfortable, and Anderson and his colleagues are now looking at low-cost alternatives.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: These include using motion-sensing technology from computer games, such as the Kinect devices developed by Microsoft for the Xbox console.
label: ignore

title: item_p0123
complex: Anderson acknowledges that agencies such as the CIA could teach agents how to counter the full-body motion method by freezing their bodies but he said that in itself would be a giveaway.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The mass collection of telephone records by government surveillance programmes poses a clear threat to the personal privacy of ordinary citizens, according to US researchers who used basic phone logs to identify people and uncover confidential information about their lives.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Armed with anonymous “metadata” on people’s calls and texts, but not the content of the communications, two scientists at Stanford University worked out individuals’ names, where they lived and the names of their partners.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: But that was not all.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The same data led them to uncover potentially sensitive information about some individuals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: One man was found to own a rifle, while another had recently been diagnosed with an irregular heartbeat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Other data pointed to a new pregnancy and a person with multiple sclerosis.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The results highlight the extraordinary power of telephone metadata – the number called, when and for how long – particularly when it is paired with public information available from services such as Google, Yelp and Facebook.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The value of the data, which is not subject to the same legal protections as the content of people’s communications, has long been recognized by the security services.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: As Stewart Baker, the former general counsel at the US National Security Agency (NSA), put it in the aftermath of Edward Snowden’s revelations: “Metadata absolutely tells you everything about somebody’s life.” Patrick Mutchler, a computer security researcher at Stanford, said that while the power of metadata was understood by those gathering the information, the public was largely in the dark because so few published studies have revealed how rich the data is.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: “That makes it difficult for people with strong opinions about these programmes to fight them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Now, we have hard evidence we can point to that didn’t exist in the past,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: For the study, the researchers signed up 823 people who agreed to have metadata collected from their phones through an Android app.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The app also received information from their Facebook accounts, which the scientists used to check the accuracy of their results.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: In all, the researchers gathered metadata on more than 250,000 calls and over 1.2m texts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Analysts who logged into the NSA’s metadata-gathering system were initially allowed to examine data up to three hops away from an individual.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: A call from the target individual’s phone to another number was one hop.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: From that phone to another was two hops.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: And so on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The records available to analysts stretched back for five years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The collection window has now been restricted to two hops and 18 months at most.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Writing in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, Mutchler describes how, on a shoestring budget, he and fellow graduate student, Jonathan Mayer, uncovered a wealth of personal information, some of it sensitive, about people who took part in the study.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Through automatic and manual searches, they identified 82% of people’s names.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The same technique gave them the names of businesses the people had called.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: When these were plotted on a map, they revealed clusters of local businesses, which the scientists speculated surrounded the person’s home address.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: In this way, they named the city people lived in 57% of the time, and were nearly 90% accurate in placing people within 50 miles of their home.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Mutchler believes some of the misses came from people not updating their Facebook page when they moved out of their parents’ home, for example, to go to college.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The scientists next delved into more personal territory.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Using a simple computer program to analyse people’s call patterns, they inferred who among the study volunteers was in a relationship.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Once they knew the owner of a particular number had a partner, identifying the significant other was easy, they report.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: For the final part of the study, the researchers delved even deeper, to see what sensitive information they could glean from telephone metadata.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: They gathered details on calls made to and from a list of organizations, including hospitals, pharmacies, religious groups, legal services, firearms retailers and repair firms.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: From these, they pieced together some extraordinary vignettes from people’s lives.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The metadata from one person in the study showed they had a long call from a cardiology centre, spoke briefly with a medical laboratory, answered a number of short calls from a local pharmacy and then made calls to a hotline for abnormal heart-rate monitoring devices.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: Another participant made frequent calls to a local gun supplier that specialized in semiautomatic rifles and later placed a number of long calls to the customer support hotline run by a major gun manufacturer that produced the rifles.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: The metadata from two others suggested one had multiple sclerosis and the other had just become pregnant.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: “All of this should be taken as an indication of what is possible with two graduate students and limited resources,” said Mutchler, who argues that the findings should make policymakers think twice before authorizing mass surveillance programmes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: “Large-scale metadata surveillance programmes, like the NSA’s, will necessarily expose highly confidential information about ordinary citizens,” the scientists write, adding: “To strike an appropriate balance between national security and civil liberties, future policymaking must be informed by input from relevant sciences.” Ross Anderson, professor of security engineering at Cambridge University, said the study provided numbers that discussions can now be based on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: “With the right analytics running over nationscale communications data, you can infer huge amounts of sensitive information on everyone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0124
complex: We always suspected that, of course, but here’s the data.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: It has charted the world’s highest peaks, the ocean floor, the Amazon rainforest and even provided a glimpse into North Korea.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: But Google’s mission to map the world has largely steered clear of the inhospitable Arctic.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Now, however, the search-engine firm is embarking on what might be the most significant update to centuries of polar cartography – and one it hopes will help provide a better understanding of life on the permafrost for millions of web users.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Google has flown a small team to Iqaluit, the largest town in the Canadian territory of Nunavut, armed with their warmest winter gear, a stack of laptop computers and an 18kg backpackmounted telescopic camera.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Helped by an Inuit mapping expert, and stalked by curious locals, the team spent four days trudging through the terrain and collecting the images and information that will give the isolated community on the tundra of Baffin Island what urbanites across the globe now take for granted.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: The town of 7,000 people will go on display via Google’s popular Street View application in July 2013.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Aaron Brindle, project leader, said: “I live in Toronto and I absolutely take for granted that everything is where it should be and that this map is kind of my world, but for so long that hasn’t been the case in the north.” Unlike more populous and accessible parts of the world, which have been mapped using a special camera mounted on a car roof, the Iqaluit project had mappers hiking the town’s snow-packed roads and traversing little-known trails, some of which are made of ice and disappear in the brief summer months.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: The team also cut a path along part of a 15km cul-de-sac known as the Road to Nowhere, despite warnings about the risk from polar bears and other wildlife.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Mayor John Graham said the digital cartographers were, however, hunted by a herd of excited and curious locals, or Google stalkers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Graham understands the enthusiasm.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: The Street View project, he said, follows in the footsteps of the English explorer Martin Frobisher, who in 1576 sailed into the bay where Iqaluit now sits while searching for the Northwest Passage, and the 1941 flight of Captain Elliott Roosevelt, a reconnaissance officer and son of the US President, which led to the site being chosen for a military airbase.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: His exploration led to the founding of the modern town of Iqaluit, which is the seat of government as well as a transport and communications hub for Nunavut.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: One of the initial challenges Google faced was gathering the raw data needed to fill in their existing map.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: What they had created using satellite images was fairly accurate, although the rapid pace of the town’s growth, which has been fuelled by a mining boom, meant they were missing one road that had been created in the past year, said Arif Sayani, the town’s Director of Planning.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Another difficulty was how to situate many businesses and homeowners that have mail sent to the local post office rather than delivered to their address.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Plotting the PO box addresses would result in a map with firms, banks and schools clustered around the Canada Post building in the centre of town.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: About 30 Inuit elders, entrepreneurs and high-school pupils turned out one night to help correct such problems.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: They were provided with a laptop computer and instructed how to ensure their homes, shops and meeting places would show up accurately on the map.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: The project is more than a novelty or cultural philanthropy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Sayani, 32, said the town would be able to use the maps as a promotional tool for those thinking of visiting or moving to the area.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: It may also speed up planning decisions that will affect Iqaluit’s growth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: The test run for the Iqaluit mapping exercise occurred last summer in Cambridge Bay, a much smaller Nunavut town of about 1,500 people located 1,700km and a time zone west of Iqaluit.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: The gravel roads and muddy puddles that can now be seen online, however, give little sense of life in a land usually covered in snow, which is one reason why Google selected the lesshospitable month of March to travel to Iqaluit.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: Brindle said he hoped to see the work continue in other northern towns, though the high costs of shipping and airfares to move people and equipment around the vast Arctic territory appears to be weighing on Google’s ambitions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: The next northern site has not yet been identified but, when it is selected, Brindle said the company might simply send one of its hi-tech backpacks and rely on volunteers to literally put themselves on the map.
label: ignore

title: item_p0125
complex: “I’m hoping that three, four, five years from now we’ll look back and see a very different map of Canada’s north,” Brindle added.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: There comes a time in some men’s lives when the days seem darker, mortality more certain, and the only sensible response is to blow the life savings on a sportscar.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: Radical and often ill-advised changes in lifestyle have become the calling cards of the midlife crisis but, if it is more than a myth, then humans may not be the only animals to experience it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: Now an international team of scientists claims to have found evidence for a slump in well-being among middle-aged chimpanzees and orangutans.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: The lull in happiness in the middle years, they say, is the ape equivalent of the midlife crisis.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: The study, which was published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, has raised eyebrows among some scientists but, according to the authors, the findings suggest that the midlife crisis may have its roots in the biology humans share with our closest evolutionary cousins.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: “There’s a common understanding that there’s a dip in well-being in middle age,” Alex Weiss, a psychologist at Edinburgh University, told the Guardian.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: “We took a step back and asked whether it’s possible that instead of the midlife crisis being human-specific, and driven only by social factors, it reflects some evolved tendency for middle-aged individuals to have lower well-being,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: The team from the US, Japan, Germany and the UK asked zookeepers, carers and others who worked with male and female apes of various ages to complete questionnaires on the animals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: The forms included questions about each ape’s mood, the enjoyment they gained from socializing, and their success at achieving certain goals.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: The final question asked how carers would feel about being the ape for a week.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: They scored their answers from one to seven.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: More than 500 apes were included in the study in three separate groups.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: The first two groups were chimpanzees, with the third made up of orangutans from Sumatra or Borneo.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: The animals came from zoos, sanctuaries and research centres in the US, Australia, Japan, Canada and Singapore.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: When the researchers analyzed the questionnaires, they found that well-being in the apes fell in middle age and climbed again as the animals moved into old age.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: In captivity, great apes often live to 50 or more.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: The nadir in the animals’ well-being occurred, on average, at 28.3 and 27.2 years old for the chimpanzees, and 35.4 years old for the orangutans.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: “In all three groups we find evidence that well-being is lowest in chimpanzees and orangutans at an age that roughly corresponds to midlife in humans,” Weiss said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: “On average, well-being scores are lowest when animals are around 30 years old.” The team explains that the temporary fall in ape well-being may result from watching depressed apes dying younger, or through age-related changes in the brain that are mirrored in humans.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: Weiss conceded that, unlike men, apes are not known to pursue radical and often disastrous lifestyle changes in middle age.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: Robin Dunbar, Professor of Evolutionary Psychology at Oxford University, was dubious about the findings.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: “What can produce a sense of well-being or contentedness that varies across the lifespan like this?
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: It’s hard to see anything in an ape’s life that would have that sort of pattern, that they would think about.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: They’re not particularly good at seeing far ahead into the future; that’s one of the big differences between them and us.” Alexandra Freund, Professor of Psychology at the University of Zurich, was also sceptical.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: She said the concept of a midlife crisis was shaky even in humans.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: “In my reading of the literature, there is no evidence for the midlife crisis.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: If there’s any indication of decline in emotional or subjective well-being it is very small and, in many studies, it’s not there at all.” But Weiss believes the findings could point to a deeper understanding of the emotional crisis some men may experience.
label: ignore

title: item_p0126
complex: “If we want to find the answer as to what’s going on with the midlife crisis, we should look at what is similar in middle-aged humans, chimps and orangutans,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: The forests – and suburbs – of Europe are echoing with the growls, howls and silent padding of large predators, according to a new study that shows that brown bears, wolves and lynx are thriving on a crowded continent.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: Despite fears that large carnivores are doomed to extinction because of rising human populations and overconsumption, a study published in Science has found that large-predator populations are stable or rising in Europe.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: Brown bears, wolves, the Eurasian lynx and wolverines are found in nearly one-third of mainland Europe (excluding Belarus, Ukraine and Russia), with most individuals living outside nature reserves, indicating that changing attitudes and landscape-scale conservation measures are successfully protecting species that have suffered massive persecution throughout human history.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: Bears are the most abundant large carnivore in Europe, with around 17,000 individuals, alongside 12,000 wolves, 9,000 Eurasian lynx and 1,250 wolverines, which are restricted to northern parts of Scandinavia and Finland.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: Only Belgium, Denmark, the Netherlands and Luxembourg in mainland Europe – like Britain – have no breeding populations of at least one large carnivore species.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: But the paper’s lead author and other conservationists said these animals’ surprising distribution across well-populated regions of Europe showed that even the British countryside could support big predators.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: Guillaume Chapron from Sweden’s University of Agricultural Sciences and researchers across Europe found wolves, in some cases, living in suburban areas alongside up to 3,050 people per square kilometre – higher than the population density of Cambridge or Newcastle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: On average in Europe, wolves live on land with a population density of 37 people per sq km, lynx in areas with a population density of 21 people per sq km and bears among 19 people per sq km.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: The population density of the Scottish Highlands is nine people per sq km.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: “In order to have wolves, we don’t need to remove people from the landscape,” said Chapron.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: According to Chapron and his colleagues, the big-carnivore revival shows the success of a “land-sharing” model of conservation – in stark contrast to keeping predators and people apart by fencing off “wilderness” areas, as occurs in North America and Africa.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: “I’m not saying it’s a peace-and-love story – coexistence often means conflict – but it’s important to manage that conflict, keep it at a low level and resolve the problems it causes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: Wolves can be difficult neighbours,” said Chapron.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: “We shouldn’t be talking about people-predator conflict; we have conflict between people about predators.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: These animals are symbolic of difficult questions about how we should use the land.” According to the researchers, this “land-sharing” approach could be applied elsewhere in the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: The reasons for its success in Europe include political stability, burgeoning populations of prey species such as wild deer and financial support for non-lethal livestock protection such as electric fences, which mean that farmers do not resort to shooting wild predators.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: Most crucial, said Chapron, has been the EU Habitats Directive, which has compelled member states to protect and revive rare species.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: “Without the Habitats Directive, I don’t think we would have had this recovery,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: “It shows, if people are willing to protect nature and if political will is translated into strong legislation like the Habitats Directive, it’s possible to achieve results in wildlife protection.” The revival was welcomed by author and commentator George Monbiot, who is launching Rewilding Britain, a new charity to encourage the return of wild landscape and extinct species.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: “It is great to see the upward trend continuing but Britain is completely anomalous – we’ve lost more of our large mammals than any country except for Ireland,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: “Apart from the accidental reintroduction of boar, we’ve done almost nothing, whereas, in much of the rest of Europe, we’ve got bears, lynx and wolves coming back.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: It’s a massive turnaround from the centuries of persecution.” The survey found the Eurasian lynx living permanently in 11 population groups across 23 European countries, of which only five were native populations, indicating the success of reintroduction efforts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: According to Monbiot, momentum is building for the reintroduction of the lynx into the Cairngorms in Scotland.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: “If it works in the rest of Europe, there’s absolutely no reason why it can’t work in the UK,” he said, pointing out that bears and wolves are found within an hour of Rome.
label: ignore

title: item_p0127
complex: “There’s no demographic reason why we can’t have a similar return of wildlife in the UK.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: They call it the Richie Rich Club and it is about to get even richer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: India’s wealthiest will quadruple their net worth between now and 2018, a report says, with hundreds of thousands of new entrepreneurs and inheritors becoming multimillionaires.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: The survey, based on interviews with 150 ultra-high-net-worth individuals, comes amid signs of returning business confidence in the world’s biggest democracy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Recent years have seen lacklustre growth, rising prices of basic foodstuffs and a weakening currency.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: But the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) won a landslide victory in May 2014 on a pledge to reinvigorate the ailing economy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Despite the slowdown, there are nearly a sixth more Indians worth in excess of $3.75m than in 2013, the report for the Kotak Mahindra Bank notes.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: “Cities are mushrooming, the middle class population growing, opportunities have increased manyfold and the political environment has improved greatly in recent months,” according to Murali Balaraman, a co-author.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Between them, India’s rich hold assets worth a trillion dollars, which is around a fifth of the total wealth in the country.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: By 2018, that total is likely to reach $4tn, the report says, making three times as many people multimillionaires.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Serving the new rich – and the old money – is a booming luxury market.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: “They really want to show or talk about their wealth in a really subtle way and consumption of luxury goods is a nice way to do it,” Balaraman said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Abhay Gupta, the CEO of brand consultancy Luxury Connect, said the market for top-end goods and experiences would “only get bigger”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: “There is a huge aspirational class who look up to what the very wealthy are doing and then copy it,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Cars are among the most popular items bought, the report says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Whereas, in 2009, locally made SUVs were shown off by the wealthy, now only foreign cars will turn heads.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Mercedes saw a 47% surge in sales in India in 2013.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: BMW has launched a new $200,000 model in Delhi.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: India’s appalling infrastructure restricts demand, however.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Lamborghini’s Chief Executive, Stephan Winkelmann, admitted, in 2013, that the traffic and roads in India “are not so suitable” for the $450,000 sports cars.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: In India, Lamborghini sells two models: the Gallardo and the Aventador, which has a top speed of 217mph.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Winkelmann said Lamborghini’s Indian customers were much younger than those in Europe, with a typical buyer being in his 30s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: However, the most popular investments remain real estate – mainly within India – and jewellery.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: India’s super-rich have long raised eyebrows around the world with their spectacular spending.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Mukesh Ambani, the country’s wealthiest man, has built the world’s most valuable home in Mumbai, the commercial capital.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: The 27-storey tower, complete with helicopter pads, indoor cinemas and a staff of more than 600, is worth an estimated $1bn.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: The three-day wedding of the niece of Lakshmi Mittal, the UK-based steel tycoon who is worth an estimated $16bn, was reported to have cost $80m.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Hundreds of guests were flown to Barcelona for the ceremony and party, which took place in a museum in the city.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: But buyers of luxury goods searching for the psychological satisfaction of exclusivity are becoming increasingly demanding, the Kotak Mahindra report says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: One ordered nine cases of Japanese whisky costing over $750 a bottle for a wedding reception.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: The attraction of the imported whisky was that no one who attended the wedding would find out how to source the same drink in India, the report adds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Another big spender systematically bought identical pairs of Louis Vuitton bags, then cut up half of them to make clothes that would match her accessories.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Even the traditional wedding is evolving fast.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: Presents such as silver plates, dried fruit or sweets once sent with wedding invitations are being replaced by gifts by top western designer brands.
label: ignore

title: item_p0128
complex: “These days, it’s Rolex watches and Louis Vuitton bags,” says Gupta.

title: item_p0130

title: item_p0130
complex: Cockrell, a former Wall Street worker turned therapist, spends large parts of his days walking through Central Park or the Battery Park in downtown Manhattan near Wall Street, as a con dant and counsellor to some of New YorkÕs wealthiest people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0130
complex: ÒI shifted towards it naturally,Ó he said of his becoming an expert in wealth therapy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0130
complex: ÒMany of the extremely wealthy Ð the 1% of the 1% Ð feel that their problems are really not problems.

title: item_p0130
complex: But they are.

title: item_p0130
complex: A lot of therapists do not give enough weight to their issues.Ó So, what issues are AmericaÕs 1% struggling with?

title: item_p0130
complex: ÒThere is guilt over being rich in the rst place,Ó he said.

title: item_p0130
complex: ÒThere is the feeling that they have to hide the fact that they are rich.

title: item_p0130
complex: And, then, there is the isolation Ð being in the 1%, it turns out, can be lonely.Ó It seems F Scott Fitzgerald was right: the very rich Òare different from you and meÓ.

title: item_p0130
complex: Counsellors argue things have become worse since the nancial crisis and the debate over income inequality that has been spurred on by movements like Occupy Wall Street and the Fight for $15 fair wage campaign.
label: ignore

title: item_p0130
complex: ÒThe Occupy Wall Street movement was a good one and had some important things to say about income inequality but it singled out the 1% and painted them globally as something negative,Ó said Jamie Traeger-Muney, a wealth psychologist and founder of the Wealth Legacy Group.

complex: The media, she said, is partly to blame for making the rich Òfeel like they need to hide or feel ashamedÓ.

complex: Traeger-Muney runs a global rm and specializes in working with inheritors, who often get a bad reputation in the press.
label: ignore

title: item_p0130
complex: ÒYou can come up with lots of words and sayings about inheritors Ð not one of them is positive: spoilt brat, born with a silver spoon in their mouth, trust-fund babies, all these things,Ó she said, adding that itÕs Òeasy to scapegoat the richÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0130
complex: ÒSometimes, I am shocked by things that people say.

title: item_p0130
complex: You would never refer to another group of people in the way that it seems perfectly normal to refer to wealth holders.Ó ÒItÕs really isolating to have a lot of money.

complex: PeopleÕs reactions to you can be scary,Ó said Barbara Nusbaum, an expert in money psychology.

complex: ÒWe are all taught not to talk about money.

complex: Things are hard.Õ You canÕt say ÔI have a ton of money.Õ You have to keep a lot of your life private.Ó As a result, Cockrell points out, the rich tend to hang out with other rich Americans, not out of snobbery but in order to be around those who understand them and their problems.

complex: Since the 2008 nancial crisis, the income gap has expanded and the situation Òhas gotten worse for the wealthyÓ, Cockrell said.

complex: ÒAs the gap has widened, the rich have become more and more isolated."

complex: ÒPeople say ÔOh, poor you.Õ There is not a lot of sympathy there,Ó she said.

title: item_p0131
complex: From all across Rwanda, and even parts of neighbouring Burundi, people ock to the southern town of Butare to a little shop called Inzozi Nziza (Sweet Dreams).

complex: ÒIce cream is important,Ó she says between mouthfuls of a honey- avoured offering.

They did not set nancial targets, but waited for 18 months before they transferred their shares to the women, who had by then proved their business credentials.

title: item_p0132
complex: “Because it’s been so difficult, I’ve been going on holiday and enjoying it.” Sue Green, of Saga, a business that sells insurance and products to the over-50s, said the majority of parents may not have planned to have their children living with them well into their 20s or 30s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: “Most will be more than happy to house them in the family home rent-free because it might help their kids get on the property ladder sooner,” she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: “Children who don’t pay rent may contribute in other ways like buying groceries, family takeaways or doing odd jobs around the home.” Angus Hanton, co-founder of a thinktank called the Intergenerational Foundation, said older generations were “the architects of the housing crisis” and children should not be blamed for staying at home.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: “The under-30s have suffered a fall in average incomes of about 20% since the 2008 downturn.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: Rents and car insurance have never been so high and mortgage lending rules have been tightened for the young but not for older buy-to-let investors, who squeeze out the young,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: “Student-fee debt is rising rapidly yet many jobs on offer – zerohour and short-term contracts – are turning younger workers into second-class citizens.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: Rather than blaming the young, we should be standing up for their interests so they can afford to build lives of their own.” Jenna Gavin, 29, lives in Southport, Merseyside, in the family home where she grew up.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: She moved out for a year to go to university but has been living with her parents ever since.

title: item_p0132
complex: She works as a medical receptionist nearby so she wants to stay in the area.

title: item_p0132
complex: But renting a one-bedroom flat would cost more than £420 a month before bills, which would take up a lot of her earnings.

title: item_p0132
complex: “I don’t want to rent – I don’t want to spend all that money and have nothing at the end,” she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: “I’ve looked at buying and seen mortgage advisers but I just can’t borrow enough to get on the property ladder.” Gavin is trying to save but is struggling to amass the necessary funds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: “You don’t really see it building up as much as you need – even a 5% deposit is such a lot of money and I would like to put down more,” she said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: Her parents are happy not to charge her rent.
label: ignore

title: item_p0132
complex: “They want me to try to save up and I contribute in other ways – I bring food in and I do things around the house.” Gavin gets on with her parents and has her own space in a room that she moved into when she was 14 but she said she had always imagined she would have her own place by the time she was 30.

title: item_p0132
complex: “I don’t see that happening as it’s next year.

title: item_p0133
complex: Ever since he was diagnosed HIV positive, Moses King, 48, has had one major problem.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: He has been able to cope with the stigma of being HIV positive – widespread in Liberia – and he was able to access antiretroviral medication, provided by the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria and distributed by the Liberian government.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: But King and his family of six children could not get the right food to eat.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: A subsistence farmer, he grew vegetables and bought rice.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: But meat and fish – expensive, luxury products in Liberian markets but essential sources of protein – remained elusive.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “Subsistence farming allowed us to survive, but we had so many problems,” said King.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “We could not get any protein, and we were not getting the nutrients we needed to sustain ourselves.” Pate K Chon, a counsellor who works with HIV sufferers in Liberia, provided an unlikely solution.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: Since watching a documentary about a fish farm in Thailand several years earlier, she had thought of setting up a similar project in Liberia, enabling HIV sufferers to have work and also access a stable source of protein.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “I saw this film about fish in a cement pool and I thought it was a good idea,” said Chon, herself diagnosed with HIV in 1992.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “So many of the people I work with don’t have the means to have a balanced protein diet and fish is such a clean source of protein – it doesn’t cause health problems like other sources, and it is something we can farm.” Chon, founder of a faith-based NGO, began building a pool in which to farm fish.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: In June 2012, Chon met John Sheehy, a ‘strategic philanthropist’ and founder of consultancy Emerging Business Lab, who raised money for the non-profit fish farm in the northeast of Monrovia, Liberia’s capital, and set about learning aquaculture, doing an online course through Cornell University and speaking to other fish farmers in Africa.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “I raised the money and built the farm, learned the proper tank layout and water flow system,” said Sheehy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “A lot of my knowledge was self-taught, and now I would love to be able to write a manual and share it with other people,” he added.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: The project has now grown into the Grow2Feed Liberia Fish Farm, with 12 tanks, which, when fully stocked, will each have 5,000 fish – providing up to 200,000 fish per year, serving a community of 1,200 predominantly HIV-positive people, including King and his family.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: In addition to the fish, waste from the tanks is collected and used to irrigate crops, also providing food and income for the community, made up of 503 women, 206 men and 346 children.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “The members of the community live near the farm, and have agreed to be part of the co-operative,” said Sheehy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “Many work on the farm, or tending the crops, and what they get in return – when the fish are fully grown after 90 days – is fish.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: They can use those fish to feed themselves and to sell in the market so that they get money to buy other staple items.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “The fish farm gives these people with HIV a way of getting back into society – now they are bartering and trading with people in the market every week.” According to Liberia’s demographic health survey, 1.5% of Liberia’s 3.5 million people are HIV positive, with 60% of those women or girls.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: Stigma and discrimination still surround the illness, and have led to around half of all cases in the country going untreated.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: Good nutrition is particularly important for people with HIV.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: Research has shown they need much higher levels of protein to prevent their health from deteriorating and to allow healthy growth.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “Nutrition is one of the key things if you are taking antiretroviral drugs,” said Chon.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “The drugs are toxic and, if you don’t have food to eat, they can make you very ill.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: But food in Liberia is very expensive.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: We buy expensive imported rice, even though we should be growing it ourselves and fish is difficult for most people to afford.” Sheehy says Grow2Feed is the first co-operative fish farm in Liberia to operate for the benefit of an HIV-positive community.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: The project has attracted the interest of the Liberian government, and the UN Food and Agriculture Organization, which has worked with Grow2Feed to provide training, will also support teaching at the University of Liberia.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: Fish farming experts say the practice has huge potential in Africa.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “Fish farming is absolutely viable in Africa,” said Paul White, owner of the HydroFish fish farm in Ivory Coast, which produces 3,000 tonnes of fish each year.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “A lot of the fish on the market comes from China and is imported frozen.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: It is of a quality that could never enter Europe or America.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: There has been a serious lack of investment and a lack of know-how in fish production.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: It is all coming to the forefront now,” he added.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: Some critics are sceptical of farmed fish, citing inbred fish and high levels of toxins.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: But Sheehy said good practice mitigated these problems.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “A lot of farmed fish is inbred, which does cause problems, but we are using a process with local fish sourced in Liberia, not fish from another region,” said Sheehy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “That means we can continue using local fish to bring in new broodstock.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “And we are not using lakes that are cornered off, where the fish absorb all the toxins in the lake.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: We can control the environment using the tanks, and we test the water and monitor it constantly.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: Sheehy hopes to replicate the model throughout Liberia and the region.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “A rice-growing co-op in Sierra Leone asked us if we could do this on our property so that they can feed their workers and we have had interest from Nigeria and Central America,” said Sheehy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0133
complex: “But we operate 100% non-profit and we will never lose our social justice aspect.”

title: item_p0134
complex: Nelson Mandela, the towering figure of Africa’s struggle for freedom and a hero to millions around the world, has died at the age of 95.

title: item_p0134
complex: South Africa’s first black president died in the company of his family at home in Johannesburg after years of declining health, which had caused him to withdraw from public life.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: The news was announced to the country by the current president, Jacob Zuma, who, in a sombre televised address, said Mandela had “departed” around 8.50pm local time and was at peace.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: “This is the moment of our deepest sorrow,” Zuma said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: “Our nation has lost its greatest son.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: What made Nelson Mandela great was precisely what made him human.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: We saw in him what we seek in ourselves.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: “Fellow South Africans, Nelson Mandela brought us together and it is together that we will bid him farewell.” Zuma announced that Mandela would receive a state funeral and ordered that flags fly at half-mast.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: Archbishop Desmond Tutu led a memorial service in Cape Town, where he called for South Africa to become as a nation what Mandela had been as a man.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: Barack Obama led tributes from world leaders, referring to Mandela by his clan name – Madiba.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: The US president said: “Through his fierce dignity and unbending will to sacrifice his own freedom for the freedom of others, Madiba transformed South Africa – and moved all of us.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: “His journey from a prisoner to a president embodied the promise that human beings – and countries – can change for the better.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: His commitment to transfer power and reconcile with those who jailed him set an example that all humanity should aspire to, whether in the lives of nations or our own personal lives.” UK prime minister David Cameron said, “A great light has gone out in the world,” and described Mandela as “a hero of our time”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: FW de Klerk – the South African president who freed Mandela, shared the Nobel Peace Prize with him in 1993 and paved the way for him to become South Africa’s first post-apartheid head of state – said the news was deeply saddening for South Africa and the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: “He lived reconciliation.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: He was a great unifier,” de Klerk said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: People gathered in the streets of South Africa to celebrate Mandela’s life In Soweto, people gathered to sing and dance near the house where he once lived.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: They formed a circle in the middle of Vilakazi Street and sang songs from the anti-apartheid struggle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: Some people were draped in South African flags and the green, yellow and black colours of Mandela’s party, the African National Congress (ANC).
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: Mandela’s death sends South Africa deep into mourning and self-reflection, nearly 20 years after he led the country from racial apartheid to inclusive democracy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: But his passing will also be keenly felt by people around the world, who revered Mandela as one of history’s last great statesmen, and a moral paragon comparable with Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi and Martin Luther King.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: It was his act of forgiveness, after spending 27 years in prison, 18 of them on Robben Island, that will assure his place in history.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: With South Africa facing possible civil war, Mandela sought reconciliation with the white minority to build a new democracy.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: He led the ANC to victory in the country’s first multiracial election in 1994.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: He then voluntarily stepped down after one term.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: Born Rolihlahla Dalibhunga in a small village in the Eastern Cape on 18 July, 1918, Mandela was given his English name, Nelson, by a teacher at his school.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: He joined the ANC in 1943 and became a co-founder of its youth league.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: In 1952, he started South Africa’s first black law firm with his partner, Oliver Tambo.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: Mandela was a charming, charismatic figure with a passion for boxing and an eye for women.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: He once said: “I can’t help it if the ladies take note of me.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: I am not going to protest.” When the ANC was banned in 1960, Mandela went underground.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: After the Sharpeville massacre, in which 69 black protesters were shot dead by police, he took the difficult decision to launch an armed struggle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: He was arrested and eventually charged with sabotage and attempting to overthrow the government.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: Conducting his own defence in the Rivonia trial in 1964, he said: “I have cherished the ideal of a democratic and free society in which all persons live together in harmony and with equal opportunities.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: “It is an ideal which I hope to live for and to achieve.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: But, if needs be, it is an ideal for which I am prepared to die.” He escaped the death penalty but was sentenced to life in prison, a huge blow to the ANC, which had to regroup to continue the struggle.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: But unrest grew in townships and international pressure on the apartheid regime slowly tightened.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: Finally, in 1990, FW de Klerk lifted the ban on the ANC and Mandela was released from prison amid scenes of jubilation witnessed around the world.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: His presidency rode a wave of tremendous global goodwill but was not without its difficulties.

title: item_p0134
complex: After leaving frontline politics in 1999, he admitted he should have moved sooner against the spread of HIV and Aids in South Africa.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: Archbishop Desmond Tutu, who headed the truth and reconciliation committee after the fall of apartheid, said: “He transcended race and class in his personal actions, through his warmth and through his willingness to listen and to empathize with others.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: And he restored others’ faith in Africa and Africans.” Mandela continued to make occasional appearances at ANC events and attended the inauguration of the current president, Jacob Zuma.
label: ignore

title: item_p0134
complex: His 91st birthday was marked by the first annual “Mandela Day” in his honour.

title: item_p0134

title: item_p0135
complex: Piles of digitized material – from blogs, tweets, pictures and videos to official documents such as court rulings and emails – may be lost forever because the programs needed to view them will become defunct, Google’s vice-president has warned.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: Humanity’s first steps into the digital world could be lost to future historians, Vint Cerf told the American Association for the Advancement of Science’s annual meeting in San Jose, California, warning that we faced a “forgotten generation or even a forgotten century” through what he called “bit rot”, where old computer files become useless junk.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: Cerf called for the development of “digital vellum” to preserve old software and hardware so that out-of-date files could be recovered no matter how old they are.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: “When you think about the quantity of documentation from our daily lives that is captured in digital form, like our interactions by email, people’s tweets and all of the world wide web, it’s clear that we stand to lose an awful lot of our history,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: “We don’t want our digital lives to fade away.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: If we want to preserve them, we need to make sure that the digital objects we create today can still be rendered far into the future,” he added.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: What is ‘bit rot’ and is Vint Cerf right to be worried?
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: Being able to access digital content in the coming decades could be less of an issue than one of the ‘fathers of the internet’ has implied.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: The warning highlights an irony at the heart of modern technology, where music, photos, letters and other documents are digitized in the hope of ensuring their long-term survival.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: But, while researchers are making progress in storing digital files for centuries, the programs and hardware needed to make sense of the files are continually falling out of use.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: “We are nonchalantly throwing all of our data into what could become an information black hole without realizing it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: We digitize things because we think we will preserve them but what we don’t understand is that, unless we take other steps, those digital versions may not be any better, and may even be worse, than the artefacts that we digitized,” Cerf says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: “If there are photos you really care about, print them out.” Ancient civilizations suffered no such problems because histories written in cuneiform on baked clay tablets or rolled papyrus scrolls needed only eyes to read them.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: To study today’s culture, future scholars would be faced with PDFs, Word documents and hundreds of other file types that can only be interpreted with dedicated software and sometimes hardware, too.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: The problem is already here.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: In the 1980s, it was routine to save documents on floppy disks, upload Jet Set Willy from cassette to the ZX spectrum, slaughter aliens with a Quickfire II joystick and have Atari games cartridges in the attic.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: Even if the disks and cassettes are in good condition, the equipment needed to run them is now mostly found only in museums.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: The rise of gaming has its own place in the story of digital culture but Cerf warns that important political and historical documents will also be lost to bit rot.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: In 2005, American historian Doris Kearns Goodwin wrote Team of Rivals: the Political Genius of Abraham Lincoln, describing how Lincoln hired those who ran against him for presidency.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: She went to libraries around the US, found the physical letters of the people involved and reconstructed their conversations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: “In today’s world, those letters would be emails and the chances of finding them will be vanishingly small one hundred years from now,” said Cerf.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: He concedes that historians will take steps to preserve material considered important by today’s standards but argues that the significance of documents and correspondence is often not fully appreciated until hundreds of years later.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: Historians have learned how the greatest mathematician of antiquity considered the concept of infinity and anticipated calculus in 3BC after the Archimedes palimpsest was found hidden under the words of a Byzantine prayer book from the thirteenth century.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: “We’ve been surprised by what we’ve learned from objects that have been preserved purely by chance that give us insights into an earlier civilization,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: Researchers at Carnegie Mellon University in Pittsburgh have made headway towards a solution to bit rot, or at least a partial one.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: There, Mahadev Satyanarayanan takes digital snapshots of computer hard drives while they run different software programs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: These can then be uploaded to a computer that mimics the one the software ran on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: The result is a computer that can read otherwise defunct files.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: Under a project called Olive, the researchers have archived Mystery House, the original 1982 graphic adventure game for the Apple II, an early version of WordPerfect, and Doom, the original 1993 first person shooter game.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: Inventing new technology is only half the battle, though.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: More difficult still could be navigating the legal permissions to copy and store software before it dies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: When IT companies go out of business, or stop supporting their products, they may sell the rights on, making it a nightmarish task to get approval.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: “To do this properly, the rights of preservation might need to be incorporated into our thinking about things like copyright and patents and licensing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0135
complex: We’re talking about preserving them for hundreds to thousands of years,” said Cerf.
