title: item_p0136
complex: Swedish prisons have long had a reputation around the world for being progressive.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: But are the country’s prisons a soft option?
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: The head of Sweden’s prison and probation service, Nils Oberg, announced in November 2013 that four Swedish prisons are to be closed due to an “out of the ordinary” decline in prisoner numbers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: Although there has been no fall in crime rates, between 2011 and 2012 there was a 6% drop in Sweden’s prisoner population, now a little over 4,500.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: A similar decrease is expected in 2013 and 2014.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: Oberg admitted to being puzzled by the unexpected dip, but expressed optimism that the reason was to do with how his prisons are run.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: “We certainly hope that the efforts we invest in rehabilitation and preventing relapse of crime has had an impact,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: “The modern prison service in Sweden is very different from when I joined as a young prison officer in 1978,” says Kenneth Gustafsson, governor of Kumla Prison, Sweden’s most secure jail, situated 130 miles west of Stockholm.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: However, he doesn’t think the system has gone soft.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: “When I joined, the focus was very much on humanity in prisons.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: Prisoners were treated well – maybe too well, some might say.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: But, after a number of high-profile escapes in 2004, we had to rebalance and place more emphasis on security.” Despite the hardening of attitudes toward prison security following the escape scandals, the Swedes still managed to maintain a broadly humane approach to sentencing, even of the most serious offenders: jail terms rarely exceed ten years; those who receive life imprisonment can still apply to the courts after a decade to have the sentence commuted to a fixed term, usually in the region of 18 to 25 years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: Sweden was the first country in Europe to introduce the electronic tagging of convicted criminals and continues to strive to minimize short-term prison sentences wherever possible by using community-based measures, which have been proven to be more effective at reducing reoffending.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: The overall reoffending rate in Sweden stands at between 30 and 40% over three years – to compare that with another European country, the number is around half that of the UK.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: One likely reason for the relatively low reoffending rate and the low rate of incarceration in Sweden (below 70 per 100,000 head of population) is that the age of criminal responsibility is set at 15.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: In the UK, for example, children aged ten to 17 and young people under the age of 21 record the highest reoffending rates: almost three quarters and two thirds, respectively.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: A good proportion of these offenders go on to populate adult jails.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: In Sweden, no young person under the age of 21 can be sentenced to life – this is not the case in many other countries – and every effort is made to ensure that as few juvenile offenders as possible end up in prison.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: One strong reason for the drop in prison numbers might be the amount of post-prison support available in Sweden.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: A confident probation service – a government agency – is tasked not only with supervising those on probation but is also guaranteed to provide treatment programmes for offenders with drug, alcohol or violence issues.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: The service is assisted by around 4,500 lay supervisors – members of the public who volunteer to befriend and support offenders under supervision.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: Gustafsson talks about broader goals and objectives for the Swedish justice department: “In 2013 and 2014, the priority of our work will be with young offenders and men with convictions of violent behaviour.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: For many years, we have been running programmes to help those addicted to drugs.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: Now, we are also developing programmes to address behaviours such as aggression and violence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: These are the important things for our society when these people are released.” I spoke to a former prisoner who now runs a social enterprise called X-Cons Sweden.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: Peter Soderlund served almost three years of a four-year sentence for drug and weapons offences before he was released in 1998.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: He was helped by a newly formed organization run by former prisoners called Kris (Criminals’ Return Into Society).
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: “The big difference between Kris and us is that we are happy to allow people who are still taking addiction medications to join us,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: Both organizations work with the same goal: helping prisoners successfully reintegrate into society after they have been released.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: And what is life like for the prisoner in Sweden?
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: “When I was inside, I was lucky.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: In Osteraker Prison, where I served my sentence, the governor was enlightened.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: We were treated well.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: But I knew that not all Swedish prisons were like that.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: I met so many people in there who needed help – after I received help from Kris, I knew I wanted to help others.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: With X-Cons, we meet them at the gate and support them into accommodation and offer a network of support.” “In Sweden, we believe very much in the concept of rehabilitation, without being naive of course,” says Gustafsson.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: “There are some people who will not or cannot change.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: But, in my experience, the majority of prisoners want to change, and we must do what we can to help to facilitate that.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: It is not always possible to achieve this in one prison sentence.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: “Also, it is not just prison that can rehabilitate – it is often a combined process, involving probation and greater society.
label: ignore

title: item_p0136
complex: We can give education and training, but, when they leave prison, these people need housing and jobs.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: A degree in Spanish got me my fi rst job as a journalist, with an international press agency in Mexico City, but it didn’t prevent me blundering badly as a rookie reporter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: I had just arrived in the Mexican capital after a Greyhound bus journey all the way from New York and the job interview was a test of my language skills.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: In my new role, day shifts were spent on the streets in political rallies and nights were spent alone in the offi ce, coordinating the coverage from strifetorn El Salvador, Nicaragua, Guatemala and the rest of Central America.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: But, I also had to report on occasional disasters: fi res, floods and explosions at firework factories.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: It was as a reporter that I soon found out that I was as bad at understanding numbers in Spanish as I was at calculating them in English.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: Phone calls meant for the police got Mexican grandmothers out of bed at 2am because I had misunderstood a number and dialled a dodgy digit.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: Even worse, victims were piled too high in my stories – almost 83 dead in a fi re at 6pm turned out to be as few as 38 by 7pm; 12 people injured in a coach crash soon became two and so it went on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: Finally, I got a call from the main offi ce in Washington.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: “I don’t know what training you have had,” an editor yelled, “but has no one ever told you a death toll can’t go down?
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: !” Why are numbers in another language such a conundrum?
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: It may have to do with different numbering systems.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: If we consider that, in German, for example, which belongs to the same Indo-European language family as English, 2.30pm becomes halb drei (half of three) and 21 becomes einundzwanzig (one and twenty), clearly different numeral systems can cause confusion and that’s without even considering indigenous languages with numeral systems so rare they are in danger of dying out.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: Some experts believe there is a link between dyscalculia – the diffi culty in comprehending arithmetic – and problems learning foreign languages, particularly if languages are learnt by rote, since this involves the sequential processes that students with dyscalculia struggle with.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: But, some students who struggle to learn languages with a grammar textbook may thrive in a foreign-language setting, where learning is more natural and less reliant on sequences of adjectives, prepositions and so on.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: In my case, I have always found languages easy enough, apart from the numbers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: But, perhaps it’s also because numbers in a non-native language are often heard out of context or in isolation, when the listener might have switched off from the foreign language and be unable to suddenly tune in.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: A straw poll of multilingual friends found that many can be fl orid in French or Italian when ordering from a restaurant menu, for example, but freeze if they have to relay numbers, especially over the phone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: Numbers seem to be taxing, but no one could really say why.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: In my case, being numerically challenged in a foreign tongue followed me from Mexico to other countries and from Spanish to German and Portuguese.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: But, in that fi rst journalism job, getting the numbers wrong didn’t always add up to failure.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: One night, a Mexican colleague learnt that the American consul in the port city of Veracruz was being held hostage at gunpoint in his offi ce.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: With no senior English-speaking reporter in the offi ce, it was left to me to try to reach the consulate by phone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: Having got the number wrong, I was put through to an extension elsewhere in the building and the identity of the person who took my call was unmistakable: I chatted for 15 minutes to the gunman.
label: ignore

title: item_p0137
complex: I may not have persuaded him to put away his gun – but my reputation as a rookie reporter still rose overnight.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: The government is bracing itself for thousands of legal claims from people who were imprisoned and allegedly mistreated during the final days of the British Empire after the High Court in London ruled that three elderly Kenyans detained and tortured during the Mau Mau rebellion have the right to sue for damages.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: The court on Friday rejected claims from the government’s lawyers that too much time had elapsed since the seven-year insurgency in the 1950s, and it was no longer possible to hold a fair trial.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: In 2011 the same High Court judge, Mr Justice McCombe, rejected the government’s claim that the three claimants should be suing the Kenyan government as it had inherited Britain’s legal responsibilities on independence in 1963.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: Human rights activists in Kenya estimate more than 5,000 of the 70,000-plus people detained by the British colonial authorities are still alive.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: Many may bring claims against the British government.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: The ruling may also make it possible for victims of colonial atrocities in other parts of the world to sue.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: But many more men and women around the world who were imprisoned and allegedly mistreated during the conflicts that often accompanied the British retreat from empire may also be considering claims: cases that could bring to light evidence of brutal mistreatment of colonial subjects and result in a new and uncomfortable understanding of recent British history.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: The Foreign Office acknowledged that the ruling had “potentially significant and far-reaching legal implications”, and said it was planning to appeal.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: “The normal time limit for bringing a civil action is three to six years,” a spokesman said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: “In this case, that period has been extended to over 50 years despite the fact that the key decision makers are dead and unable to give their account of what happened.” Friday’s historic victory for Paulo Muoka Nzili, 85, Wambugu Wa Nyingi, 84, and Jane Muthoni Mara, 73, was the result of a three-year battle through the courts.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: They had suffered what their lawyers describe as “unspeakable acts of brutality” including castration, beatings and severe sexual assaults.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: A fourth claimant dropped out while a fifth, Susan Ciong’ombe Ngondi, died two years ago, aged 71.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: In the Kenyan capital Nairobi, the news from London was relayed to two of the complainants, Nyingi and Mara, by mobile phone.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: They had been sitting silently with their supporters in a sun-scorched garden and reacted with joy when the word came, hugging, dancing and eventually raising their hands to the sky to pray.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: Nyingi, who was detained for about nine years, beaten unconscious and bears the marks from leg manacles, whipping and caning, said: “For me … I just wanted the truth to be out.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: Even the children of my children should know what happened.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: What should happen is that people should be compensated so they can begin to forgive the British government.” Mara said: “I’m very happy and my heart is clean.” Asked what she would tell her four children, she said simply: “I will tell them I won.” McCombe said in 2011 that there was “ample evidence … that there may have been systematic torture of detainees”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: On Friday he ruled that a fair trial was possible, and highlighted the fact that thousands of documents came to light in 2011 after the Foreign Office admitted to a secret archive of colonial-era files.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: During the course of their attempts to have the claims struck out – efforts that the claimants’ lawyer, Martyn Day, described as “morally repugnant” – the British government’s lawyers accepted that all three of the elderly Kenyans were tortured by the colonial authorities.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: Day said: “The British government has admitted that these three Kenyans were brutally tortured by the British colony and yet they have been hiding behind technical legal defences for three years in order to avoid any legal responsibility.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: There will undoubtedly be victims of colonial torture from Malaya to the Yemen, from Cyprus to Palestine, who will be reading this judgment with great care.” Among those who are known to have been watching the case closely are a number of veterans of the Eoka insurgency in Cyprus in the 1950s.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: One has already met the Mau Mau claimants’ lawyers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: Any Cypriot claimants would be able to rely not only on British documentation, but upon the archives of the International Committee of the Red Cross in Geneva.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: Those files are kept secret for 40 years, and then opened to public scrutiny.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: The Red Cross documented hundreds of torture cases in Cyprus, where reporters covering the conflict referred to British interrogators as HMTs – Her Majesty’s Torturers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: There may also be claims from Malaysia, where large numbers of people were detained during the 12-year war with communist insurgents and their supporters that began in 1948.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: Relatives of 24 unarmed rubber plantation workers massacred by British troops are currently fighting through the British courts for a public inquiry.
label: ignore

title: item_p0138
complex: Many former prisoners of the British in Aden may also have claims against the British government, although, as Aden is now part of Yemen, British lawyers may have difficulty making contact with potential clients there.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: The small space is set up to look like a classroom.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: Its corrugated iron walls are hung with educational charts – illustrated letters of the alphabet and a map of Bangladesh.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: But, the constant sound of hammering and the chemicals in the air that catch in the back of the throat and irritate the eyes make it hard to concentrate.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: The children who learn in this three-square-metre room are the lucky ones, however.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: They have escaped working in the factories opposite.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: For 14 years, SOHAY, a grassroots nongovernmental organization (NGO) funded by the Global Fund for Children and Comic Relief, has been working in slum areas of Dhaka to get child labourers into school.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: It focuses on children working in hazardous conditions – in aluminium and plastic factories, and tanneries.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: The classroom is one of 23 urban development centres that SOHAY has set up across the capital.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: The centres prepare children for primary school with classes that help them catch up on their education.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: Once they are in primary school, the children can do homework at the centres, with help from their peers.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: Alamin, ten, who used to work in a plastic factory, attends one of the centres.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: His father is a street seller and his mother a part-time domestic worker.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: They are all happy that he’s now in school and away from hazardous work.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: His friend Rabi says he wants to forget his past in the factory.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “I like school,” he says.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “The urban development centres aim to create an education-friendly environment in the communities and change their cultural mindset towards the children,” says SOHAY’s programme manager, Mohammed Abdullah al-Mamun.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: SOHAY also runs sessions for parents and employers to discourage child labour and offers skills training to increase family income.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “Getting working children into formal education is really very challenging,” says Mamun.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “Their psychological and physical condition is not like other children in society.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: After they leave work, they sometimes find it difficult to make friends and adapt to school.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: It is also very challenging to ensure they stay in school – the dropout rate is very high for these children.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: In this context, it’s important to work with schools so they have more sensitivity and care about them.” Seven-year-old Zhorna Akter Sumayya has two older brothers, both of whom are in work (one at a restaurant, one at a local club).
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: But, after being introduced to education at one of SOHAY’s centres, she now goes to a state primary school.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: Her family live in the slum and her parents can’t survive without the income their sons bring home.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: Her father works in a rickshaw garage and her mother is a domestic worker, but they were keen for their daughter to go to school.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: In 2015, SOHAY helped 1,540 children to leave hazardous work and 2,125 vulnerable children – those in danger of entering work – into school.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: About 780 more children are preparing to enter school in 2017.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: The organization is also helping 635 children who are working in hazardous conditions to know their rights under Bangladesh’s 2010 child labour elimination code of conduct.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: The policy aimed to eradicate all forms of child labour by 2015 but that target was missed.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: The Labour Law of Bangladesh 2006 bans children under the age of 14 from working but, according to the UN children’s agency, UNICEF, 4.7 million children under that age are employed in the informal sector and 1.3 million aged five to 17 work in hazardous industries.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “It was difficult to get them into school without any compensation for their time,” says Sadia Nasrin, who runs Sonjag, another Dhaka grassroots NGO.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “To overcome this challenge, Sonjag started working closely with the community in the slums where the children live.” The organization talked to community members about why it was important to get children into school.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: They selected community volunteers who were motivated to change children’s lives and formed groups with social workers, community leaders, mothers, young volunteers and the local government.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “The groups play a vital role in motivating employers to let children leave for two to three hours a day to attend school and to ensure a safe workplace for the children.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: The ultimate change-makers are the community people,” says Nasrin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: She adds that people living in slums face threats of eviction, police raids and displacement.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “The national plan of action for children does not recognize the needs of street children,” says Nasrin.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “Legislative measures are limited.” When the children have missed starting school at five years old, it is a race against time to prevent them from growing up without an education.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “After they cross their school age, it is really very difficult to admit them into school,” says Mamun.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: “Children are just passing their time without education and waiting to become involved in hazardous work.
label: ignore

title: item_p0139
complex: We are working to block the child labour flow.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: A Canadian man who sprang to fame after offering a free round-the-world trip to a woman with the same name as his ex-girlfriend has returned from the jaunt with his chosen namesake, although, to the dismay of those following the story, love did not blossom between the pair.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: Jordan Axani, a 28-year-old Toronto real-estate developer turned charity founder, made it back to Canada with Elizabeth Quinn Gallagher but said the pair had “forged a brother-sister-like relationship”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: Axani had made headlines in 2014 after offering an air ticket to any Canadian named Elizabeth Gallagher.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: He had booked a three-week vacation with his ex-girlfriend but, after they split up, he was unable to change the name on the flights.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: That’s where Axani’s new travelmate, a 23-year-old student from Cole Harbour, Nova Scotia, came in.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: Gallagher, who goes by the name Quinn, replied to a Reddit post Axani had submitted – along with other hopeful Elizabeth Gallaghers – and was selected.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: Gallagher had made it clear before the trip that she had a “pretty serious” boyfriend but that had not stopped romantics, and journalists, from hoping the globetrotters might fall for one another.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: Unfortunately, it was not to be.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: “I’m going to be explicitly clear,” Axani said, shortly after the pair returned to Toronto.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: “This was never a romantic endeavour.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: It was strictly platonic.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: I do not think of Quinn in a romantic light in the least.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: There is no future for us romantically.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: She is a good friend.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: I think of her as a little sister but that will be it.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: And her feelings are entirely mutual in that regard.” It took work to establish that brother-sister, good-friend, no-future-for-us-romantically relationship, however.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: “It wasn’t easy and it certainly wasn’t immediate.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: It took us about a week to really figure each other out,” Axani said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: There was a certain amount of “natural stumbling” around “the dos and don’ts of travelling together” as the pair got to know each other.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: “At the end of it, we’d developed a really great rhythm of, one second, having really funny inside jokes, and, the next second, knowing when the other needed space.” Although the pair failed to fall for one another, Axani said the trip, which took in Milan, Venice, Vienna, Prague, Khao Lak (in Thailand) and Hong Kong, was “fantastic”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: A favourite place was Prague, Axani said, where they “had the largest groundswell of people reaching out”.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: “Over the course of two and a half days, I think we met about two dozen people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: So that’s a lot of stories, that’s a lot of individuals and that’s a lot of love for their home city of Prague.” People were following the pair on Twitter and Instagram, Axani said, and they were even recognized in the street in Hong Kong.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: “It was a real adventure.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: We had a blast.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: We learned a lot about ourselves and about each other.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: I think, coming out of it, I can’t imagine it going much better than it did.” Axani made it back to Toronto at 3am on Monday, 12 January, when the holiday came to an abrupt halt.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: He went straight into a board meeting with fellow board members at his charity, A Ticket Forward.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: Axani started the non-profit organization after his Reddit post went viral and intends to offer round-the-world-trips to survivors of abuse, cancer and war.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: Alongside that, Axani is also in talks to spin his story into a television show or film, although he would not comment on what form those productions might take.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: “Suffice to say there’s been significant interest from many production companies.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: We’re well advanced.” In terms of his love life, Axani said he was not looking for his next Elizabeth Gallagher just yet.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: “I’m not looking for anything, per se, but life happens and we’ll see,” he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0140
complex: “As always, life’s a journey.”
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Scientists have created an Òatlas of the brainÓ that reveals how the meanings of words are arranged across different regions of the organ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Like a colourful quilt laid over the cortex, the atlas displays in rainbow hues how individual words and the concepts they convey can be grouped together in clumps of white matter.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: ÒOur goal was to build a giant atlas that shows how one speci c aspect of language is represented in the brain, in this case semantics or the meanings of words,Ó said Jack Gallant, a neuroscientist at the University of California, Berkeley.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: No single brain region holds one word or concept.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: A single brain spot is associated with a number of related words.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: And, each single word lights up many different brain spots.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Together, they make up networks that represent the meanings of each word we use: life and love, death and taxes, clouds, Florida and bra.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: All light up their own networks.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Described as a Òtour de forceÓ by one researcher who was not involved in the study, the atlas demonstrates how modern imaging can transform our knowledge of how the brain performs some of its most important tasks.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: With further advances, the technology could have a profound impact on medicine and other elds.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: ÒIt is possible that this approach could be used to decode information about what words a person is hearing, reading or possibly even thinking,Ó said Alexander Huth, the rst author on the study.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: One potential use would be a language decoder that could allow people silenced by motor neurone disease or locked-in syndrome to speak through a computer.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: To create the atlas, the scientists recorded peopleÕs brain activity while they listened to stories read out on The Moth Radio Hour, a US radio show.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: They then matched the transcripts of the stories with the brain activity data to show how groups of related words triggered neural responses in 50,000 to 80,000 pea-sized spots all over the cerebral cortex.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Huth used stories from The Moth Radio Hour because they are short and compelling.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: The more enthralling the stories, the more con dent the scientists could be that the people being scanned were focusing on the words and not drifting off.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Seven people listened to two hours of stories each.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Per person, that amounted to hearing roughly 25,000 words Ð and more than 3,000 different words Ð as they lay in the scanner.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: The atlas shows how words and related terms exercise the same regions of the brain.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: For example, on the left-hand side of the brain, above the ear, is one of the tiny regions that represents the word ÒvictimÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: The same region responds to ÒkilledÓ, ÒconvictedÓ, ÒmurderedÓ and ÒconfessedÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: On the brainÕs right-hand side, near the top of the head, is one of the brain spots activated by family terms: ÒwifeÓ, ÒhusbandÓ, ÒchildrenÓ, ÒparentsÓ.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Each word is represented by more than one spot because words tend to have several meanings.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: One part of the brain, for example, reliably responds to the word ÒtopÓ, along with other words that describe clothing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: But, the word ÒtopÓ activates many other regions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: One of them responds to numbers and measurements, another to buildings and places.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: The scientists have created an interactive website where the public can explore the brain atlas.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Strikingly, the brain atlases were similar for all the participants, suggesting that their brains organized the meanings of words in the same way.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: The scientists only scanned ve men and two women, however.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: All are native English speakers and two are authors of the study published in Nature.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: It is highly possible that people from different backgrounds and cultures will have different semantic brain atlases.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Armed with the atlas, researchers can now piece together the brain networks that represent wildly different concepts, from numbers to murder and religion.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: ÒThe idea of murder is represented a lot in the brain,Ó Gallant said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Using the same data, the group has begun work on new atlases that show how the brain holds information on other aspects of language, from phonemes to syntax.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: A brain atlas for narrative structure has so far proved elusive, however.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: ÒEvery time we come up with a set of narrative features, we get told they arenÕt the right set of narrative features,Ó said Gallant.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Uri Hasson, a neuroscientist at Princeton University, praised the work.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Unlike many studies that looked at brain activity when an isolated word or sentence was spoken, GallantÕs team had shed light on how the brain worked in a real- world scenario, he said.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: The next step, he added, was to create a more comprehensive and precise semantic brain atlas.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: Ultimately, Hasson believes it will be possible to reconstruct the words a person is thinking from their brain activity.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: The ethical implications are enormous.
label: ignore

title: item_p0141
complex: One more benign use would see brain activity used to assess whether political messages have been effectively communicated to the public.
label: ignore

title: item_p0144
complex: Names matter in Iceland, a country of barely 320,000 people, whose phone book lists subscribers by their first name for the very sensible reason that the vast majority of Icelandic surnames simply record the fact that you are your father’s (or mother’s) son or daughter.

title: item_p0148
complex: Sweden is the best country for older people, Afghanistan the worst – but general affluence does not necessarily mean better conditions for the over-60s, according to the first global index on ageing.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: While Sweden’s top ranking – followed by Norway, Germany, the Netherlands and Canada – may be predictable, the Global AgeWatch index throws up some surprising results.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: The US, the world’s richest country, is down in eighth place, while the UK fails to make the top ten at number 13.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Sri Lanka ranks 36, well above Pakistan at 89, despite similar levels of gross domestic product (GDP).
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Bolivia and Mauritius score higher than the size of their economies may suggest, while the emerging economies of Brazil, Russia, India and China are a mixed bag.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Brazil and China rank relatively high on the index; India and Russia are much lower.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: “This survey shows that history counts,” said Mark Gorman, director of the HelpAge International advocacy group.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: “The top-ranked countries are what you would expect, but Scandinavian countries were not wealthy when they introduced universal pensions.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: The older population in Sri Lanka today is benefiting from good basic education and health care – those countries made certain policy choices.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Everybody faces scarce resources, but they should not forget that, when they make investment decisions, they should also address issues of old age.” The index, developed with the UN Fund for Population and Development, spans 91 countries and 89% of the world’s older people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: The survey comes amid a major demographic shift: by 2050, there are expected to be two billion people aged 60 and over, who will comprise more than a fifth of the world’s population.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Population ageing – when older people account for an increasingly large proportion of people – is happening fastest in developing countries.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: More than two-thirds of older people live in poor countries; by 2050, this proportion is expected to be about four-fifths.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: While it took 115 years for the older population of France to double from 7% to 14% between 1865 and 1980, Brazil is likely to make the same shift between 2011 and 2032 – in just 21 years.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: The index shows that the fastest ageing countries – Jordan, Laos, Mongolia, Nicaragua and Vietnam, where the number of older people is predicted to more than triple by 2050 – fall into the lower half of the ranking, suggesting that policymakers need to tackle ageing head-on if they are to adequately support their populations.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: There are gender differences among ageing populations, with women generally outliving men.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: In 2012, for every 84 men aged 60 and over, there were 100 women.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Lack of paid work (hence savings), less decision-making power in the family and vulnerability to violence contribute towards the disadvantage many women face in old age.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: However, if appropriate measures are implemented, population ageing does not inevitably lead to significantly higher health care spending, according to the report, which highlights the importance of long-term investments in education and health care for older people.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Bolivia, ranked 46, despite being one of the poorest countries, has introduced progressive policies for older people, with a national plan on ageing, free health care and a non-contributory universal pension.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Nepal, ranked 77, introduced a basic pension in 1995 for people over the age of 70 without other pension income.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Though limited in value and eligibility and with uneven coverage, it is an example of how a poor country has chosen to make a start in addressing poverty in old age.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Good basic health care introduced decades ago in Chile and Costa Rica has served the ageing populations of those countries.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: A good education system – basic literacy is crucial for older people as they deal with the pensions bureaucracy – is of great benefit later in life.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: In the Philippines, older people have benefited from the educational reforms introduced after independence in 1946, which made elementary and high school education compulsory.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: The same is true for Armenia, which, like other countries of the former Soviet Union, benefited from a robust education system.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: South Korea, a surprisingly low 67 on the ageing index, performed worse than similar countries on a GDP-per-head basis, partly because it introduced a pension only recently.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: The ageing index is calculated using 13 indicators under four headings: income security, health care, employment and education, and an enabling environment.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: All indicators have equal weight, except for pension income coverage, life expectancy at 60, healthy life expectancy at 60 and psychological well-being.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: These categories were given increased weighting because of better data quality and countries were included only if there was sufficient data.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: Professor Sir Richard Jolly, creator of the human development index, said: “This groundbreaking index broadens the way we understand the needs and opportunities of older people through its pioneering application of human development methodology.
label: ignore

title: item_p0148
complex: It challenges countries in every part of the world to raise their sights as to what is possible.”
